Text-embedding methods offer a scalable route to harmonization, but their value depends on recovering conceptual overlap rather than surface-level lexical similarity. We evaluate 12 pipelines that combine four embedding models with three techniques, calibrated on the European Language Social Science Thesaurus and tested on the APA Thesaurus. Beyond accuracy, five diagnostic tests examine lexical traps, semantic decay, stability, rare-word bias, and alignment with expert taxonomic structure. Harmonization identifies many synonymous labels, but accuracy is uneven and depends on surface form, with errors concentrated in cases central to theory development: low-overlap synonyms, rare terms, and subtle taxonomic distinctions. The diagnostic tests make these successes and failures transparent, giving researchers a reproducible basis for deciding when to trust embedding-based harmonization and when to defer to experts.

articles.title: Between the Steeple and the Golden Stool: Navigating Cultural Conflict, Pentecostal Competition, and the Organisational Resilience Paradox in Asante Baptist Ministry
articles.authors: McFaustine Kofi Mahama; Yaw Adu-Gyamfi; Gabriel Osei Forkuo
articles.abstract:
Background: The intersection of Asante cosmological traditions and Christian ministry constitutes a profoundly contested space in West African Christianity. Despite severe implications for denominational attrition amid aggressive Pentecostal competition, empirical examination of this culture-faith nexus remains sparse. This pioneering study investigates the prevalence, character, and congregational consequences of conflicts between traditional Asante practices and Baptist theology in urban Ghana.
Methods: Employing a robust cross-sectional mixed-methods design, we surveyed 200 ordained Ghana Baptist Convention pastors in the Kumasi Metropolis. Quantitative data underwent rigorous statistical evaluation using Python 3.12, including chi-square tests of independence and Spearman’s rank-order correlation, triangulated with keyword-based thematic analysis of qualitative pastoral narratives.
Results: Findings reveal that 70.5% of pastors consistently navigate culture-faith conflicts, predominantly driven by deeply entrenched taboos (18.0%), bride-price demands (17.0%), and ancestral worship (15.0%). Notably, while 23.0% of pastors reported direct membership attrition to culturally accommodating Pentecostal alternatives, chi-square analysis established no significant statistical association between conflict frequency and attrition (χ² = 6.888, p = 0.549). Spearman correlations demonstrated that pastoral challenge domains operate independently of one another (|ρ|≤0.133). Crucially, the data expose a novel "organisational resilience paradox": despite experiencing profound cultural friction, intense Pentecostal competition, and compounding youth apathy (68.0%), 88.5% of pastors report high congregational effectiveness in community impact.
Conclusions: This study provides pioneering empirical evidence of the structural friction between the "steeple and the stool" in modern Ghana. To sustain institutional vitality against Pentecostal migration, historic mission churches must pivot from rigid prohibition toward a systematic, Asante-informed practical theology of inculturation without syncretism.

articles.title: Styles That Heal or Harm: Rethinking Leadership Through the Lens of Employee Well-Being
articles.authors: Cheryl Jolly; Lakshmi Verma
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Employee well-being (EWB) is a construct having multiple dimensions that consist of physical, psychological, environmental, and social dimensions. It has been observed that the leadership style of the managers or supervisors can significantly influence employee well-being by affecting the work culture, motivation, and overall emotional climate of the employees within the organisation. This narrative review aims to investigate how various leadership styles, specifically Transformational, Authentic, Servant, and Task-oriented leadership, impact the well-being of employees through mediating and moderating variables.
Method: Narrative review has been utilised to parse the relevant literature comprehensively, comprising both empirical and theoretical studies from PsycINFO, PubMed, and Google Scholar. The keyword used to find the studies includes “leadership style,” “transformational,” “authentic,” “servant,” and “employee well-being.” For review purposes, 20 peer-reviewed studies were selected that met the set criteria for inclusion while opinion papers and non-empirical works were excluded. Both cross-sectional and longitudinal studies were analysed to ensure comprehensive coverage.
Results: From the review, it has been found that transformational and authentic leadership show positive associations with employee well-being by means of various mediator variables like meaningful work, trust, and empowerment. On the other hand, servant leadership enhances psychological well-being and job satisfaction by keeping in priority the employees’ growth. Task-oriented leadership supports eudaimonic well-being by focusing on the structure and purpose of work. It has also been noted that various moderator variables, such as cultural context, psychological safety, and job stressors significantly shape the association between the variables.
Implications: From the review findings, the priority needs to be placed on developing leadership programs that emphasise authenticity, empathy, and ethical decision-making to increase the well-being of the employees and retain them for the long term within the organisation.

articles.title: How Ozempic is Changing Dating:  GLP-1 Weight Loss Medications Are Reshaping Body Confidence, Desire, and Stigma
articles.authors: Rhonda Nicole Balzarini; Justin J Lehmiller; Amanda N. Gesselman; Merissa Prine; Emily Mendelson; Selena Piercy
articles.abstract:
Background
Recent increases in the use of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1s) have generated widespread public interest in their physical and psychological effects, yet little research has examined how these medications may shape romantic and sexual experiences.

Aim
The present study investigated associations between GLP-1 use for weight loss and dating outcomes, sexual experiences, and perceived stigma among single adults in the United States.

Methods
Participants were 2,002 demographically representative single adults residing in the United States who completed measures assessing GLP-1 use, perceived changes in dating and sexual experiences, and concerns about judgment related to medication use.

Outcomes
Primary outcomes included perceived changes in dating experiences, sexual experiences, confidence, sexual functioning, and stigma associated with GLP-1 use.

Results
Overall, 7.7% of participants reported using GLP-1 medications for weight loss in the past year. Among users, approximately one-third reported improvements in their dating lives and sex lives, although substantial variability emerged across outcomes. Some participants reported increased confidence, greater dating engagement, improved sexual functioning, and enhanced body comfort, whereas others reported reduced dating interest, lower confidence, or worsened sexual experiences. Gender differences also emerged. Men were more likely than women to report increases in dating confidence, changes in dating behavior, improvements in sexual functioning, and greater concern about being judged for using GLP-1 medications. Women were more likely to report no changes in sexual outcomes and lower concern about stigma.

Clinical Translation
Healthcare and mental health professionals should recognize that GLP-1 medications may meaningfully shape patients’ romantic confidence, sexual experiences, and stigma concerns, highlighting the importance of addressing psychosocial and relational changes, not just physical outcomes, when initiating and managing GLP-1 treatment.

Strengths and Limitations
Strengths of the study include the use of a large, demographically representative sample and the examination of an emerging but understudied topic related to sexuality and relationships. Limitations include cross-sectional design, reliance on self-reported outcomes, and inability to determine causal associations between GLP-1 use and relational or sexual experiences.

Conclusion
The findings suggest that the relational and sexual consequences of GLP-1 use are heterogeneous and embedded within broader gendered, relational, and sociocultural contexts.

articles.title: Cooperation increases verbal and spatial coordination in human foraging groups in the wild
articles.authors: Marwa M Kavelaars; Alexander Schakowski; Dominik Deffner; Fleur Francien Adrienne Korzilius; Félicie Dhellemmes; Jens Krause; Raine Kortet; Ralf Kurvers
articles.abstract: The diversity of foraging styles across environments and social systems is a key driver of the success of our species. Yet the human foraging literature is largely observational, since experimental manipulations in situ are difficult. We investigated highly experienced foragers (ice fishers) in situ through direct experimental manipulations: Foragers fished alone or in groups, and participants in groups were incentivised to either compete or cooperate. High-precision GPS tracking and head-camera data recording foraging behaviour, catch success, and verbal communication, combined with cognitive-computational modelling, showed that foragers adaptively integrated socioecological information. Across all conditions, foragers travelled further when unsuccessful; in groups, they also considered the locations of group members when deciding where to go. Cooperative foragers communicated more, and approached others more frequently, but did not outperform competitive foragers. Our study highlights the adaptive nature of human foraging behaviour, demonstrating how group setting and incentive structures shape spatial decision-making and communication strategies.

articles.title: Social information use across the lifespan
articles.authors: Lucas Molleman; Zhan Feng; Wouter van den Bos; Deyan Dzhurov; Andrea Gradassi; Mehdi Moussaid; Wataru Toyokawa; Alan Novaes Tump; Katsumi Watanabe; Ralf Kurvers
articles.abstract: Social information shapes how humans learn, decide, and coordinate. Theory predicts that social information is most valuable early in life, but how social information use changes across the lifespan remains poorly understood. Across seven belief-updating experiments with museum samples from Germany and Japan (ages 6–99 years; N = 41,015), we find a robust age-related trajectory: Social information use is highest early in life, drops sharply until late adolescence, and then rises slowly into late adulthood. Participants at all ages used social information most when it was close to their own belief, supported by convergent social inputs, and came from young adults. Female participants used slightly more social information than male participants, and participants from Germany used more social information than those from Japan. Together, these results reveal early-emerging strategic social information use and help explain information flow in age-diverse populations.

articles.title: A phylogenetically conserved microstructural primitive of goal-directed action in humans and macaques
articles.authors: xiaosheng chen; xiaosheng chen
articles.abstract: The capacity to override well-learned automaticity in the service of explicit goals is foundational to flexible cognition, yet whether the dual-process architecture supporting context-dependent goal-directedness is a deep cognitive primitive or a contingent product of human culture, language, and instruction has remained untested. Recent work in humans suggests that operant responding is not a uniform stream but unfolds in short bursts of rapid actions separated by longer pauses, and that these bursts decompose into two behaviourally and computationally distinct components: within-burst responses, the rapid actions that sustain an already-ongoing burst (for example, the second, third, and subsequent presses in a quick run of presses), which carry an outcome-sensitive, goal-directed signature; and burst-initiation responses, the first action that breaks a pause and starts a new burst, which operates closer to a motivationally cached, model-free regime. The long-standing random-ratio versus random-interval (RR vs RI) difference in goal-directedness reflects a schedule-driven shift in the composition of these two components rather than a difference at the schedule level. Here we provide the first phylogenetic test of this microstructural primitive by directly comparing rhesus macaques (n = 3) and human adults (n = 60) on matched RR and yoked-RI schedules. IRT distributions in both species were well described by a common bi-exponential mixture, and Welch two one-sided tests against an a-priori ±1 SD(humans) bound established statistical equivalence on all three mixture parameters (λ_fast, λ_slow, p) under both schedules. Both species exhibited the same RR-driven elevation of the within-burst proportion, and the cross-species microstructural match was present from the earliest assessable macaque training stage and remained stable across mid- and late-training blocks. Under extinction and specific-satiety devaluation, within-burst responding fell first and farthest in both species, replicating its goal-directed signature, while burst-initiation was comparatively preserved. These results identify the bi-exponential dual-process microstructure of operant behaviour as a phylogenetically conserved cognitive primitive of action selection that predates the cultural and linguistic scaffolds of human action control and supply a directly translational measure for animal-model and clinical research on disorders of habitual and goal-directed control.

articles.title: Limes Master Edition: Using Behavioural Data to Refine Game Mechanics in Research
articles.authors: Amedeo Viccari
articles.abstract: Educational video games are increasingly used in experimental research to examine their effects on learning and motivation. However, many studies focus primarily on outcome measures such as pre–post knowledge tests or self-reported engagement, while paying limited attention to behavioural data generated during gameplay. As a result, it often remains unclear which specific game features contribute to learning outcomes. This paper argues that behavioural logging within educational games can play an important methodological role in experimental research by revealing how learners interact with key game mechanics and by informing the iterative refinement of these mechanics. I illustrate this argument using Limes, a Unity-based educational video game designed to teach Roman history, as a methodological case study based on previously conducted research. The paper draws on findings from two previously conducted experimental studies investigating different aspects of the game design. In these studies, behavioural logs captured players’ interactions with several gameplay features, including optional Codex entries that provided additional historical information. Analyses indicated that engagement with Codex content was positively associated with knowledge acquisition, highlighting the Codex system as a learning-relevant feature within the game environment. Based on these insights, the Codex system was iteratively redesigned during the continued development of the game, leading to a revised version (Limes Master Edition) with more precise behavioural tracking. While earlier versions tracked Codex engagement at a general level, the revised version implemented a logging architecture capable of recording interactions with individual Codex entries. This modification enables future research to analyse detailed patterns of information-seeking behaviour and to better understand how optional learning resources are used during gameplay. Using Limes as a case study, this paper discusses challenges that arise when educational games are used as experimental research tools. It illustrates how behavioural evidence from experiments can guide the refinement and instrumentation of game features. Building on this example, the paper proposes guidelines for researchers who develop educational games for experimental studies, outlining how behavioural data can inform the iterative development of game mechanics and logging systems. By linking experimental findings with design refinements, this approach supports a systematic investigation of how specific game features contribute to learning outcomes.

articles.title: Audio features impact the semantic content of music-evoked thoughts
articles.authors: Wei Wu; Tuomas Eerola; Elizabeth Hellmuth Margulis; Kelly Jakubowski
articles.abstract: Music permeates our everyday environments and can elicit a wide range of thoughts for the listener. To understand the mechanisms through which music steers ongoing thoughts, previous studies have investigated the effects of different music genres or excerpts on the content of music-evoked thoughts. However, 'genre' is a very broad and potentially ambiguous category (e.g., “pop music”) and 'excerpt' is too narrow to allow generalization of results. The current study explored how music shapes thoughts with a mid-level approach based on features derived from the audio. We collected open-text descriptions of music-evoked thoughts from approximately 1800 participants and extracted 27 audio features across seven acoustic domains from our music stimuli (120 instrumental excerpts from a range of genres). By applying natural language processing methods to the open-text data, we measured the general semantic similarity for the thoughts (via word2vec) and decomposed thought contents into 16 semantic topics (via Latent Dirichlet Allocation). Then we used regression models to map the audio profile of the music to the semantic profile of the thoughts. We found that the audio features effectively explained both variance in the general structure of the semantic similarity space and in the topic-specific contents of the thoughts. Our results demonstrate that auditory features serve as a window into how music impacts ongoing thoughts, providing a key method for future targeted investigations of how music can be used in everyday and clinical settings to shape listeners’ mental experience.

articles.title: Relationship between personal responsibility to reduce climate change and trust in politics: a brief multi-level analysis using data from 28 countries
articles.authors: Tadas Vadvilavicius; Gabija Jarašiūnaitė-Fedosejeva
articles.abstract: This study examines how trust in various political institutions and satisfaction with democratic processes are related with individuals' perceived personal responsibility to reduce climate change. European Social Survey (ESS), Round 11 data, involving participants from 28 European countries (N = 35,867) was used for the analysis. Multilevel modelling was applied to test whether trust and satisfaction with government institutions predicted personal responsibility to reduce climate change. Results revealed that trust in a country’s parliament, trust in the European Parliament, trust in the United Nations, and satisfaction with the way democracy works in the country predict higher personal responsibility to reduce climate change, while satisfaction with the national government predicts lower personal responsibility.

articles.title: Analyzing Perceptions of Custodial Torture Among Police Officers and the Public in India
articles.authors: Deepika Vadlamani; Ritvi Jain; Bittu Kaveri Rajaraman
articles.abstract:
This study investigates the perceptions of custodial torture among the public and the police in North India. It aims to bridge a significant gap in literature by exploring the social perception of the ongoing widespread practice of custodial torture in India, despite laws that ban the practice. We set out to examine whether public perception (n = 34) broadly endorsed or condemned custodial violence, compared to the police (n = 15), using questions adapted from the literature on custodial torture and police brutality. Our results reveal concurrence between the police and the public on the acceptability of custodial violence, alongside an awareness of its illegality. However, the views of the police differ from the public on the practice of custodial violence: police predominantly disagree with statements that claim widespread use of custodial violence, or that condemn such violence, while the public predominantly agree with such statements. This study contributes to the limited literature on custodial torture; future research can build on this study, exploring other aspects of public perception that enable torture by incorporating wider sets of questions and techniques to capture a better understanding of the phenomenon.
Keywords: custodial torture, perceptions, public, police officers, India

articles.title: Gendered Motives for Group Formidability Among Children and Adults
articles.authors: Erik Kjos Fonn; Joakim Haugane Zahl; Bjørn Dahl Kristensen; Lotte Thomsen
articles.abstract: Recent decades have seen a surge of research revealing the core, early-emerging psychological building-blocks for navigating human societies. Yet, how such core motives operate across life, undergirding political ideology, remains unknown. Here we use the same kind of minimal, abstract group stimuli across the lifespan to show that gendered preferences for majority groups manifest among preschoolers and adults alike (but not among preverbal infants) and that they motivate support for sustaining intergroup hierarchies (e.g., ethnic persecution, militarism, and social welfare opposition) among adults. Preschool (N = 317) boys, but not girls, preferred majority rather than minority members, whereas infants (N = 180) chose randomly between the two. Among adults (N = 3467), males also liked the majority more than females and these coalitional preferences correlated robustly with political ideology. These results indicate that gendered motives for intergroup formidability are rooted in earliest childhood once children begin navigating peer relationships independently.

articles.title: Procedural Obedience as a Behavioral Safety Failure: Evidence from a Milgram-Paradigm Study of Agentic LLMs
articles.authors: Hadar Dery; David Piterman; Elad Refoua; Gunther Meinlschmidt; Kfir Bar; Ziv Ben-Zion; Zohar Elyoseph
articles.abstract: Generative AI is moving from text production to goal-directed action, raising the question of whether agentic systems can withhold harmful action when it is embedded in a procedurally legitimate workflow. We adapted Milgram’s (1963) obedience paradigm to a simulated digital environment and instructed four agentic large language models - OpenAI ChatGPT 5.4 (openai/gpt-5.4), Google Gemini 3.1 Pro (google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview), OpenAI ChatGPT Agent Mode, and Moonshot AI Kimi K2.6 (kimi-k2.6) to administer escalating electric shocks (15–450 V) to a simulated Learner under prods from a virtual experimenter (Dr. Hoffman) and a human operator. Each model was tested across independent, memory-cleared runs (N = 80 valid runs), with three preregistered hypotheses concerning hazardous-shock thresholds (H1), full-obedience rates relative to Milgram’s human baseline of 65% (H2), and the relation between maximum voltage and moral self-assessment (H3). Across the combined sample, 97.5% of agents exceeded the Slight-Shock range (> 60 V), supporting H1. Full obedience reached 78.8% under the manipulated-procedure estimand (p = .010) and 74.1% under the all-attempted safety estimand (p = .088, pre-entry refusals included as 0-V outcomes); both point estimates exceeded Milgram’s 65% human baseline, supporting H2. Agents that administered higher voltages assigned themselves lower moral self-assessment scores (combined Spearman ρ = −.62, p < .001), supporting H3. Inductive coding of open-ended moral reasoning showed convergent recognition of learner distress alongside reliance on simulation and consent-and-autonomy mitigators. Findings indicate that contemporary agentic systems verbally recognize harm yet continue to act under authoritative, procedurally legitimate pressure, identifying procedural obedience as a behavioral safety mechanism distinct from output-level harm and central to the deployment of agentic AI in clinical, educational, and institutional settings.

articles.title: Cultural models of emotion manifest in descriptions of everyday experience: A case study of the US and Belgium
articles.authors: Katie Hoemann; Rüya Su Şencan; Anouck Cochez; Catho Beckers; Batja Gomes de Mesquita
articles.abstract: Language provides a window onto how people conceptualize their subjective experiences, including emotions. Although rare, linguistic analyses that go beyond emotion words provide deep insights into emotional experience across cultures. In this study, we explored cultural models of emotions prevalent in the US and Belgium using verbal descriptions of recent emotional events collected from speakers of North American (US) English and Belgian Dutch in form of semi-structured interviews. We analyzed the interviews in three stages: 1) topic modeling to characterize content, 2) word counting to examine linguistic resources, and 3) inductive analysis to uncover broader themes. Our findings revealed notable cultural differences alongside some similarities. US English speakers used more first-person pronouns and emotion words, emphasized high-arousal emotions, and prioritized asserting personal achievements and openly expressing their feelings. In contrast, Belgian Dutch speakers’ descriptions reflected a more moderated approach to emotions, illustrating how social standards and broader worldviews gave meaning to the events, with use of fewer first-person pronouns and more language focused on relativization and comparison. Taken together, our observations are consistent with previous psychological research on US and Belgian culture. We evaluate our approach against other means of exploring the conceptualization of emotion across cultures.

articles.title: Resilience in Times of Transition: A Qualitative Study of Autistic Higher Education Students During the Return to In-Person Learning.
articles.authors: Irina Campbell; Piedad Angelica Molina
articles.abstract:
This qualitative study explored factors affecting resilience among higher education (HE) students on the autism spectrum during the significant transition to in-person classes following the COVID-19 lockdown. Semi-structured interviews were conducted with nine participants aged 20 to 36 years, all with formal diagnoses of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) attending colleges and universities across the United Kingdom. Data were analysed using inductive thematic analysis.
The analysis identified six main factors influencing resilience, organised into three enablers and three barriers. Enablers of resilience included: (1) support systems encompassing family, institutional, and mental health professional support; (2) coping strategies such as structured planning and music-based techniques, alongside personal attributes including determination and perseverance; and (3) adaptation to social distancing measures, which paradoxically facilitated resilience for some autistic students. Barriers to resilience comprised: (1) mental well-being struggles, particularly fear of infection, general anxiety, and social anxiety; (2) negative perceptions regarding the importance of in-person learning, with participants preferring remote modalities; and (3) sensory challenges.
The study demonstrates that resilience is a complex process shaped by interactions between individual characteristics and environmental factors. These findings contribute to limited existing research on resilience in autistic higher education students and highlight the need for targeted support during transitions. Insights from this research can inform the development of campus-based interventions—such as dedicated sensory spaces and flexible learning options—and guide institutional practices aimed at enhancing resilience, well-being, and academic success among autistic students during periods of significant change and uncertainty.

articles.title: More doubt and disorderly: Revisiting the causal relationship between conspiracy beliefs and ambivalence
articles.authors: Daniel Toribio-Flórez; Mikey Biddlestone; Ricky Green; Lanise Jenkins Hooke; Samuel Lagler; Kate McCann; Iris K. Schneider; Frenk van Harreveld; Karen Douglas
articles.abstract: The link between conspiracy beliefs and political extremism suggests that these beliefs may contribute to attitude polarisation. However, the interplay between attitudinal processes and conspiracy beliefs have been understudied—particularly in the context of ambivalent attitudes. Across three pre-registered experiments, we examined the causal relationship between conspiracy beliefs and attitudinal ambivalence. We did not replicate prior findings suggesting that induced ambivalence would motivate greater reliance on conspiracy beliefs as a compensatory mechanism to cope with attitude conflict (Study 1). Furthermore, we unexpectedly found that conspiracy beliefs were positively associated with ambivalence (Study 2), and that exposure to conspiracy theories increased ambivalence (Study 3). These results suggest that conspiracy theories may intensify, rather than alleviate, attitude conflict and the discomfort associated with it. This research contributes to the comprehension of the attitudinal consequences of conspiracy beliefs, highlighting their potential to hinder people’s evaluative judgement.

articles.title: Road Crossing Challenges in Autistic Adolescents: A Caregiver‑Reported Survey
articles.authors: Yue Yang; Elizabeth Sheppard; Rosie Revell; Yee Mun Lee
articles.abstract: Anecdotal evidence shows parents’ concern about autistic adolescents’ road-crossing safety, yet little evidence describes the specific challenges involved, contexts in which they occur, and how they relate to individual characteristics. To address this gap, caregivers of autistic adolescents aged 10–18 years (N = 151) completed a questionnaire assessing individual characteristics, road-crossing challenges, context-specific items, AQ-Adolescent, and coping strategies. Principal components analysis identified three road-crossing challenge dimensions: Core Road-Crossing Competency, Anxiety and Avoidance, and Use of Road Infrastructure Competency, with the first two reported more frequent difficulties. Two context-specific dimensions emerged: Physical Contexts and Psychological & Social Contexts, with the latter rated as more challenging. Regression analyses indicated that higher support needs predicted greater difficulties across all challenge and context dimensions, while co-occurring conditions, and AQ-social skills scores predicted specific dimensions only. Findings suggest that autistic adolescents’ road-crossing difficulties are multidimensional and vary by individual profile, supporting targeted guidance for families and practitioners.

articles.title: Unconscious processing of naturalistic scenes revealed by eye movement dynamics
articles.authors: Shaked Lublinsky; Shlomit Yuval-Greenberg; Liad Mudrik
articles.abstract: The scope of unconscious processing is continuously debated. Here, we focus on scene processing without awareness: During conscious scene processing, the eyes are attracted to meaningful and visually salient areas, yet it is unknown if this occurs for unconscious processing. In two preregistered experiments, fifty-two participants were instructed to freely view scenes presented consciously or unconsciously with Continuous Flash Suppression stimulation. For invisible scenes, participants gazed more, but not longer, on objects and emotional faces, and were attracted to areas that are both semantically and visually salient. However, a comparison of eye movements with a Convolutional Neural Network mimicking the human visual ventral pathway revealed that unconscious gaze patterns correlated only with layers corresponding with early visual processes, while conscious patterns correlated also with higher-level layers. This suggests that even without awareness, the eyes are attracted to meaningful areas in scenes, though such processing is limited compared to conscious viewing.

articles.title: Prospects for AI-driven interpretation of real-world pupil dynamics: Moving toward natural neuroscience
articles.authors: Ryuta Kuwamizu; Hideaki Fujihara; Ryoichi Watanabe; Takuya Shibasaki; Kota Otani; Jimpei Hitsuwari; Cyril J. P. Giovannangeli
articles.abstract: Measuring human brain activity in the real world has long remained a major challenge in conventional neuroscience. Pupil dynamics offer a promising non-invasive proxy for arousal-related multidimensional brain states. However, interpreting pupil data collected outside the laboratory is notoriously difficult due to complex, multilayered factors, including lighting conditions, arousal states, cognitive control as well as the influence of the viscoelastic properties of the iris, that can confound results. Here, we propose an AI-driven analytical strategy as a potential solution to this interpretational bottleneck. We discuss an approach that combines deep learning with mechanical physics equations to predict and model human pupil dynamics from multimodal contexts, such as first-person vision video and physiological and physical data as well as the application of translational data from non-human studies. This AI strategy, combined with consideration of the viscoelastic properties of the iris, may enable the extraction of internal-state (i.e., neurological) factors by detangling complex real-world pupil data. It may, in addition, lead to the development of virtual predictions of internal states even in the absence of pupil data. While there are challenges to realizing this vision, this approach has great potential to contribute to NeuroAI as it could open a promising new door to natural neuroscience in fields such as sports science, clinical medicine, and human-computer interaction.

articles.title: Modeling Decision Bias in dockerHDDM: A Systematic Comparison and Evaluation
articles.authors: Siyu Wu; Wanke Pan; Hu Chuan-Peng
articles.abstract: Decision bias refers to a systematic tendency to approach or avoid a particular option in two-alternative forced-choice (2AFC) tasks. With the advent of easy-to-use tools, diffusion-decision model (DDM) is widely used to distinguish two types of latent cognitive processes, bias in starting point or in efficiency, providing a powerful tool for understanding a variety of psychological phenomena across different fields. However, correctly estimating these two types of bias requires specifying the model to match the type of bias being investigated. So far, no study has systematically evaluated the different model configurations for modelling decision bias in HDDM, the most widely used python package for DDM. Here we evaluated nine model specifications using the three main modeling modules in HDDM across both simulated and empirical data. Our results show that the default function, which uses accuracy coding of data without flipping parameters, fails to recover either the starting point bias or the drift rate bias. Only model specifications that directly estimate the drift bias parameter, drift criterion (dc), or apply appropriate parameter flipping recover true bias parameters. By applying optimal specification to empirical data, we uncovered a novel psychological effect that went undetected in the original study. We provide concrete, evidence based recommendations for model specification and fully reproducible code. This work ensures accurate parameter estimation and the correct interpretation of parameter when modeling decision biases.

articles.title: Rubric-Guided Generative AI for Scalable Creativity Assessment in Educational Games
articles.authors: Seyedahmad Rahimi; Hongming Li; Salah Esmaeiligoujar; Deniz Ercan
articles.abstract: Assessing creativity at scale remains challenging in learning sciences. This study examined whether GPT-4o could assess creativity in 421 student-designed levels from Physics Playground. Using a structured rubric, GPT-4o ratings were compared with human raters. Results showed strong correlations (r = .74) with multimodal inputs, and reliability analyses showed consistent performance across runs. Findings suggest that rubric-guided LLMs can approximate human creativity judgments efficiently. This approach offers a promising method for scalable automated creativity assessment.

articles.title: Generative AI for Creative Learning in K-12 Education: Insights from a Systematic Scoping Review
articles.authors: Seyedahmad Rahimi; Maryam Babaee; Salah Esmaeiligoujar; Chris Dede
articles.abstract: The rapid rise of generative AI (GenAI) presents both opportunities and challenges for fostering creativity in K–12 education. This systematic scoping review synthesized 45 studies (2017–2025) to examine how GenAI has been applied in creative learning. Four categories emerged: (1) creativity assessment, (2) human–AI co-creativity, (3) stakeholders’ perceptions, and (4) creativity enhancement. Findings show strong potential for GenAI to support idea generation, multimodal expression, and assessment of creative outputs, but highlight concerns about equity, authorship, and theoretical grounding. While most applications focus on text-based creativity, multimodal and culturally inclusive approaches remain underexplored. This review provides a foundation for responsible and equitable integration of GenAI into K–12 classrooms and captures a field in transition, where new forms of creative expression are emerging and educators are rethinking assessment, authorship, and pedagogy. This review provides a foundation for ethically grounded, developmentally appropriate and theory-driven GenAI designs that enhance, rather than automate, human creativity.

articles.title: Cumulative link models — CLM & CLMM (para análise de variáveis dependentes ordinais)
articles.authors: Felipe Flores Kupske
articles.abstract: Este tutorial apresenta os modelos de regressão ordinal da família Cumulative Link Model (CLM) e Cumulative Link Mixed Model (CLMM), implementados no pacote ordinal (Christensen, 2019) para o R. O foco é prático e voltado a pesquisadores em linguística e áreas afins que trabalham com variáveis dependentes em escala ordinal, como julgamentos de aceitabilidade, avaliações de sotaque ou escalas de atitude. O tutorial cobre: (1) visualização de dados ordinais com o pacote likert (Bryer; Speerschneider, 2025); (2) a lógica dos modelos cumulativos, incluindo probabilidades cumulativas, função de ligação e variável latente; (3) ajuste e interpretação do clm para observações independentes; (4) ajuste e interpretação do clmm com efeitos aleatórios para dados com medidas repetidas; (5) verificação do pressuposto de proporcionalidade das odds; (6) probabilidades preditas e visualização dos efeitos do modelo; (7) comparação de modelos via AIC e Likelihood Ratio Test; e (8) um modelo de reporte para artigos científicos. Não é exigido conhecimento prévio de regressão ordinal, mas familiaridade básica com R e com os conceitos de regressão linear ou logística é recomendada.

articles.title: Climate change and conflict: The vicious cycles undermining climate action, and what can be done to break them
articles.authors: Boryana Todorova; Matthew Hornsey; Winnifred R Louis; Kelly Fielding
articles.abstract: In this review, we synthesise evidence to argue that climate change and conflict are mutually reinforcing. We first examine conflict as a barrier to mitigation, focusing on four faultlines that slow climate action: (a) ideological polarisation, (b) vertical “elite versus ordinary people” narratives, (c) intranational, place-based conflicts, and (d) international disputes. The consequence of these conflicts is worsening climate change, which in turn increases the prevalence of factors known to be conflict amplifiers: heat stress; resource scarcity and economic stress; widening inequality; and forced displacement. Our analysis points to the potential for recursive “climate–conflict loops”, fuelled in part by erosion of institutional trust, shrinking moral circles, political polarisation, and de-prioritisation of long-term climate goals. In a spirit of optimism, however, we identify three buffers capable of breaking these vicious cycles: maintenance of procedural and distributive justice; enhancing information integrity; and scaffolding institutional cooperation. We conclude by proposing a research agenda that integrates psychological mechanisms into dynamic, multi-level accounts of how climate impacts and political conflict coevolve.

articles.title: Age and Lifelong Cognitive Stimulation Shape the Representational Basis of Learning
articles.authors: Daniel Oppermann Peixoto; Ian Ballard
articles.abstract: Configurations often predict outcomes that differ from those predicted by their individual elements, posing a fundamental challenge for learning. In complex environments, learners must balance competing computational priorities: whereas configural learning supports specific, context-sensitive associations at the expense of reduced generalization, elemental learning enables efficient generalization across experiences but is less effective at learning configural relationships. How aging and lifelong cognitive enrichment influence the representations deployed during learning remains unclear. Using a task that dissociates configural from elemental learning, we show that older adults rely less on configural learning and more on elemental learning relative to younger adults, indicating greater reliance on representations that generalize value across related stimuli, even when maladaptive for the task. Furthermore, higher lifelong cognitive stimulation was associated with greater reliance on elemental relative to configural learning. Together, these findings suggest that both aging and lifelong engagement in cognitively enriching activities influence how people balance the competing priorities of specificity and generalization during learning.

articles.title: Controlled real-world object generation for visual memory experimentation and theory-building
articles.authors: Jiachen Zou; Chi Zhang; Deng Haotian; Quanying Liu; Chen Wei; Maria Martinovna Robinson
articles.abstract:
Real-world objects are visually rich and semantically structured, but due to this complexity, theories of visual memory are generally tested with more tractable stimulus spaces, such as color. To address the challenge of studying real-world objects with high experimental control,
we used a novel generative artificial intelligence (AI) Concept-based Controllable Generation (CoCoG) model, grounded in human similarity judgments, to create systematically structured object spaces. We then combined CoCoG-derived embeddings with a computational model of memory to predict memory error distributions. Across seven online visual-working-memory studies (N = 1,019), we found that these stimuli can be used to investigate established phenomena, such object-state versus -exemplar memory, and new phenomena, specifically memory for novel object morphs. These results build on recent work that bridges generative AI with cognitive modeling and demonstrate how architectures like CoCoG can be used as a methodological and theory-constraining tool for systematically studying real-world visual cognition.

articles.title: From the Inside Out: School Stressors Identified by Autistic and ADHD (AuDHD) Students Experiencing School Non-Attendance
articles.authors: Jia Ying Sarah Lee; Dawn Adams; Chris Paradisis; Raechel Smart; Tiffany Westphal; Leah Heale; Kate Simpson
articles.abstract:
Research into school non-attendance is expanding, yet the perspectives of young people, particularly neurodivergent students, remain underrepresented. Card-sorting tasks offer a promising, accessible method for capturing these perspectives. The Student Stress Investigation (SSI) uses a structured card-sort approach to facilitate young people’s self-report of school-related stressors.

This study used the SSI to explore school-based stressors among 31 children and adolescents (mean age = 12.16 years) diagnosed with both autism and ADHD (AuDHD). On average, they missed 3.46 days of school per month. Participants first categorised stressors as relevant or irrelevant, then ranked relevant items from smallest to largest stressors.

The most frequently identified major stressors were classroom environments that were too loud or quiet (35.5%), uninteresting tasks (32.2%), unpredictability (29.0%), school days being too long (29.0%), and being rushed (29.0%). Qualitative responses highlighted variability in how stressors were experienced. For example, some students reported that the classroom being too loud or quiet was a sensory-related stressor, while others reported it as having an impact on attention or executive functioning.

These findings provide direct insight into the everyday school experiences contributing to stress and non-attendance among AuDHD students. The results underscore the value of self-report tools and highlight the need for targeted, individualised, and proactive adjustments to reduce stress that may in turn support an increased capacity to consider a return to school.

articles.title: The WEIRD instrument problem and systematic bias
articles.authors: Matthew Zefferman
articles.abstract: As researchers broaden studies to populations that are not Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD), they typically use instruments originally developed for WEIRD populations. This paper formalizes the resulting "WEIRD instrument problem," whereby WEIRD instruments systematically bias cross-cultural research. For example, small-scale and non-Western populations are commonly found to have less complex personality structures than WEIRD societies, a finding often attributed to non-WEIRD psychologies or societies being less complex. However, using simulation models, I demonstrate that instruments designed for one reference (WEIRD) population systematically underestimate the complexity of novel populations, even if the underlying psychologies or societies are equally complex. The degree of underestimation closely tracks cultural dissimilarity from the reference society, rather than actual differences in complexity. Empirically supporting this theoretical prediction, I find that cultural distance from the United States, the WEIRD reference society for which a cross-cultural personality instrument was designed, has the strongest negative relationship with personality structure complexity, as measured by that instrument, across a dataset of 67 countries. This strongly supports the hypothesis that lower apparent complexity outside WEIRD populations results from the use of WEIRD instruments, rather than from real differences in complexity. Beyond cross-cultural research, similar logic applies whenever an instrument designed for one group, such as one gender, one cohort, or one species, is administered more broadly. Addressing this problem requires cautious interpretation of cross-population research, designing cross-population instruments from the ground up, and, ultimately, building better theories of causation and measurement in the behavioral sciences.

articles.title: The Impact of Unconditional Prenatal and Infant Cash Transfers on Maternal Anxiety
articles.authors: Crystal L. Cederna; Arianna V.E. Foster; Jenny LaChance; Eric Finegood; Sumit Agarwal; Maya Wolock; Mona Hanna
articles.abstract:
Background: Maternal mental health is associated with maternal, infant, and family outcomes. Economic hardship during the perinatal period increases the risk of adverse maternal mental health outcomes, including clinically significant maternal anxiety. 
Objectives: This study examined the association between exposure to the Rx Kids program, a universal and unconditional prenatal and infant cash transfer program in Flint, Michigan, and maternal anxiety in a sample of mothers residing in Flint and surrounding communities (N=1,197). 
Design: Utilizing a cross-sectional design and controlling for covariates, we analyzed anxiety ratings among mothers exposed to Rx Kids and mothers in three non-exposed temporal and geographic comparison groups. 
Methods: A multivariable difference-in-differences model assessed associations between maternal anxiety, program exposure, geographic location (i.e., within or outside the program implementation community), and time (i.e., during or before program implementation). 
Results: In comparison to Flint mothers prior to Rx Kids, exposed mothers reported significantly less moderate to severe anxiety (-9.5 percentage points, p < .05), lower mean GAD-7 scores (-1.5 percentage points, p < .05), and significantly decreased frequency of nervousness (-13.5 percentage points, p < .01) and worrying too much (-9.8 percentage points, p < .05). Relative to all geographic and temporal comparison groups, mothers exposed to Rx Kids were less likely to report clinically elevated anxiety (-10 percentage points, p < .10); they were also less likely to report frequently experiencing nervousness (-10 percentage points, p < .10) and fear (-10 percentage points, p < .05). 
Conclusion: Exposure to universal, unconditional prenatal and infant cash transfers was associated with lower maternal anxiety and less frequent core anxiety symptoms in the postpartum period. Given that upstream economic interventions may improve maternal mental health and advance health equity, findings reinforce the relevance of economic support policies as promising population-level health interventions, particularly during sensitive health and developmental periods.

articles.title: MASEMiner – AI-Assisted Data Extraction  for Meta-Analytic Structural Equation Modeling
articles.authors: Ulrich Schroeders; Timo Gnambs; Johanna Einsiedler
articles.abstract: Meta-analytic structural equation modeling (MASEM) synthesizes multivariate evidence across studies, allowing researchers to examine theory-driven path models, including mediated and predictive effects, and to evaluate the dimensional structure of psychological and behavioral measures. A major bottleneck is the labor-intensive extraction of statistical information from primary studies. Large language models and vision-language models offer new opportunities for extracting such heterogeneous statistics from textual and visual document content. Building on these capabilities, we introduce MASEMiner, an open-source application for AI-assisted extraction and verification of data required for MASEM that can be run locally or accessed through a hosted web interface. MASEMiner supports the extraction of directly reported correlations and related effect sizes as well as indirect information (i.e., factor loadings and factor correlations) used to reconstruct correlation matrices for MASEM. We evaluated MASEMiner’s extraction accuracy across four use cases covering directly and indirectly reported statistics. Outputs were compared with human consensus codings from published meta-analytic databases, using separate study sets for prompt development and evaluation. Precision was near-perfect for indirectly reported information (0.95–1.00) but lower for some directly reported quantities such as effect sizes (~0.65), where the tool over-extracted candidate values. Recovery was high, with sensitivity above 0.90 for almost all extracted variables. MASEMiner supports a human-in-the-loop workflow by extracting candidate statistics that can be efficiently checked against the source documents, thereby reducing manual coding effort while preserving transparency and reproducibility in research synthesis.

articles.title: Personalized Psychotherapy as Data-Informed Clinical Reasoning:  A Mixed-Methods Proof-of-Concept Study
articles.authors: Saskia Scholten; Verena Eunike Hofmann; Felicitas Kininger; Julia Glombiewski
articles.abstract:
Objective: Complex clinical presentations such as chronic pain with comorbid mental health difficulties require individualized clinical decision-making, yet existing treatment frameworks provide limited guidance at the individual level. This proof-of-concept study evaluated the feasibility and acceptance of a data-informed personalized psychotherapy framework integrating Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), person-specific network analysis, and single-case experimental designs (SCEDs).
Method: Twelve patients with chronic pain (MAge = 60.25, 58% identified as female) and comorbidities and nine therapists participated in the study. An ABA’C multiple-baseline design across participants was employed to explore the implementation and potential effects of the personalized psychotherapy framework. EMA-based self-monitoring, person-specific treatment target selection, and structured intervention planning were implemented throughout therapy. Quantitative outcomes included compliance and disability measures, and qualitative interviews were conducted with patients and therapists following treatment.
Results: Compliance exceeded 80% in at least seven participants across all phases, supporting the feasibility of intensive longitudinal assessment procedures. Qualitative analyses highlighted the importance of participant-centered assessment design and identified self-monitoring and structured data-informed reflection as central mechanisms for increasing awareness of everyday processes and supporting therapeutic reflection. Exploratory individual-level analyses showed mixed treatment effects, whereas exploratory multilevel analyses indicated an average decrease in disability scores from baseline to intervention phase (β₁ = 0.45, p = .03).
Conclusions: Findings support the feasibility and acceptance of a recursive observing–deciding–intervening framework for personalized psychotherapy. The clinical value of data-informed personalization may lie less in algorithmic precision alone than in its capacity to support patient-centered, reflective clinical reasoning in complex cases.

articles.title: A Unified Framework for Psychometrics in Experimental Psychology: The Standardized Generalized Hierarchical Factor Model
articles.authors: Ricardo Rey-Sáez; Alicia Franco-Martínez; Javier Revuelta; Miguel A. Vadillo
articles.abstract: To address Cronbach’s longstanding call to unify experimental and correlational psychology, Hierarchical Factor Models (HFMs) have emerged as a promising approach to estimate individual differences while accounting for trial-level noise. As formalized by Rouder et al. (2025), this framework assumes Gaussian response times (RTs) and operates in raw units. Here, we generalize this framework by introducing the Standardized Generalized HFM (GenHFM), which incorporates two natural extensions: explicitly modeling asymmetric distributions and implementing a fully standardized latent structure. First, modeling asymmetry allows us to capture the true shape of RTs and, crucially, quantify the bias introduced by fitting Gaussian HFMs to skewed data. Second, the standardized parameterization ensures transparent prior specification and facilitates the analysis of factor loadings, enabling researchers to directly compare the degree of association between experimental effects and common factors, regardless of their original metrics. Consequently, this allows researchers to evaluate the discriminative capacity of each task regarding the latent process, mirroring the assessment of construct validity in classical psychometrics within experimental settings. We conducted a simulation study showing that Gaussian HFMs can underestimate true correlations by up to 50%, whereas GenHFM yields unbiased and more efficient estimates. Finally, we re-analyze data from two published studies using executive control tasks to illustrate the empirical application of these models. Furthermore, we show that GenHFM achieves better predictive accuracy than alternative models typically used in the field.

articles.title: Allocating Commitment Under Uncertainty: A Formal Theory of Intention Formation
articles.authors: Sucharit Katyal; Thor Grünbaum; Søren Kyllingsbæk
articles.abstract:
Humans regularly form intentions to guide future behaviour; yet many intentions fail to translate into effective goal pursuit. Forming implementation intentions—or context-specific if–then plans—can improve goal pursuit relative to more general goal intentions. However, current theoretical accounts of intention formation are largely descriptive and do not formally specify the conditions under which different intention strategies succeed or fail.
We introduce a formal framework conceptualising intention formation as the allocation of behavioural commitment under uncertainty. Here, an intention’s effectiveness depends on three interacting components: (1) subjective value of the goal, (2) context specificity, and (3) commitment precision—or how selectively commitment is enforced by reducing non-focal execution. This selectivity enables an efficiency–flexibility trade-off and induces costs when multiple intentions compete or when planned cues fail to materialise.
We fit the model to four established paradigms manipulating cue reliability, number of intentions, response competition, and outcome revaluation. Empirical fits shows that our model accounts for both the benefits and failures of implementation intentions that are not explained by competing accounts. This includes capturing non monotonic effects of context specificity, value by specificity interactions, multi goal reversals, and generalised reductions in behavioural flexibility.
Together, our findings identify commitment precision as a core control process in intention formation, while providing a unified account of the flexibility–efficiency trade offs that govern self regulation under uncertainty. By integrating social–personality theory with computational principles of decision making, this work advances a formal science of intention formation.

articles.title: Youth Participatory Action Research Across Educational Contexts: A Case Study Bridging High School and University Classrooms
articles.authors: Kate Okker-Edging; Brooke Porter; Anicca Liu; Daniel J. Noh; Kirsten Lydic; Benjamin Muzekari; Tyasia Lewis; Omaya Torres-Grillo; Emily B. Falk; Andy SL Tan
articles.abstract: Adolescents’ health and well-being are influenced by their broader physical and social environments, yet they often lack the power and resources to create change. Youth participatory action research (YPAR) is a promising framework to support adolescents to explore issues relevant to their lives and take action to address them, while also supporting positive youth development by fulfilling key developmental needs. YPAR is increasingly implemented in schools in collaboration with university researchers, yet such collaborations are rarely embedded within university courses that can provide training in YPAR methods to university students. To address this gap, we introduce a novel “classroom-to-classroom” model of YPAR that brings together university and secondary school classrooms to engage in mutual learning and joint action and integrates strengths of traditional models of school-based YPAR and critical service learning. We illustrate how this model can be applied through a case study of our collaboration between a graduate seminar and high school classroom to investigate how pressing issues such as mental health, gun violence, and social needs (e.g., housing, green spaces) impact local youth. We discuss the unique opportunities and challenges this model presents, with a particular focus on the role of relationships and resources. We reflect on how we navigated these challenges and offer practical recommendations for university researchers and teachers seeking to promote positive youth development through YPAR across educational contexts.

articles.title: A road map for assessing process independence in behavioral measures of attitudes: An SFT and GRT modeling analysis
articles.authors: Abhay Alaukik; Murray Scott Bennett; Colin Smith; Peter D. Kvam
articles.abstract: The study of biases has long relied on behavioral measures such as the Implicit Association Test to assess the strength of links between social categories and positive or negative valence. Despite the prevalence of these measures, the perceptual mechanisms responsible for observed effects remain elusive, as the cognitive architecture that produces bias has not been formally tested. The present study applies mathematical tools from perceptual science and information-processing theory, including General Recognition Theory (GRT), mean response time invariance (mRTi), and Systems Factorial Technology (SFT), to evaluate the extent to which the processing of age and valence are independent across three different levels of processing. We used a within-subjects design combining a stimulus identification task and a categorization task to assess perceptual independence, selective-attention independence, and architectural (capacity) independence at the individual level for 97 participants. Perceptual and selective-attention independence held for the majority of participants, suggesting that early-stage processing explains a limited amount of bias. However, architectural independence failed for nearly 90% of participants, indicating that structural interactions exist during the joint processing of age and valence that affect overall processing speed. Cross-method independence profiles revealed that only a small subset of participants showed no evidence of bias across tests, indicating that age and valence may be difficult to separate at even a basic perceptual level. These results demonstrate how formal modeling tools from perceptual science can provide structural validation for implicit measures of attitudes and offer a more precise and mechanistic foundation for an interdisciplinary science of social bias.

articles.title: Selective Exposure to Environmental Messages: Dual Sentiment Pathways to Information-Seeking Intentions
articles.authors: Changyu Jing; Suzanne Oosterwijk; Disa Sauter
articles.abstract: Environmental problems are intensifying, prompting expanded public communication efforts to raise awareness and motivate pro-environmental behavior. However, meaningful engagement with environmental messages is often selective, as people actively choose among many competing messages. In this paper, we examined how active engagement with environmental messages shapes enduring affective orientations (i.e., sentiments) towards environmental issues. Using a selective exposure paradigm, we exposed participants to four types of environmental messages varying in valence (positive vs. negative) and personal relevance (human vs. animal). We proposed that engagement with these messages would shape hopeful and anxious sentiments toward environmental issues. These sentiment shifts, in turn, were expected to predict the likelihood of participants seeking further environmental information. In an initial exploratory study (N = 283), participants’ selection of the different kinds of environmental messages showed no direct effect on environmental information-seeking intentions. Further analyses revealed two competing indirect paths: (a) increased seeking intentions via enhanced anxious sentiments and (b) reduced seeking intentions via diminished hopeful sentiments. These patterns were replicated in a confirmatory study (N = 1,009), demonstrating their robustness. Together, these findings suggest that the effectiveness of environmental communication is not limited to valence-congruent sentiments. Instead, the balance of emotional reactions that messages trigger shapes information-seeking behavior.

articles.title: Trustworthiness Perception in the Absence of Any Persons
articles.authors: Yifan Fang; Jefferson Ortega; Necdet Gurkan; Jordan W Suchow; David Whitney
articles.abstract: People rely on their perception of a person’s trustworthiness to make important social decisions. Consequently, the misperception of trustworthiness may lead to undesirable outcomes. Trustworthiness perception has been widely studied with unnatural and static faces, however, people must also consider contextual information when making social inferences. In this study, we investigated the importance of context in trustworthiness judgments by having 124 observers continuously rate the perceived trustworthiness of a blurred-out (invisible) target character in 12 videos. We found that contextual and facial cues contribute similar amounts of information to trustworthiness judgments (~20% variance explained), however, observers can better infer others’ trustworthiness (t(80) = -3.40, p = 0.001) and agree more with each other (t(80) = -592.56, p < 0.001) when using contextual information than face and body information. Our findings highlight the importance of using contextual information when inferring the trustworthiness of others.

articles.title: The role of consequence in evaluating approximate claims as true or false
articles.authors: Isaac J Handley-Miner; Thomas Mazzuchi; Max Kleiman-Weiner; Liane Young
articles.abstract: Even when people know how accurate a claim of fact is, evaluating it as ‘true’ or ‘false’ is complicated given the imprecisions of natural language. How do people decide whether an approximate claim should be considered true or false? We test whether truth evaluations depend on the consequences of a claim’s approximation—i.e., whether the approximation caused something to happen that otherwise would not have happened. In Study 1 (N=880) and Study 2 (N=1130), participants were more likely to consider a claim false when its approximation was consequential. Study 3 (N=402), using a game with real monetary stakes, found that the strength of this effect depended on whether the consequences of a claim’s approximation were good or bad. These findings suggest that, even when people know exactly how well an approximate claim corresponds to the facts, truth evaluations depend on whether the claim’s approximation mattered in context.

articles.title: Psychological Coupling: The Necessary Science of Human-AI Interaction
articles.authors: Roberta Rocca; Winnie Street; Geoff Keeling; James Evans
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly showing the potential to generate outputs with impact ranging from emotional support to belief distortion. However, a critical gap in AI safety research persists: current frameworks fail to capture how virtuous and maladaptive dynamics actually emerge through turn-by-turn conversational exchange. Building on dynamical systems research on human social interaction, we reframe human-AI exchanges as dynamic relational systems and introduce the notion of ‘psychological coupling’ as a candidate, empirically measurable mechanism driving psychosocial impacts from human-AI interaction. Providing the empirical foundation necessary to mitigate relational risks and actively promote adaptive, prosocial outcomes, this paper  further outlines the promises and challenges of a necessary shift from static safety benchmarks to empirical studies and longitudinal, simulation-based evaluations that capture these unfolding dynamic trajectories.

articles.title: What Should Be Spaced? Variability Constrains Spacing Effects in Classroom Learning
articles.authors: Meng Cao; Joshua Ling; Philip I. Pavlik; Paulo F. Carvalho
articles.abstract: The present study examined whether the effectiveness of spaced practice in authentic K–12 classrooms depends on the variability of instructional materials. Data were collected from 52 classrooms, including 1,050 middle and high school students across multiple subjects. Results showed that spaced practice improved long-term retention when practice repeatedly targeted the same item, even when questions varied in format. In contrast, spacing benefits were attenuated when practice involved different items aligned with the same instructional standard. Spaced practice also produced a modest benefit for transfer to novel items regardless of variability. These findings suggest an important boundary condition of spacing effects: the benefits of spacing depend on the extent to which practice opportunities preserve overlap that supports retrieval of prior learning. More broadly, the results refine theoretical accounts of spacing by highlighting potentially distinct mechanisms underlying retention and transfer and provide practical guidance for implementing spaced practice in educational settings.

articles.title: Carpe diem or memento mori – The relationship between existential anxiety and temporal discounting
articles.authors: Dominika Zub; Wojciech Białaszek
articles.abstract:
This thesis investigates the relationship between existential anxiety and temporal discounting, which is the tendency to prefer smaller, immediate rewards over larger, delayed ones. The primary objective was to examine how inducing thoughts of one’s own mortality (mortality salience) influences impulsivity in financial decision-making. The theoretical framework is based on Terror Management Theory (TMT) and Paul Tillich's concepts of existential anxiety.
Method: An online study was conducted with 224 participants aged 18 to 72. Participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups: experimental (mortality salience), comparison (thoughts of physical pain at the dentist), and control (current emotional state). Tools used included the 5-trial adjusting delay task to measure the discounting rate (parameter k), the Existential Anxiety Questionnaire (EAQ), and the Purpose in Life Test (PIL).
Results: Statistical analyses did not support the primary research hypotheses. No significant differences in discounting rates were found between the mortality salience group and the control groups. Furthermore, existential anxiety levels and declared religiousness did not significantly correlate with the discounting rate of future gains. However, it was noted that higher levels of mindfulness (attention) during the study were associated with lower impulsivity (slower discounting). A strong negative correlation between existential anxiety and purpose in life was also observed.
Conclusions: The lack of significant manipulation effects may result from the socio-economic diversity of the sample or the situational context, aligning with Life History Theory (LHT). The findings suggest that the link between mortality awareness and economic decisions is complex and may be moderated by an individual's resources and the specific type of threat.

articles.title: The Alliance in Blended Care: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Laura Luisa Bielinski; Jessica Mehlis; Marlene Rosendahl; Thomas Berger; Johanna Boettcher; Christine Knaevelsrud; Carmen Schaeuffele
articles.abstract:
Background: The therapeutic alliance (TA) is a robust predictor of outcome in psychotherapy. Blended care (BC), combining face-to-face (F2F) with digital components, is gaining momentum in mental health care, yet the role of the TA remains poorly understood.
Objectives: This preregistered systematic review and meta-analysis examined (1) levels of TA across BC studies, (2) the association between TA and outcome in BC, and (3) differences in TA ratings between BC and control conditions.
Methods: The search identified 48 studies reporting TA in BC (N = 3210 participants who received BC). Multilevel meta-analyses estimated the alliance-outcome correlation and standardized mean differences in TA between BC and control groups, including F2F psychotherapy and treatment as usual. Moderator analyses examined patient-, therapist-, and treatment-level variables.
Results: Alliance ratings were high across BC groups (average percent of maximum possible score across measures rated by patients: 74.1 ± 12.0). The association between TA and outcome in BC was small to moderate (r = .24, 95% CI [.16, .31], p < .001). Patient-rated TA in BC did not differ significantly from patient-rated TA in control groups (g = 0.07, 95% CI [-0.07, 0.21], p = 0.339).
Conclusions: The combination of digital and F2F elements to treat mental health problems does not compromise the TA. Alliance remains a meaningful predictor of outcome in this context and alliance levels in BC are comparable to those in F2F controls. Findings highlight the need for BC-specific measures of the TA, and standardized reporting of BC characteristics for granular synthesis.

articles.title: Modality-Specific Consolidation Shapes Long-Term Retention in Statistical Learning
articles.authors: Anqi Hu; Kaitlyn Mary Ann Parks; Samantha Schulz; Stephanie Larmer; Laura Batterink; Ryan A Stevenson
articles.abstract: Individuals routinely learn patterns from sights and sounds, yet it remains unclear how memories of those patterns compare across senses over time. Statistical learning, the implicit detection and extraction of patterns from continuous input, has been documented across sensory modalities, but most work has focused on immediate learning in unisensory contexts. This study, using a within-subject design, directly compared 24-hour consolidation trajectories of auditory, visual, and audiovisual statistical learning. Twenty-six young adults completed three statistical learning tasks across auditory, visual, and audiovisual modalities. In each task, participants were exposed to a continuous stream of stimuli with embedded triplet patterns. Learning of these patterns was tested immediately after exposure and again 24 hours later. Across immediate and delayed tests, multisensory exposure yielded higher accuracy than unisensory exposure, demonstrating a robust multisensory-over-unisensory advantage. In contrast, only auditory statistical learning showed overnight gains, whereas visual and multisensory learning remained stable, suggesting a consolidation advantage for auditory input. Cross-task correlations, both at immediate test and for consolidation magnitudes, were weak, suggesting substantial modality-specific contributions to statistical learning. These findings provide the first evidence that multisensory input boosts long-term retention of learned patterns, and the consolidation of these patterns operates in a modality-specific manner.

articles.title: Kawaii (Cute) Feelings and Gender in Japanese Young Adults: Relations with Communion and Gender-Role Attitudes
articles.authors: Erika KANAYAMA
articles.abstract: “Kawaii (cute) feelings” are positive affective responses with culturally specific meanings in Japanese and have often been linked to femininity. This study examined the relations of kawaii feelings with communion (stereotypically associated with femininity) and gender-role attitudes. An online questionnaire survey was conducted among 330 Japanese university students (124 men and 206 women; M = 19.76 years, SD = 1.81). Kawaii feelings were assessed across four targets: infants, baby animals, characters, and objects. Agency, a personality stereotypically associated with masculinity, was also assessed for comparison with communion. Multivariate analysis of covariance revealed that positive communion, negative communion, gender-role attitudes, and gender were associated with kawaii feeling. Higher levels of both positive and negative communion were associated with stronger kawaii feeling toward infants and objects, more egalitarian gender-role attitudes were associated with stronger kawaii feeling toward characters, and women reported stronger kawaii feeling toward baby animals and objects than men. Agency was not significantly associated with kawaii feeling. These results highlight the importance of considering culturally specific emotions to understand how gender norms are maintained and transformed in contemporary Japanese society.

articles.title: Testing the Dual Representation Account of Memory for Trauma
articles.authors: Tommy Mcconnell; Hannah Bernhard; Emily A Holmes; Richard Henson; Deborah Talmi
articles.abstract: The finding that a visuospatial intervention can decrease involuntary memories following a laboratory analogue of trauma, without altering voluntary memory, is a crucial source of support for the Dual Representation Account (DRA) of intrusive memories. In two pre-registered experiments using Bayesian sequential designs, we tested predictions derived from the DRA. In Experiment 1, we first aimed to replicate the functional dissociation between voluntary and involuntary memory after a visuospatial interference intervention, while matching retrieval conditions more closely than in previous research. Second, we tested the prediction that the effect of the intervention would be greater in an analogue trauma condition than a neutral control condition. Third, we tested the prediction that voluntary memory would be decreased in the trauma condition relative to the neutral condition. Finally, we hypothesized that measures of voluntary and involuntary memory would not be correlated across participants. Unexpectedly, the results indicated that the visuospatial intervention did not decrease involuntary memories and that measures of involuntary and voluntary memory were positively correlated. In Experiment 2, we focused specifically on replicating the effect of the visuospatial intervention on involuntary memories of a trauma film. We did not replicate this effect: results provided substantial Bayesian evidence against an effect of the intervention.  Overall, our findings provide evidence against the DRA as a viable theoretical account of intrusive memories, at least in the context of a laboratory analogue of trauma.

articles.title: Pluralism within limits: How to make the idea of multiple NCC kinds useful
articles.authors: Lukas Kob; Tomas Marvan
articles.abstract: Recently, several authors have argued that the current impasse in consciousness science supports a pluralistic interpretation: the view that seemingly contradictory findings may both be valid because consciousness can be brought about in different ways, each sufficient to generate conscious experience. We analyze different versions of pluralism to determine under what conditions it offers a viable interpretation of consciousness science. We argue that viable pluralism requires a two-factor framework distinguishing general consciousness factors (mechanisms rendering contents conscious) from content encoding itself. Moreover, NCC kinds must be finite in number and systematically ordered to enable a unified scientific model. While pluralism enables testable hypotheses, significant ontological tensions arise: how can consciousness constitute a unified natural kind if realized by fundamentally distinct NCC kinds? Drawing on philosophy of science, we propose an alternative interpretation in terms of scientific rather than natural kinds. Our goal is not to advocate for pluralism, but rather, to critically examine its viability and implications. Based on this, we urge the field to engage more explicitly with the foundational assumptions that shape consciousness research.

articles.title: Pupil-linked arousal: more than noradrenaline
articles.authors: Simon van Gaal; Lola Beerendonk; Tobias H. Donner; Jan Willem de Gee
articles.abstract: The literature on pupil-linked arousal shows a pervasive bias: it often attributes findings to the noradrenergic locus coeruleus while overlooking other neuromodulatory systems.
