Here, we quantify the extent of this bias and emphasize that correcting it is essential for refining theoretical models and improving interpretability in this rapidly evolving field.

articles.title: The effect of semantic similarity on re-learning of vocabulary in activation and inhibition deficit in aphasia
articles.authors: Nazbanou Nozari; Michael Crawley Freund; Brenda Rapp
articles.abstract: Semantic similarity between known items (e.g., cat/dog) can interfere with a speaker’s ability to name them. Recent studies have shown that this interference extends to the acquisition of new vocabulary in neurotypical adults. However, the influence of such similarity on long-term retention of relearned vocabulary in individuals with aphasia remains unknown. This study examines this issue in two individuals with lexical retrieval deficits, who nevertheless show distinct profiles: one has difficulty activating lexical items (activation deficit), the other inhibiting lexical competitors (inhibition deficit). Participants were trained for six weeks using blocks of semantically related, unrelated, and mixed items. Learning was assessed during training, immediately after training, one week after each training session during the training period, and finally, two, six, and sixteen weeks after the last training session. We found a detrimental effect of semantic similarity on long-term retention in the inhibition, but not the activation, deficit. This dissociation shows that semantic similarity interacts with processes of activation and selection in a manner predicted by theories of incremental learning in language production. With regard to clinical implications, we found that, similar to neurotypical learners, semantic similarity among items in a training set can hurt the long-term retention, but that this effect can be ameliorated by spreading the related items across different training blocks within a session.

articles.title: The Adoption of Open Science Practices in Communication Research: Taking Stock of the Field in 2025
articles.authors: Jasmijn Kruijt; Chaoyi Gao; Xiaojing Ke; Sofie Pribylova; Wenyuan Yang; Bert N. Bakker
articles.abstract: Five years after Dienlin et al.’s (2021) agenda-setting article explicitly called for communication research to adopt open science practices, this study systematically assesses the current state within the field. Through manual content analysis of articles published in 2025 across 20 leading communication journals, we evaluated the adoption of six Transparency and Openness Promotion (TOP) Research Practices provided by the Center for Open Science, namely study registration, study protocol, analysis plan, materials transparency, data transparency, and analytic code transparency. The results show limited adoption of TOP research practices in communication research. Sharing of research materials is done more frequently than other practices, yet its extent of transparency remains limited. Quantitative studies achieve higher transparency scores than qualitative studies. Critically, justifications for non-sharing of materials, data, or analytic code, as well as explicit reporting of deviations from preregistrations, are mostly absent, despite their importance for transparency and openness. We argue that voluntary adoption may be insufficient and discuss whether stricter enforcement mechanisms are needed to establish open science as a normative standard. By mapping the current implementation of open science practices, this study contributes to critical reflection on the reproducibility and credibility of communication research and informs the development of infrastructures to support greater transparency.

articles.title: Goals, Predictions, Valence: A Model Comparison of Agency Emergence
articles.authors: Jonas Richter; Marcel Raphael Schreiner
articles.abstract: The sense of agency (SoA), our feeling of control over actions and their environmental effects, has been explained through different theoretical models. This study compares three: the comparator model, proposing that SoA emerges from matching motor predictions to perceptual feedback, the ideomotor model, proposing that SoA emerges from monitoring goal achievement, and a self-serving bias model, proposing that SoA reflects self-attribution of positive and external attribution of negative outcomes. Participants performed a target-hitting task with manipulated visual feedback of positive or negative valence and rated their SoA on a subset of trials. The three models predict different SoA patterns based on motor activity appropriateness, goal achievement, and outcome valence. The ideomotor model best fit the empirical data, outperforming both alternatives, though outcome valence showed an additional influence on SoA. These findings question the role of motor-based predictions in SoA emergence and highlight goal achievement as a key determinant of SoA.

articles.title: Learning words in diverse narrative contexts disrupts subsequent recall of their meanings
articles.authors: Rachael Catherine Hulme; Kate Nation; Jennifer M Rodd
articles.abstract: Recent research suggests that encountering words in varied narrative contexts may disrupt the earliest stage of learning (Hulme et al., 2023). The current experiments explore (i) how this diversity disadvantage changes over learning, and (ii) whether diversity primarily impacts encoding or subsequent recall. Adults (Experiment 1: N = 105; Experiment 2: N = 111) encountered novel words with invented meanings within narrative paragraphs across three days. Words were presented within one coherent narrative (low diversity) or across nine different narratives (high diversity). We replicated Hulme et al.'s (2023) results for word-form learning, finding moderate/strong evidence for null effects of diversity on word-form recall and recognition (Experiment 1), and speeded word-form recognition accuracy (Experiment 2), and only anecdotal evidence of a diversity benefit on response times. For word-meaning learning, Experiment 1 replicated the diversity disadvantage on word-meaning recall reported by Hulme et al. (2023; extreme evidence), but in Experiment 2 there was moderate evidence for a null effect on semantic feature recognition. Findings indicate that while narrative diversity does not strongly influence participants’ ability to encode or retain semantic features for new words, words learned in low-diversity conditions initially build more coherent contextually-bound representations that facilitate recall of semantic features.

articles.title: Executive functions in people with Williams syndrome: a scoping review
articles.authors: Raquel Baeza; Paula Gómez Sánchez-Vizcaíno; María Sotillo Mendez; José M. López-Frutos
articles.abstract:
Introduction. Williams syndrome (WS) is a genetic neurodevelopmental disorder associated with mild to moderate intellectual disability and a distinctive cognitive profile characterized by relative strengths in language and visuospatial difficulties. People with WS are characterized by an atypical pattern of hypersociability, manifested as marked social disinhibition. While their neurobehavioral profile strongly suggests significant impairments in executive functions (EF), empirical findings remain heterogeneous. Therefore, this scoping review aimed to synthesize and map the existing literature on EF in people with WS.
Methods. Following the PRISMA-ScR framework, ProQuest, Scopus, Web of Science, EBSCOhost databases were searched. Inclusion criteria required empirical data published between 2000 and 2025 targeting EF assessments or interventions in genetically confirmed WS cohorts as a distinct group. A total of 85 documents were included.
Results. Included studies predominantly featured cross-sectional comparative designs, often with small sample sizes. Findings revealed impairments across all executive domains examined. EF was significantly associated with emotional, behavioral, social, and adaptive outcomes, suggesting a central role in everyday functioning. However, considerable methodological heterogeneity was identified across studies.
Conclusions. People with WS exhibit severe and generalized executive dysfunction, which plays a critical role in their emotional, behavioral and social adaptation. However, the scarcity of longitudinal data limits the understanding of executive developmental trajectories. Furthermore, limited intervention evidence highlights the need for future studies to clarify which specific executive components are most amenable to improvement.

articles.title: Digital shadows in mental health map how LLMs simulate depression, anxiety, and stress through language and psychometrics
articles.authors: Emma Franchino; Rodolfo Rizzi; Edoardo Sebastiano De Duro; Ali Aghazadeh Ardebili; Massimo Stella
articles.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used for mental-health support, yet few datasets characterise how prompting conditions shape LLMs' psychological and linguistic outputs. To fill this gap, we introduce Mental Health Digital Shadows (MHDS), a benchmark of 75,000 outputs from 15 LLMs in conversation, under human-shadow or LLM-assistant conditions. MHDS includes outputs generated by several model families (Grok, DeepSeek, Mistral, and Qwen) or providers (Microsoft, IBM, and OpenAI). Human shadows are conditioned on sociodemographic and personality traits alongside assessments of their depression, anxiety, and stress levels, based on the Depression Anxiety and Stress Scale-21 (DASS-21). MHDS incorporates DASS-21 psychometric scores and three types of natural language texts: (i) an item-by-item explanation of shadows' DASS-21 numerical scores; (ii) a list of words expressing feelings; and (iii) six topic-oriented open-ended replies addressing mental health topics (e.g., stigma, support, and AI psychologists). Validating MHDS across its two conditions identifies a tendency for human shadows to alter their linguistic replies and psychometric scores according to prompting conditions, a pattern reminiscent of human behaviour and absent in LLM assistants. Further validation against human data finds that LLMs can produce distress scores resembling the distribution of human populations. Hence, MHDS offers a psychometrically grounded resource for studying how LLMs can debate mental health conditions, with applications in NLP, AI safety, bias detection, and well-being AI support tools.

articles.title: The Construct Validity of Automated Written Interview Competency Assessments
articles.authors: Vincent Berthet
articles.abstract: We report evidence on the construct validity of automated written interview competency assessments (AWI-CAs). Participants (N = 117) provided written responses to 12 open-ended interview questions designed to assess four behavioral competencies (Collaboration, Communication, Leadership, Critical Thinking) along with a neutral question. Responses were scored by a large language model (LLM) on an 11-point scale. Scores were substantially intercorrelated, including correlations with the neutral question, suggesting substantial common method variance. Accordingly, we partialled out the neutral-question score from each behavioral question. Principal axis factor analysis with varimax rotation yielded a three-factor solution: Communication and Leadership loaded on a common factor, whereas Collaboration and Critical Thinking formed distinct factors. In addition, the Communication/Leadership factor was primarily associated with Open-Mindedness, the Collaboration factor with Conscientiousness, and the Critical Thinking factor with verbal reasoning. Overall, these findings support the construct validity of automated behavioral interviews beyond a general response tendency.

articles.title: Do action video gamers search better than non-gamers?
articles.authors: Zoe (Jing) Xu; Josef Spjut; Ben Boudaoud; Simona Buetti; Alejandro Lleras; Ruth Rosenholtz
articles.abstract: Studies have shown that action video gamers are better at visual search than non-gamers, but less is known about the specific ways in which gamers excel. We conducted an extensive set of experiments to examine whether and how gamers and non-gamers differ in their search performance. We used both simple, efficient feature search tasks and difficult, inefficient configuration search tasks, including search for game-style figures. In four of the experiments, we adopted a psychophysical staircase procedure to determine the minimal display duration or target-distractor color difference required for participants to find the target. In the other eight experiments, we measured response time and search efficiency for efficient as well as inefficient search tasks. This study is the first to use both staircase procedures and response time measures to give a comprehensive examination of group differences between gamers and non-gamers in various aspects of visual search behavior. We found only minimal evidence for superior search skills among gamers. Only in one of four cases did we find a gamer benefit in their perceptual threshold - gamers’ color discrimination exhibit a higher robustness against search difficulty. In one of four efficient searches did gamers respond marginally faster or switch faster. On the other hand, in one of four inefficient searches gamers actually suffered more from increased set size, suggesting a less efficient search than non-gamers. These findings call for more cautious consideration of the advantage of gamers in visual search.

articles.title: Predictions and outcomes independently shape the subjective experience of regret
articles.authors: Krisztina Jedlovszky; Daniel Yon
articles.abstract: Humans have the capacity to feel a subjective sense of regret – a rather sophisticated emotion that involves counterfactual simulation of the options not pursued when we act and decide. Pathological levels of regret are also a hallmark of multiple psychiatric conditions. However, little is understood about how subjective feelings of regret connect to the learning and inference processes we use to make decisions – and how alterations in these processes may explain atypical regret experiences. Here, we modified a reward learning task to include sporadic probes for post-choice regret. Computational modelling revealed that subjective feelings of regret were connected to reward outcomes, but importantly, were also independently tied to evolving predictions about action values. This perspective explains how the subjective experience of regret could be generated from the building blocks of more basic representations used in learning and inference.

articles.title: The role of interoception in dissociation etiology: a network analysis of dissociation, trauma, sleep, and interoception
articles.authors: Marta Łukowska; Ari Nowacki; Weronika Różycka; Eva Schäflein
articles.abstract:
A transtheoretical framework for dissociation acknowledges emotional regulation. However, interoception—closely linked to emotional and autonomic regulation—remains underexplored, despite growing evidence connects it to dissociation and its correlates. This study applied a network analysis to examine multidirectional associations among dissociation, trauma exposure, post-traumatic symptoms, sleep quality, and interoception. A cross-sectional design was employed in a community sample of 574 adults (18–60 years) from Upper Silesia, Poland. Participants completed validated self-report measures of dissociative experiences (Dissociative Experiences Scale; Somatoform Dissociation Questionnaire), interoception (Multidimensional Assessment of Interoceptive Awareness; Body Perception Questionnaire), sleep quality (Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index), trauma exposure (Life Events Checklist for DSM-5), post-traumatic stress symptoms (International Trauma Questionnaire), and demographic and health variables.
The network was estimated using a graphical Gaussian model with the graphical LASSO algorithm. Absorption showed the highest strength and expected influence, affective dysregulation the greatest closeness and betweenness, while autonomic reactivity and emotional awareness also emerged as highly central nodes. Contrary to theoretical expectations derived from post-traumatic and sociocognitive models, neither trauma exposure nor sleep quality occupied central positions in the network.
These findings suggest that dissociation may be more tightly embedded in interoceptive and autonomic regulatory processes than in trauma exposure per se. Atypical interoception may represent a transdiagnostic mechanism linking posttraumatic and sociocognitive accounts of dissociation, while aligning with attachment-based perspectives emphasizing disrupted bodily and affective co-regulation. These results provide a rationale for incorporating interoceptive-focused interventions in the treatment of dissociative symptoms.

articles.title: Best practices and pitfalls in multivariate pattern analysis of event-related potentials: A systematic review of preprocessing and analytical configurations
articles.authors: Ying Xin; Limin Hou; Xinran Wang; Fengyu Cong; He Weiqi; Wenbo Luo; Guanghui Zhang
articles.abstract: Multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA, decoding) has been increasingly used in event-related potential (ERP) research. This growing use reflects several advantages of decoding approaches, such as their ability to exploit high-dimensional spatiotemporal ERP features, detect subtle and distributed neural differences between conditions, avoid subjective electrode selection, and provide time-resolved insights into cognitive processing. However, decoding performance is highly sensitive to preprocessing choices and analysis settings, many of which are directly inherited from conventional ERP practices despite not always being optimal for decoding analyses. Evidence indicates that preprocessing steps can differentially affect univariate ERP measures and decoding outcomes. Similarly, decoding-specific analysis parameters, including classifier selection, trial construction strategies, overfitting control methods, cross-validation schemes, and evaluation metrics, can substantially shape decoding performance and its interpretation. Importantly, commonly used metrics such as accuracy and AUC, while practical and widely implemented in popular toolboxes, may introduce bias under conditions such as class imbalance, which are prevalent in ERP paradigms. Here, we present a systematic review of ERP decoding studies published between January 2021 and December 2025, providing a quantitative overview of prevailing preprocessing pipelines and decoding configurations. We integrate these results with empirical and methodological evidence to discuss the mechanisms, benefits, and potential risks associated with common analytical choices, highlighting key divergences between decoding-oriented and conventional ERP methodologies. Finally, we identify recurrent methodological pitfalls and offer practical recommendations aimed at improving transparency, reproducibility, and methodological validity in future ERP decoding research.

articles.title: Trauma Work as Emotional Labour: A Process Model Linking Trauma Exposure to Worker Outcomes
articles.authors: Andrea Fischbach
articles.abstract: Trauma work — repeated task-related confrontation with traumatic events or their aversive details as a work-role requirement — is an occupational hazard, yet work-specific mechanisms remain under-specified in clinical PTSD models. This conceptual paper proposes theoretical relationships among trauma exposure, emotion regulation, resource dynamics, and emotional labour climate. The model differentiates extraordinary trauma events at work (DSM-5 A1/A2/A3) from ordinary task-related exposure (DSM-5 A4) and introduces trauma dose (type, duration, density, intensity) as a distinct stressor. Four interdependent mechanisms link exposure to trauma sequelae (PTSD, complex PTSD, burnout, empathic distress): (1) autonomous stress responses; (2) controlled emotion regulation and self-regulation; (3) cumulative resource dynamics with loss and gain spirals; and (4) emotional labour climate as the cause of the cause — the human resource practices that build resources and buffer exposure. Four testable propositions follow, with organisational leverage points for protecting health and enabling positive working experiences in trauma-exposed occupations.

articles.title: Thinking clearly about sampling and representation with the Total Survey Error Framework
articles.authors: Taym Alsalti; Julia M. Rohrer; Ruben C. Arslan
articles.abstract: Collecting a sample that represents the population of interest well constitutes a challenge across the social and behavioural sciences. Psychology in particular frequently relies on convenience samples—most notably students and, increasingly, online participants—with a tendency to either (implicitly) assume representativeness without substantive justification, or to acknowledge a lack of it only in passing. In contrast, researchers rarely engage with the actual implications for their inferences, which undermines the generalisability of psychological findings. Critically, representativeness must be defined with respect to variables relevant to the target of inference, rather than superficial demographic diversity. Here we present the Total Survey Error (TSE) framework as a methodological tool that systematically addresses the multifaceted sources of error—particularly those related to representation—that emerge throughout the research cycle. Although TSE originated in survey research, its principles are broadly applicable to any psychological study seeking inference from sample to population. We offer practical strategies for identifying, preventing, and mitigating representation errors to improve the credibility and generalizability of psychological research.

articles.title: State of Social Cognition Research: Challenges, Future Directions, and Efforts Toward Consensus Building
articles.authors: Niklas Bürgi; Anabela Cantiani; Beatriz Catoira; Mado Gautier; Ilenia Gori; Annika Konrad; Lara Maliske; Annabel Nijhof; Arthur Pabst; Ekaterina Pronizius
articles.abstract: Social cognition research has become a key area of cognitive science, producing a wealth of theories, methods, and empirical findings. However, the field faces significant challenges that constrain further progress and broader impact. These challenges include a lack of conceptual clarity as well as methodological and psychometric issues. Conceptually, there is substantial variation in how social cognition is defined, subdivided, and labeled, alongside a limited understanding of its basic structure. Methodologically, existing assessment tools often show shortcomings, including inadequate assessment and reporting of psychometric properties. Furthermore, sufficiently interactive paradigms are still scarce, and the clarification and experimental control of participants’ strategy use and underlying cognitive processes involved in task performance remain insufficient. As a group of early-career researchers and clinicians with a broad variety of backgrounds and research foci reflecting the thematic diversity of the field, we aimed to develop an overview of these issues and propose ways forward. We argue that progress requires both humility and courage: acknowledging our field’s theoretical fragmentation and methodological limitations, and embracing complexity through multimethod, interactive, and computational approaches. We further call for consensus-building initiatives, open science practices, and large-scale collaborative research to address these challenges and further advance our understanding of social cognition.

articles.title: What Do We Know About the Effects of Artificial Intelligence in Education? A Second-Order Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Valentin Emslander; Marlit Annalena Lindner; Alexander Eitel; Enkelejda Kasneci; Lisa Bardach
articles.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming increasingly integrated into education. However, fundamental questions about its effects remain unresolved, with existing meta-analyses differing substantially in reported effect sizes, moderators that amplify or attenuate these effects, and methodological quality. To synthesize the fragmented evidence base and examine sources of these variations, we conducted a preregistered second-order meta-analysis aggregating 45 meta-analyses (129 effect sizes) based on approximately 197,000 learners from prekindergarten through university. Three-level second-order meta-analyses showed that AI applications in education had a medium effect across educational outcomes. Effects were larger in more recent meta-analyses, in meta-analyses including tertiary-education students, and for general academic outcomes than for STEM performance outcomes. We did not find significant differences between AI applications. Methodological quality of meta-analyses did not explain variance in effects, but our assessment identified systematic quality deficits (e.g., regarding reproducibility and open science). Insufficient statistical power was common among the included meta-analyses. Overall, these findings advance a more differentiated understanding of the current state of research on AI in education, highlighting both the potential of AI and the limitations of the evidence base.

articles.title: Putting Out Statistical Fires: Assessing Model Fit Using DHARMa Randomized Quantile Residuals
articles.authors: Shawn Hemelstrand
articles.abstract: Regression models and statistical tests abound in the social sciences. But are any of these models actually correct? Evidence shows that most models in psychology, and likely other social sciences as well, are not assessed for mis-specification, which damages the reliability of psychological research. This tutorial paper aims to bridge this gap. The paper primarily showcases a generalized approach to model fit using randomized quantile residuals from the DHARMa package in R. The paper is also accompanied by a website which provides a variety of examples with simulated and real-world social science data (https://dharma-for-social-science.netlify.app/). The purpose of this paper and website is to educate social scientists, particularly psychologists, on how to assess model fit across a wide spectrum of models, which may improve the reliability of findings in the field.

articles.title: Pro Environmental Attitudes, Emotions, and Donations After Playing an  Ecogame - The Role of Post Gameplay Reflection
articles.authors: Moritz Edlinger; Stefan E. Huber; Marie Lisa Kogler; Manuel Ninaus
articles.abstract:
Background 
Video games are increasingly recognised as promising tools for environmental education 
because of their potential to positively influence environmental attitudes and behaviours at 
scale. However, while effects of persuasive games appear consistent, effect sizes are generally 
small. In the specific context of ecogames, which are game about environmental issues, more 
experimental investigations of their effects are needed. 
Objectives 
This study investigated whether structured post-gameplay reflection amplifies the effects of the 
persuasive ecogame Gibbon: Beyond the Trees on pro-environmental attitudes and pro
environmental behaviour. Besides, it sought to strengthen the empirical evidence on ecogames 
by using an experimental design, full-game playthroughs, and including an outcome measure 
that extends beyond self-reported attitudes. 
Methods 
After completing a full playthrough, participants were randomly assigned to one of three 
conditions that differed in the level of structured post-gameplay reflection (none, moderate, or 
substantial). The study used a pre-test/post-test design with an optional one-week follow-up, to 
examine changes in palm-oil-related attitudes, wildlife value orientations, generalized 
environmental attitudes, and affective reactions to gameplay. Pro-environmental behaviour was 
assessed through voluntary donations to environmental NGOs. 
Results and Conclusions 
On average, this study observed improvements in pro-environmental attitudes post-gameplay, 
alongside affective changes aligned with the game’s narrative. Further, participants showed 
substantial behavioural responses, donating nearly half of their compensation (1.951,80€ in 
total) to environmental NGOs. Post-gameplay reflection did not significantly moderate 
attitudinal changes or donation amounts, suggesting that well-received commercial ecogames 
with strong alignment between narrative content and targeted outcomes can meaningfully 
support environmental education even without additional reflection activities.

articles.title: From Sound-Colour Association to Sensory-Affective Worlds:  A Qualitative Case Study of Chromaesthesia in Embodied Music Listening
articles.authors: Eiko Matsuda; Tatsuya Daikoku
articles.abstract: Synesthesia has typically been studied through temporal consistency in inducer--concurrent pairings, privileging stable one-to-one mappings between graphemes/sounds and colours. This qualitative case study examines chromaesthesia (sound-colour synesthesia) as a multisensory, affective, and embodied experience that unfolds during music listening. A semi-structured interview was conducted with an adult with chromaesthesia while listening to Debussy's Clair de lune; the interviewer also had chromaesthesia, and her self-reports during the dialogue were included reflexively in the analysis. The transcript was analyzed using Steps for Coding and Theorization (SCAT). The analysis showed that music-induced concurrents were not limited to colours or shapes, but involved movement, space, temperature, smell, taste, bodily sensation, and affect. These sensory-affective images were organized as coherent motifs and emerged as an integrated sensory world in which the body could be felt as located or oriented. This world was not a reproduction of real scenes; real-world terms served as resources for articulating an idealized sensory world. These findings suggest that chromaesthesia cannot be reduced to sound-colour association, but may render bodily, affective, and abstract musical structures as concrete sensory--affective worlds shaped by memory, learning, and dialogue.

articles.title: Reading Anxiety in Children and Adolescents and Its Negative Association With Word Reading Performance
articles.authors: Silvia Siu Yin Clement-Lam; Deborah Anne Schneider-Richardson; Allison Kalva; Celinene M. Lay; Brianna Paquette; Caroline G. Richter; Devin M. Kearns; Fumiko Hoeft
articles.abstract: Over the past decade, research on reading development and reading disorders has expanded beyond its traditional focus on cognitive-linguistic factors to give increasing attention to socioemotional functioning. The present study leveraged two existing datasets (112 elementary school children aged 7 to 10 years and 110 early-and-mid adolescents aged 11 to 15 years) to examine various dimensions of reading anxiety (RA), including cognitive symptoms, physiological symptoms, and behavioral avoidance, as well as the overall RA effects. Our findings revealed that (1) RA was prevalent among both children and adolescents, although correlations among RA dimensions were stronger in adolescents than in children, (2) higher RA was moderately associated with lower word reading performance in both age groups, (3) no significant differences in RA were observed between children and adolescents or between girls and boys, and effects were not moderated by reading performance, and, lastly, (4) RA was negatively associated with reading performance across levels of reading ability, and this association was not stronger among readers at the lower end of the distribution. These findings reinforce the observed moderate negative association between RA and reading performance in prior research and suggest that RA may affect individuals across reading levels and developmental periods.

articles.title: Modeling Decision Bias in dockerHDDM: A Systematic Comparison and Evaluation
articles.authors: Siyu Wu; Wanke Pan; Hu Chuan-Peng
articles.abstract: Decision bias refers to a systematic tendency to approach or avoid a particular option in two-alternative forced-choice (2AFC) tasks. With the advent of easy-to-use tools, diffusion-decision model (DDM) is widely used to distinguish two types of latent cognitive processes, bias in starting point or in efficiency, providing a powerful tool for understanding a variety of psychological phenomena across different fields. However, correctly estimating these two types of bias requires specifying the model to match the type of bias being investigated. So far, no study has systematically evaluated the different model configurations for modelling decision bias in HDDM, the most widely used python package for DDM. Here we evaluated nine model specifications using the three main modeling modules in HDDM across both simulated and empirical data. Our results show that the default function, which uses accuracy coding of data without flipping parameters, fails to recover either the starting point bias or the drift rate bias. Only model specifications that directly estimate the drift bias parameter, drift criterion (dc), or apply appropriate parameter flipping recover true bias parameters. By applying optimal specification to empirical data, we uncovered a novel psychological effect that went undetected in the original study. We provide concrete, evidence based recommendations for model specification and fully reproducible code. This work ensures accurate parameter estimation and the correct interpretation of parameter when modeling decision biases.

articles.title: Successful Modal Reasoning Depends On WHAT Went WHERE
articles.authors: Peter Mazalik; Justin Halberda
articles.abstract: Modal reasoning requires quantifying over possibilities (e.g., categorizing events as necessary, possible, and impossible). This suite of abilities matures during the preschool years, but toddlers perform better in some modal reasoning tasks than others. We propose that these mixed findings reflect a cognitive distinction: toddlers perform worse when reasoning about ambiguous locations of objects (WHERE) than when reasoning about ambiguous kinds of objects (WHAT). In Experiments 1 and 2, we directly compared these forms of ambiguity using matched tasks. Consistent with the hypothesis, toddlers performed substantially better when reasoning about ambiguous kinds than ambiguous locations. Experiment 3 revealed a similar asymmetry in language comprehension, with preschool children showing better comprehension of modal terms such as “can” and “have to” in a kind-based task than in a location task. Thus, modal reasoning emerges earlier than previously suggested, and diversity in results reflects the contributions of distinct cognitive systems.

articles.title: What’s Brown and Sticky? Peering Into the Ineluctable Comedic Mystery of Dad Humor with a Handful of Machine Learning Models, Hundreds of Humans, and Tens of Thousands of Dad Jokes
articles.authors: Paul Joseph Silvia; Meriel I. Burnett
articles.abstract: A stick, of course. This masterpiece of human creativity is an example of a dad joke, a clean, good-natured joke founded on puns, literalism, and wordplay. After unpacking the structures, contents, and comedic devices that distinguish dad jokes from related genres, the present research explored both the features of dad jokes that make them funny and the kinds of people who find them funny. Study 1 coded over 32,000 jokes scraped from the Reddit r/dadjokes community. Ridge regression models found that a joke’s upvotes and comments were modestly predictable from its text and topic features (test sample R2 = 4.02% and 4.42%). After curating a hypercube subsample of 501 dad jokes, Study 2 found that both text features (e.g., concreteness, semantic distance, question framing) and topic features (e.g., moms, pregnancy, and numbers) predicted the funniness ratings made by adults (n = 621) much more effectively (cross-validated R2 = 28.2%). Study 3 found that individual differences in people’s funniness ratings were associated with cultural conventionality (e.g., parenthood, more education, affluence, religiosity, and conservatism) and social boldness (e.g., need for uniqueness, low honesty-humility, and boldness; cross-validated R2 = 32.4%), and that dads found the dad jokes funnier. Seemingly simple wordplay humor illuminates the psychology of verbal creativity and extends the psychology of aesthetics into art encounters that provoke feelings of amusement, playfulness, and absurdity. The coded dad jokes are freely available for researchers interested in studying humor or hassling their teenagers.

articles.title: Faith and the Other. Revisiting the Transcendence of the Other in the Psychology of Religion
articles.authors: Heinz Streib
articles.abstract: This article is an exercise in revisiting the concept of ‘faith’ in the psychology of religion. It addresses questions such as: What is faith? Is faith different from religion? In what ways? What does it mean defining faith as “ultimate concern”—as some colleagues already do? Answers are inspired by the philosophies of Paul Tillich and Emmanuel Levinas. An examination of Tillich’s philosophy of religion reveals that “ultimate concern” is a highly critical concept that, exactly by pointing to the Ultimate, entails a specific, radically semiotic understanding of transcendence. From reading Levinas, we conclude that ethics is grounded in the primacy of the Other, the transcendence one encounters in the face of the Other, and in the diachrony of the Good. Taken together, the insights from Tillich and Levinas suggest conceptualizing ‘faith’ as “ultimate concern” by which we are “grasped and turned to the infinite” (Tillich) and respond to the experience of transcendence that speaks to us in the face of the Other (Levinas). This confronts psychology with the challenge of operationalizing such experiences of transcendence of the Other. While the model of faith styles, which is based on Fowler’s theory of faith development, can be regarded as a beginning, advancements in future research are necessary.

articles.title: Multilayered Trauma and Identity Integration Framework (MTIIF): A Clinical Decision-Making Architecture for Complex Trauma and Forced Migration Distress
articles.authors: Valentyna Petrenko
articles.abstract:
MTIIF is an evidence-informed, field-grounded clinical framework that organizes trauma-focused, schema-based, cognitive-behavioral, and relational interventions for working with complex trauma, identity disruption, and forced migration-related distress.

The Multilayered Trauma and Identity Integration Framework (MTIIF) conceptualizes psychological distress as a dynamic interaction across five layers: neurobiological, existential, identity-related, social-adaptive, and relational. The framework organizes evidence-based approaches — EMDR, schema therapy, CBT, narrative and relational work — through a structured clinical decision-making logic.

The clinical contribution of MTIIF consists of four signature concepts: Layer Burden Profile (LBP), Threshold-Governed Progression, Relational Entry Point (REP), and Agency Restoration Index (ARI). Together they address four practical questions: where is the current entry point, when to transition to the next phase, how to structure group format, and how to evaluate recovery.

The framework emphasizes that post-migration conditions — legal uncertainty, housing instability, social isolation — may substantially maintain distress between therapeutic sessions (elaborated in Sections 1.2 and 4). Preliminary practice-based observations suggest potential improvement in symptoms and agency restoration. A formal pilot study is under development to verify these observations.

Claims regarding outcomes are based on practice-based observations, not controlled study data. Preliminary data indicate the model's potential — not demonstrated efficacy.

articles.title: Iatrogenic Harm, Ethics, and Epistemic Justice: A response to Swan et al. (2026)
articles.authors: Laurence Cobbaert
articles.abstract: Swan et al. [1] highlight challenges in multidisciplinary care for borderline personality disorder (BPD), including fragmentation and relational strain. This response argues that their analysis is limited by selective engagement with evidence, exclusion of lived experience, and insufficient critique of the diagnostic construct. Evidence indicates that commonly endorsed treatments produce harm, while lived experience research highlights epistemic injustice and identity-related harm. Furthermore, harms associated with BPD may be inherent to the construct itself, not solely consequences of stigma or poor care. These issues have persisted for decades, suggesting structural rather than incidental problems requiring critical re-evaluation of the diagnostic construct.

articles.title: The Moralization of Artificial Intelligence
articles.authors: Victoria Oldemburgo de Mello; Éloïse Côté; Reem Ayad; Yoel Inbar; Jason Plaks; Michael Inzlicht
articles.abstract: Resistance to artificial intelligence (AI) is widespread and persists even when AI is shown to be instrumentally advantageous. What explains this persistent aversion? Across four studies, we investigate whether moral reactions to AI—beliefs rooted in deeply held notions of right and wrong—explain resistance beyond pragmatic concerns. In Study 1, we analyze moral language in AI-related news headlines from a major US media corpus (COCA, 2018–2024). AI was moralized at levels comparable to GMOs and vaccines, technologies whose moral opposition has received considerable attention. In Studies 2a, 2b, and 3, representative samples of Americans reported attitudes toward several AI applications. Although few participants opposed AI outright (6–44%), most opponents indicated consequence insensitivity, quantity insensitivity, and universalism, suggesting moral motives for opposing AI. Structural equation models revealed that AI moralization was best captured by a single latent factor, indicating a generalized moral sentiment rather than domain-specific risk–benefit appraisals. Qualitative analyses further showed that moralizers typically invoked pragmatic language to justify their opposition. In Study 4, participants from Studies 2b and 3 were re-recruited 75 and 573 days later to complete a behavioral task. Earlier moralization scores predicted greater reluctance to use AI even when instrumentally beneficial—a one standard deviation increase in moralization corresponded to a 42% decrease in AI usage. Together, these findings demonstrate that AI resistance is partly moral in nature, suggesting that reaping the benefits of AI may require addressing moral concerns rather than relying solely on pragmatic arguments.

articles.title: Bayesian Networks for Benchmark Validity in Score Reporting: An ePIRLS Demonstration
articles.authors: Xuejun Ryan Ji
articles.abstract: Large-scale assessments often report achievement through ordered benchmark categories. In PIRLS and ePIRLS, benchmark cut points are derived from the achievement scale and interpreted through scale anchoring. This paper proposes a supplementary Bayesian network (BN) framework for examining whether reported benchmark classifications are supported by local item-response evidence. The framework is not intended to replace item response theory scaling, scale anchoring, or official benchmark procedures. Instead, it treats the benchmark category as an observed reporting classification and evaluates four forms of evidence derived from BN conditional and posterior probabilities: ordering, adjacent-level separation, subgroup transportability, and posterior support for assigned benchmark classifications. Using an ePIRLS 2016 Canadian reading-comprehension subtest with 3,006 students and 17 binary-scored items, the demonstration shows that item-response probabilities were broadly ordered across five benchmark levels, but that evidence varied by item, item type, process of comprehension, benchmark boundary, and language version. Retrieval and Evaluation items showed perfect mean ordering, constructed-response items showed stronger ordering and adjacent separation than multiple-choice items, Integration items showed the smallest average English-French gaps, and posterior support was strongest for middle benchmark levels and weaker at the extremes. The results illustrate how BNs can make benchmark evidence inspectable at the item, item-type, process-of-comprehension, boundary, and subgroup levels. The proposed framework is best understood as a diagnostic validity tool for benchmark interpretation and as a basis for more transparent score-reporting evidence.

articles.title: Disrupting the Mad Genius Myth: Emotional Instability Undermines Creative Self- Beliefs Through Lower Subjective Well-Being in Professional Artists
articles.authors: Florence Khoriaty; Arielle Bonneville-Roussy; Pier Luc de Chantal
articles.abstract: The relationship between emotional instability and creativity in professional artists has long been debated and is often framed through the “mad genius” narrative. Although emotional instability has been associated with creativity, it has also been linked to lower life satisfaction and greater psychological distress, suggesting that it may simultaneously support and undermine creativity-related self-perceptions. This study examined whether life satisfaction and psychological distress mediate the relationship between emotional instability and creative self-beliefs among professional performing artists. A sample of 201 professional performing artists completed the Satisfaction With Life Scale (SWLS), questions from the Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (anxiety) and the Patient Health Questionnaire (depression) to measure psychological distress, the Temperament Evaluation of Memphis, Pisa, Paris, and San Diego (TEMPS-A, for emotional instability), and a validated measure of creative self-beliefs. Confirmatory factor analyses and structural equation modelling were conducted. Results indicated that emotional instability positively predicted psychological distress and negatively predicted life satisfaction. Life satisfaction positively predicted creative self-beliefs, whereas psychological distress was not significantly associated with creative self-beliefs. The association between emotional instability and creative self-beliefs was fully mediated by life satisfaction. These findings challenge the “mad genius” narrative by suggesting that life satisfaction is the principal mechanism linking emotional instability to creative self-beliefs.

articles.title: Sex Differences in Overhand Throwing are Completely Due to Socialization: A Blank Slate “Hypothesis” Unconstrained by Evidence
articles.authors: Robert Deaner; Michael P. Lombardo
articles.abstract: Contemporary evolutionary biology–including evolutionary psychology–assumes an interactionist framework, recognizing the importance of both biological (nature) and environmental (nurture) influences. Despite this scientific consensus, within women’s studies and gender studies, socialization-only explanations of sex differences in behavior persist. This commentary evaluates a recent prominent example: Anne Fausto-Sterling’s 2025 critique of our 2018 Quarterly Review of Biology thesis that the large human sex difference in overhand throwing partly reflects an evolved male adaptation. Fausto-Sterling (FS) argues that our ancestral selection scenarios are unrealistic and that, based on a qualitative study of ten infants, childhood throwing differences are fully explained by early socialization. We demonstrate that FS’s critique fails in several ways. First, FS’s objections about our evolutionary scenarios rely on flawed characterizations of the hunter-gatherer literature, ignoring our primary focus on ancestral combat and discounting cross-cultural data showing far greater male involvement in hunting. Second, FS erroneously implies that childhood throwing performance is our sole evidence, ignoring extensive data presented on elite athletic performance, cross-cultural consistency, prenatal hormone exposure, and distinct skeletal sexual dimorphisms. Finally, FS treats evolutionary and developmental processes as mutually exclusive, failing to recognize that evolutionary theory predicts that socialization will often mediate and amplify evolved behavioral predispositions. We conclude that the persistence of FS’s socialization-only, Blank Slate perspective can only be maintained by ignoring relevant evidence.

articles.title: Does Teacher Praise Make Children Feel Less Smart? Two Preregistered Virtual Reality Experiments
articles.authors: Lena-Emilia Schenker; Jellie Sierksma; Milica Nikolic; Eddie Brummelman
articles.abstract: Does teacher praise make children feel less smart? When two students perform equally, teachers might praise the student they believe worked harder, implicitly conveying to that student that they have lower ability. We tested this hypothesis through two preregistered experiments in a novel Virtual Reality classroom with children aged 7-13 (Study 1: N = 212, Study 2: N = 225). After the child and a virtual classmate performed equally on a task, the teacher praised the child, the classmate, both, or neither. Contrary to hypotheses, there was no evidence that praise made children feel less smart, think that the teacher believed they were less smart, or experience elevated physiological arousal (heart rate, heart rate variability, blushing, skin conductance). Study 2 suggests that children instead viewed praise as a sign of being liked by the teacher. Our studies offer robust evidence that teacher praise may not make children feel less smart.

articles.title: Motives of social media use are differentially associated with mental health outcomes in adolescents
articles.authors: James Lian; Morgane Künzi; Jack L Andrews; John D Gallacher; Sarah Bauermeister
articles.abstract:
Background: Social media use is ubiquitous among adolescents, yet its relationship with mental health may depend on why it is used. This study distinguishes between avoidant-escapist and social-recreational motives for social media use and examines their associations with mental health, wellbeing, and resilience in a large cohort of UK schoolchildren.
Methods: Cross-sectional data were drawn from the BrainWaves study (N = 6820, Mage = 16.72). Social media motives were assessed using 10 items and derived using exploratory factor analysis (EFA) into two latent factors: avoidant-escapist (boredom, addiction, distraction, procrastination) and social-recreational (socialising, communication, entertainment, observation) use. Confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) was used to estimate factor scores, which were examined in linear regression models. Structural equation modelling (SEM) was then applied to estimate associations with anxiety, depression, wellbeing, and resilience, adjusting for gender and deprivation.
Results: In linear regression analyses, avoidant-escapist social media use was associated with higher anxiety and depressive symptoms, whereas social-recreational use was associated with lower levels of both outcomes. No interaction between gender and social media motives was observed. The SEM was a good fit to the data and showed that avoidant-escapist use was associated with higher anxiety and depressive symptoms, and lower wellbeing and resilience. In contrast, social-recreational use was associated with lower anxiety and depressive symptoms, and higher wellbeing and resilience. 
Conclusions: The associations between social media use and adolescent mental health differ by underlying motives. Avoidant-escapist use is linked to poorer outcomes, whereas social-recreational use is associated with more favourable outcomes. Focusing on why adolescents engage with social media, rather than overall use alone, is necessary for understanding risk and informing interventions. However, given the cross-sectional design, causality cannot be inferred, and associations may be bidirectional.

articles.title: A Conversation Guide to Support Color-Conscious Racial Socialization among White Parent-Child Dyads
articles.authors: Sylvia Perry; Jamie L Abaied; Allison Louise Skinner; Deborah Jean Wu; Sara Waters; Meghan George; Lauryn Hoard
articles.abstract: Family discussions may be an important context for shaping White children’s developing racial attitudes, yet the majority of White parents in the U.S. report that they either de-emphasize or avoid discussions of race and racism with their White children. The current study presents a qualitative analysis of racial messages communicated during a guided discussion task designed to scaffold color-conscious conversations about vignettes depicting interpersonal racism. Participants, recruited from a Midwestern U.S. city between 2018 and 2020, included 87 self-identified White parents (91.5% women, 8.5% men; Mage = 42.57 years, SD = 5.14) and their parent-identified White children (ages 8–12; 53% girls, 47% boys; Mage = 8.95 years, SD = 1.16). Most parents were highly educated, middle-class or upper-middle-class, married, and liberal or liberal-leaning. Findings revealed that many parents and children used racial language and endorsed color-conscious messages, such as acknowledging that racial prejudice had occurred in the vignettes. However, some dyads redirected the conversation away from race or made external attributions for racist behavior rather than attributing responsibility to White perpetrators. Together, these findings suggest that structured scaffolding may support more explicit conversations about racism in White families while also highlighting variability in how parents and children interpret and discuss racist behavior.

articles.title: What is it to be aware? A Pragmatic Approach to Comparing Animal Awareness
articles.authors: Nadine Meertens
articles.abstract: Efforts to understand animal abilities face longstanding conceptual and methodological challenges, especially regarding cognition and consciousness. Awareness has largely suffered a similar fate, having been subsumed into these discussions rather than examined on its own merit. This paper asks what it means to be aware. The proposal is to understand it as an organism’s abilities to selectively process, store, and use information in the service of goal-directed action. A pragmatic approach to awareness for comparative research is developed and briefly illustrated through an example of spatial awareness in desert ants.

articles.title: The Accommodation Trap: An Attractor Model of Plasticity-Driven Persistence in Delusion-Like Fixation
articles.authors: Muhammed Joghatayi
articles.abstract:
Delusional disorder is characterized by fixed, encapsulated beliefs embedded in a largely functional cognitive system. Existing computational accounts often emphasize aberrant precision weighting, anomalous salience, or atypical belief updating. Less specified is how a belief-like configuration persists after the initiating condition resolves.
We introduce attractor accommodation, an attractor network account of plasticity-driven persistence. When a belief-like element is held fixed, Hebbian-like plasticity may reorganize surrounding connections, so configurations incorporating it become lower-energy states. What begins as an externally maintained contradiction can become an internally supported attractor.
We implemented this in a Hopfield network with one clamped node and bounded Hebbian-like plasticity and report three findings. (1) Bounded plasticity creates persistent post-clamp fixation, with a sharp threshold (η_critical≈0.008 at α=100) above which all seeds remain trapped. (2) A weight-swap control isolates persistence to the plastic weight matrix: from the same accommodated state, the original matrix produced 100% recovery, the plastic matrix 0% (n=30). (3) The accommodated basin’s resistance to perturbation scales with η×α, and the weight changes building it spare high-connectivity nodes (r=-0.80); the state remains a single-node (1-2/N) deviation across N∈{50,100,200}. Findings replicated under weight-decay normalization and generalized across loads (P/N=0.06"-" 0.14); recovery depended more on accommodation depth and sustained focal duration than on perturbation structure.
The model is a hypothesis-generating prototype demonstrating that bounded Hebbian-like plasticity can produce attractor configurations that persist after their initiating conditions are removed, relevant to delusional encapsulation and treatment-resistant fixation.

articles.title: Predicting and Interpreting Psychological Distress and Mental Health Treatment Experience from Life Experiences and Everyday Behaviors
articles.authors: Takumi Kitahara; Masashi Mizuno
articles.abstract: The aim of this study was to examine whether psychological distress and treatment experience among university students can be predicted using machine learning models based on daily experiences and behaviors. Participants were university students who completed a web-based survey assessing psychological distress (K10 scores), treatment experience, and various life and behavioral factors. To reduce self-report bias, behaviorally specific and concrete variables were selected as predictors. Multiple models, including linear/logistic regression, Random Forest, and CatBoost, were trained and evaluated using nested five-fold cross-validation. The models explained approximately 30% of the variance in K10 scores, and classification performance reached an AUC of .77. Across both studies, key predictors consistently included seeking mental health information, persistent rumination over minor issues, and perceived lack of parental respect. Additional predictors included satisfaction with university entrance exam results and drinking frequency for distress, and experiences of bullying and family history of mental illness for treatment experience. These findings suggest that everyday experiences and behaviors may serve as useful indicators of psychological distress and help-seeking among university students.

articles.title: No evidence for predicting from conflict: A reverse cueing study reveals the limits of congruency-stimulus learning
articles.authors: Nicola Vasta; David Gallego; Luc Vermeylen; Elger Abrahamse; luis jimenez; Senne Braem
articles.abstract: The interplay between cognitive control, memory, and learning is as relevant as it is elusive. Despite broad interest in the notion that people learn to adjust control in response to cues, consensus on control learning is lacking. Previous research struggled to find evidence for cross-trial control learning when different levels of control were cued by stimulus features of the previous trial. We examined whether these inconsistencies arise from a general inability to prepare for control, or from an inability to encode control information within cross-trial associations. To test this, we turned around the cue-control paradigm by using a Stroop task where congruency levels (i.e., control demands) predicted target colors (i.e., stimulus features). Four independent experiments (n = 50; 125; 50; 56) were conducted. We found that people struggle to learn to benefit from congruency-color associations, with no clear evidence for faster reaction times on trials preceded by predictive congruency levels. Post-hoc analyses suggest that participants may have integrated individual congruency-color sequences into predictor-outcome episodes and retrieved these episodes when encountering a given congruency value, only facilitating performance when the same congruency-color sequence was repeated. Overall, our findings suggest that people are largely unable to use control levels as cues to predict task-relevant features across trials, indicating that task-generated conflict may not function effectively as a cue. However, we also offer a novel interpretative framework for researchers interested in investigating episodic retrieval in control-learning paradigms.

articles.title: The relationship between social media use and social isolation in young adults: a systematic review
articles.authors: Yiting Liu; Sojen Pradhan; Xin Wang
articles.abstract: Social isolation has become a growing public health concern, with increasing attention to the potential role of social media in shaping social connection and disconnection. This systematic review synthesises 21 empirical studies published between 2000 and 2024 that examined associations between social media use and social isolation among young adults. Following the PRISMA guidelines, studies were selected from five databases: PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, EBSCOhost, and Google Scholar. Thematic analysis identified three major categories: (1) motivations and patterns of social media use, (2) Effects of Text-based and Visual Communication Channels, and (3) No Association Between Social Media and Social Isolation. While 15 studies reported a positive association between certain patterns of social media use and increased feelings of isolation, four identified potential benefits, and two found no significant relationships. Specifically, excessive or passive use without interaction was more commonly linked to negative outcomes, whereas purposeful and interactive use appeared to support social connection. Differences in platform specifics (e.g., text-based vs. image-based) and contextual variables such as the COVID-19 pandemic contributed to the variability of results. Most studies adopted cross-sectional designs and applied varied measurement tools, which may have influenced the consistency and comparability of results.

articles.title: Beyond Individual Resilience: Institutional Stressors, Burnout, and Coping among Baptist Pastors in Urban Ghana
articles.authors: McFaustine Kofi Mahama; Yaw Adu-Gyamfi; Gabriel Osei Forkuo
articles.abstract:
Background: Baptist pastors in Ghana face intense urban pressures, yet empirical studies on their occupational wellbeing are scarce. This research investigates burnout and institutional conflict, utilizing a novel socio ecological framework to uniquely link macro environmental pressures with denominational policy.
Methods: A cross sectional survey instrument was administered to 200 ordained GBC pastors in Kumasi. Using Python, descriptive statistics, Pearson and Spearman correlations, Cronbach alpha reliability, Kruskal Wallis, and chi square analyses were computed alongside qualitative content analysis. A novel four level conceptual framework mapped distal context, proximal stressors, and coping strategies.
Results: Church conflict was highest rated (M = 3.51, SD = 1.00; 71.5% agreement), followed by training gaps (64.5%) and work life imbalance (63.5%). Qualitative findings highlighted financial pressure, burnout, and family disruption. Mid career pastors (11-20 years) exhibited highest burnout, which did not differ significantly by location. Sabbaticals (55.5%), training (53.5%), prayer and fasting (52.0%), peer support (50.5%), and professional counselling (44.5%) were common coping strategies.
Conclusions: The novel framework reveals that pastoral distress is a systemic crisis driven by institutional deficits rather than individual vulnerability. Distal urban pressures interact with proximal stressors to generate distributed distress, which is only partially moderated by coping resources. By conceptualizing these dynamic, nested connections, the framework provides a comprehensive roadmap for GBC policy interventions, demonstrating that the denomination must establish funded mental health systems, reform mandatory transfer policies, and implement structured continuing education pathways to proactively address early career vulnerability and ensure long term ministerial health across sub Saharan Africa.

articles.title: The Experimental Study of Habitual Behavior in Humans: A Review of the Validity of Current Protocols
articles.authors: Pablo Martínez-López; Francisco Garre-Frutos; Miguel A. Vadillo; David Luque
articles.abstract: Although habits are widely recognized as playing a key role across psychological science, current laboratory methods may limit our understanding of these behaviors. Within the dual‑system framework, habits are defined as context-triggered, outcome‑insensitive responses that emerge gradually with repeated instrumental training. Many human experimental paradigms have been developed in an attempt to test this characterization of habits. However, in most cases, they have not provided clear evidence of habit formation, suggesting a gap between habit theory and existing methodologies. In the present study, we systematically review evidence on the construct validity of fourteen paradigms in which the amount of training was manipulated. We also reanalyze publicly available datasets from studies using these paradigms to assess the psychometric reliability of their main measures, thereby extending the available validity evidence. Paradigms that most closely mirror classical approaches derived from habit theory have consistently failed to induce habits, whereas more recent approaches have revealed training effects. However, these novel paradigms often rely on broader operationalizations that substantially diverge from traditional definitions of habit. Among the paradigms reviewed, the forced‑response task stands out as capable of inducing habitual behavior while providing a narrowly operationalized measure with acceptable reliability. Finally, we argue that strengthening the credibility of the field will require greater attention to the psychometric properties of habit measures, including precise operationalization, reliability, and systematic validity testing.

articles.title: Situational and longitudinal associations between motivational beliefs, anxiety, and learning during retrieval practice
articles.authors: Elisabeth Graf; Jakob Schwerter; Daria Katharina Benden; Thomas Dimpfl; Fani Lauermann
articles.abstract: Motivational and emotional processes are presumed to play a key role in shaping who engages in retrieval practice. Furthermore, students' success or struggle with retrieval practice may shape their subsequent motivations and emotions, yielding “feedback loops” in which these processes are mutually linked. Building upon situated expectancy-value theory and control-value theory, the present study examined a) concurrent intraindividual associations between control and value beliefs, anxiety, and engagement and achievement during retrieval practice, b) their reciprocal intraindividual associations over time, and c) whether students' aggregated motivational and emotional experiences predicted their end-of-term achievement and anxiety. In addition to an begin- and end-of-term questionnaire, students (N=636) participated and reported their motivational experiences weekly during retrieval practice sessions across a semester. Dynamic structural equation models revealed that control, intrinsic value, and utility value were negatively related to anxiety, while cost was positively related to students' anxiety during retrieval practice. Anxiety was negatively associated with engagement and achievement. Additionally, reciprocal within-person associations indicative of feedback loops emerged, with engagement and partly achievement relating positively to subsequent control and value. Students' aggregated achievement during retrieval practice positively predicted their end-of-semester achievement, whereas trait- and aggregated state motivational and emotional experiences showed no incremental predictive effects. However, higher aggregated state anxiety was associated with higher end-of-semester test anxiety. Overall, these findings suggest that retrieval practice exercises can contribute to self-reinforcing motivational-emotional cycles across the semester, pointing to a pathway through which momentary experiences during retrieval practice may accumulate into end-of-term differences in test anxiety and academic achievement.

articles.title: A Dialectical Model of Self-Love: Worth, Compassion, and Embodied Self-Engagement
articles.authors: Kristyna Losova
articles.abstract: Although the concept of self-love has gained cultural and clinical prominence, it remains theoretically diffuse and frequently conflated with self-esteem, narcissism, or self-compassion. This article proposes a dialectically structured framework that conceptualizes self-love as a dynamic mode of self-relation rather than a static trait or evaluative attitude. The model identifies three interdependent dimensions: (1) inherent worth, defined as the recognition and protection of non-contingent dignity and value; (2) self-compassion, conceptualized as a regulatory process balancing soothing and corrective motivation in response to distress; and (3) embodied self-engagement, defined as the bodily capacity to remain present with activation, pleasure, effort, and intensity without abandoning contact with oneself. Each dimension contains an internal tension between receptive and agentic functions, allowing self-relation to remain flexible across contexts of success, failure, and growth. By specifying the integration of evaluative, compassionate, and embodied processes, the framework offers a conceptual account of self-love as a flexible mode of self-relation across conditions of comfort, adversity, activation, and change. The article outlines conceptual distinctions from related constructs and proposes a programmatic agenda for empirical validation.

articles.title: Modeling Decision Bias in dockerHDDM: A Systematic Comparison and Evaluation
articles.authors: Siyu Wu; Wanke Pan; Hu Chuan-Peng
articles.abstract: Decision bias refers to a systematic tendency to approach or avoid a particular option in two-alternative forced-choice (2AFC) tasks. With the advent of easy-to-use tools, diffusion-decision model (DDM) is widely used to distinguish two types of latent cognitive processes, bias in starting point or in efficiency, providing a powerful tool for understanding a variety of psychological phenomena across different fields. However, correctly estimating these two types of bias requires specifying the model to match the type of bias being investigated. So far, no study has systematically evaluated the different model configurations for modelling decision bias in HDDM, the most widely used python package for DDM. Here we evaluated nine model specifications using the three main modeling modules in HDDM across both simulated and empirical data. Our results show that the default function, which uses accuracy coding of data without flipping parameters, fails to recover either the starting point bias or the drift rate bias. Only model specifications that directly estimate the drift bias parameter, drift criterion (dc), or apply appropriate parameter flipping recover true bias parameters. By applying optimal specification to empirical data, we uncovered a novel psychological effect that went undetected in the original study. We provide concrete, evidence based recommendations for model specification and fully reproducible code. This work ensures accurate parameter estimation and the correct interpretation of parameter when modeling decision biases.

articles.title: Good Kitty, Bad Bank? Rescoring Miscalibrated CATs Improves Accuracy
articles.authors: NA; Benjamin Domingue; Nilam Ram; Michael C. Frank
articles.abstract: Computerized adaptive tests (CAT) depend on accurately calibrated item banks, but in practice, the item parameters governing deployment of the item banks are often based on small pilot samples, transported across populations, or assigned placeholder values pending proper calibration. We decompose the impact of miscalibration on subsequent ability estimates into two components: item selection error and scoring error. While selection error is not recoverable, rescoring using better calibrations, and without any additional testing burden, can potentially provide a post-hoc fix to scoring errors.  In a simulation study, we find that rescoring substantially reduces root mean squared error (RMSE), with scoring error accounting for 86 to 96% of miscalibration-attributable error. In Study 2, we apply this framework to data from 2,657 children (ages 5 to 12) assessed across three pilot sites in the LEVANTE project. Using identical scoring methods with old versus new item parameters, we found that rescoring shifted scores substantially and reordered children's relative standing; external validation confirms the rescored estimates are better, not just different. Low-ability and younger children are disproportionately affected by rescoring, underscoring the equity implications. These results highlight the utility of rescoring for any CAT using early-stage, transported, or AI-generated item parameters, and how rescoring can enable rapid deployment of assessments to new populations.

articles.title: The Entangled Loop: Emotion, quantum-like interference and hierarchy in the architecture of consciousness
articles.authors: Morten L. Kringelbach; Fernando Rosas; Ruben Eero Laukkonen; Shamil Chandaria; Yonatan Sanz Perl; Gustavo Deco
articles.abstract: We propose three entangled characteristics that explain the functional properties of consciousness: Emotion, quantum-like interference and hierarchy. The argument proceeds from thermodynamic first principles. Biological systems must stay far from equilibrium at minimal energy cost, and emotion is one of evolution’s heuristic solutions to this constraint. Valenced summary statistics compress the intractable decision space of survival into rapidly evaluable approximations. The same thermodynamic pressure selects for greater representational power at lower energy cost by promoting quantum-like inference – through interference in coupled oscillators across spectral gaps in structural connectivity – and hierarchical integration through a core group of orchestrating regions. Here we first derive emotion as the thermodynamic compression of adaptive decision-making and review the direct causal tractability of the distributed hedonic network through deep brain stimulation and optogenetic intervention. Second, we show how quantum-like inference follows from the same thermodynamic pressure demanding hierarchical orchestration, and we distinguish the physical mechanism of interference from the computational function of inference it makes possible. Third, we ask whether emotion is architectural rather than a weighted parameter within an otherwise neutral model. Support comes from recent mechanistic interpretability research on large language models, recovering the three features of two-dimensional geometry of mammalian emotion, quantum-like representations and synergistic hierarchy in human linguistic output. We argue that these signatures are the echo of the consciousness-generating architecture in the language it produces, not evidence that language models are themselves conscious. We draw out some of the consequences of this perspective for theories of consciousness, for the hard problem, for falsifiable empirical prediction and for the clinical restoration of flourishing. This convergent evidence supports our account of the Entangled Loop: A perspective on consciousness in which emotion is architecturally primary, quantum-like inference binds conscious content, and where the orchestrating regions coordinate both. This offers a principled route to rebalancing the affective architecture in neuropsychiatric disorders and to flourishing understood as meaningful pleasure achievable in the moment.

articles.title: From Retreat to Reality: A Qualitative Study of How Adolescents Apply Mindfulness One Month After a Weeklong Retreat
articles.authors: Jiaxiong Li; Brian Galla; Michael James Tumminia
articles.abstract:
Objectives 
Mindfulness programs are increasingly delivered to adolescents to help them navigate emotional and social challenges, yet little is known about how they apply these skills after program completion. While most research has focused on short-term, school-based programs and intra-personal outcomes, this qualitative study explored how adolescents integrated mindfulness into their lives one month after participating in an intensive, weeklong retreat. 
Methods
Fifteen adolescents (Mage = 16.74 years, SD = 1.58 years; 73% girls, 27% boys; 53.3% White, 6.7% African American, 6.7% East Asian, and 33.3% Multiracial) completed interviews one month after a six-day mindfulness retreat. Participants reflected on retreat takeaways and described how they applied mindfulness to personal and social challenges. Data were analyzed using a three-cycle coding process to identify patterns in intra- and inter-personal applications.
Results	
Adolescents described intra-personal applications, such as self-awareness and regulation (100% of adolescents; e.g., regulating thoughts and emotions), practicing self-compassion and acceptance (73%; e.g., being less self-critical), and gaining a broader perspective (60%; e.g., focusing on what matters). They also reported inter-personal applications, including navigating social anxiety and building connections (73%; e.g., using mindfulness to reduce social apprehension), engaging in compassionate communication (67%; e.g., active listening and providing emotional support), and greater social awareness and attunement (53%; e.g., practicing empathy and perspective-taking). All adolescents described experiences in both domains.
Conclusions
This study offers insights into how adolescents integrate mindfulness into daily life after an immersive retreat and suggests such experiences foster transferable skills supporting developmental needs. Implications for contemplative science are discussed.

articles.title: Defining Wellness: Conceptions of Ideal Mental Health in Ancient Yogic Texts and Contemporary Indian Views
articles.authors: Kriti Gupta; Jyotsna Agrawal
articles.abstract:
The prevailing modern operationalisations of positive mental health have been criticised for their inherent focus on sense-based, egotistical happiness. Addressing this concern, many contemporary mental health professionals have turned to indigenous ideas and classical philosophical texts to gain insights into optimal well-being. With the same aim, a cross-sectional, mixed-methods exploratory research was designed to uncover the meanings of ideal mental health among 240 urban adults (Male =136, Female = 104; mean age, 38.17 years) from North India, along with exploring themes of ideal mental health in selected classical texts from Indian psychology, i.e., The Bhagavad Gita (700 verses) and Patanjali’s Yogasutras (196 verses). Their content analysis revealed five broad themes: Sthitaprajñā (Steady wisdom), Anasakti (Non-attachment), Svadharma (Personal duty),  Shraddha (Faith), Ishwar Pranidhana (Surrender to the Divine/God). From the thematic analysis of subjective meanings, two major themes emerged: Describing ideal mental state as a positive mental state and Dismissive attitude towards ideal mental state. A notable similarity was found between the traditional, non-egotistical understanding of mental health and contemporary community aspirations in India. However, there were certain critical differences in opinions regarding the attainability of such goals. This research provides important contextual evidence that can help expand the framework of well-being.
Keywords: Ideal mental health, Yogic scriptures, well-being, Indian psychology
