articles.title: Does Teacher Praise Make Children Feel Less Smart? Two Preregistered Virtual Reality Experiments
articles.authors: Lena-Emilia Schenker; Jellie Sierksma; Milica Nikolic; Eddie Brummelman
articles.abstract: Does teacher praise make children feel less smart? When two students perform equally, teachers might praise the student they believe worked harder, implicitly conveying to that student that they have lower ability. We tested this hypothesis through two preregistered experiments in a novel Virtual Reality classroom with children aged 7-13 (Study 1: N = 212, Study 2: N = 225). After the child and a virtual classmate performed equally on a task, the teacher praised the child, the classmate, both, or neither. Contrary to hypotheses, there was no evidence that praise made children feel less smart, think that the teacher believed they were less smart, or experience elevated physiological arousal (heart rate, heart rate variability, blushing, skin conductance). Study 2 suggests that children instead viewed praise as a sign of being liked by the teacher. Our studies offer robust evidence that teacher praise may not make children feel less smart.

articles.title: Adolescents value learning about a newly acquainted peer in a face-to-face interaction
articles.authors: Kathryn A. McNaughton; Katherine Rice Warnell; Sarah Nelson; Eva H. Telzer; Elizabeth Redcay
articles.abstract: Adolescence is a developmental period characterized by heightened importance of peers. Adolescents actively seek out opportunities to learn about peers, while discriminating between different peers in the learning process. Previous work has predominantly focused on how youth value learning about close friends, popular kids, or majority opinions, and has not yet investigated how adolescents may value learning about other sources of peer information, such as a peer they have just met. Here, we present findings from a novel behavioral task in which adolescents 11-15 years old had the opportunity to earn money as they made choices to learn information about a newly acquainted peer interaction partner (‘interaction peer’), the most common response of other adolescents their age (‘majority peer’), or trivia facts. Adolescents valued learning about the interaction peer over the majority peer and trivia facts, giving up nearly a penny for each opportunity to learn about the interaction peer. They also valued learning about the majority peer over trivia. Age and gender significantly interacted to predict heterogeneity in youth’s choices: with increasing age, male adolescents were increasingly motivated to learn about the majority peer, while female adolescents were decreasingly motivated to learn about the majority peer. Together, these results highlight gender differences in how peer information is valued across adolescent development. Characterizing how youth value learning about peers informs understanding of the way peers impact adolescents’ social worlds through relationship development and social influence.

articles.title: Distinct Neural Representations across Discrete and Continuous Non-symbolic Ratios
articles.authors: Rebekka Lagacé-Cusiac; Jessica Adrienne Grahn; Daniel Ansari
articles.abstract: Relative magnitudes, such as ratios and proportions, are crucial to everyday life. According to the ratio processing system (RPS) theory, ratios across different formats share a common neural code. Prior neuroimaging studies have shown that ratios across different visual formats are processed by overlapping brain areas, thus providing support for this hypothesis. However, overlapping activation found from univariate analyses does not necessarily imply common representations. The aim of this study was to probe the key prediction of the RPS theory by investigating the neural representation of ratios depicted in discrete and continuous magnitudes using multivariate fMRI analyses. Thirty participants completed a delayed ratio comparison task on discrete (sets of dots) and continuous (line lengths) magnitudes while in the MRI scanner. Using representational similarity analysis (RSA), we examined the similarity between ratio representations across high-level variations, such as magnitude type (length and numerosity), and low-level variations, such as variations in line orientation and dot size, throughout the brain, notably in parietal areas. Results did not support a common ratio representation across discrete and continuous magnitudes. Instead, ratios in numerosity and length seemed to be encoded in qualitatively distinct ways. Altogether, results suggest that ratios are not represented in a format-independent way in the brain, thus challenging the RPS theory.

articles.title: Beyond the Western Canon: Broadening the Foundations of Psychological Science
articles.authors: Leher Singh; Megan Pham
articles.abstract: Modern psychology has attended closely to the limits of WEIRD samples. At the same time, the field has paid far less attention to the foundational narrative which preceded and contributed to a WEIRD sampling bias. This paper connects skews sampling to a WEIRD philosophy of mind, expressed through two interconnected canons: an institutional canon that centers Euro-American laboratory psychology and a philosophical canon that incorrectly represents questions of mind as emerging primarily from a Western intellectual lineage. Drawing on transregional histories of psychological science and philosophies of mind, the paper formulates a broader institutional and philosophical canon and discusses the contributions of scholars, traditions, and intellectual lineages often left outside psychology’s standard narrative. The aim of this paper is to broaden the historical and conceptual foundations of psychological science and thus expand areas of inquiry and explanatory frameworks available to the field.

articles.title: Rated E for Emotional: Children's Understanding of Death, Conversations About Death, and Parental Predictors
articles.authors: Henrique Santos; David Menendez
articles.abstract: Understanding death involves integrating biological, spiritual, and affective concepts, which are shaped by watching media and interacting with parents. However, parents sometimes shield children from information about death because they believe the children are too young to understand it. We examined the alignment between children’s actual understanding of death and their parents’ predictions of their understanding. We also had dyads watch and discuss a short animated film depicting death to observe their conversations about death. We recruited 68 4- to 6-year-olds (31M, 36F, 1NB; 56% White) and their parents from the United States from April 2024 to May 2025. We found that children’s biological understanding increases with age, whereas spiritual and affective understanding remained stable across this age period. Parents tended to overestimate their children’s understanding of several biological and affective aspects of death, suggesting that parents’ shielding beliefs are not related to children’s actual understanding of death. Although most families mentioned death, their conversations were brief, showing that death conversations are more common and less in-depth than prior research suggests. Parents had an active role in the conversation and focused predominantly on affective aspects of death. These findings highlight that promoting in-depth conversations about death might require supporting parents.

articles.title: Optimizing Care for Older Transgender and Non-Binary Adults: A Co-created Agenda for Future Research
articles.authors: Mara Pieri; Nessa Millet; Martijn Anthonio; Alex Bakker; Maya Chew; Elia Covolan; Zowie Davy; Tim de Jong; Joost Derwig; Koen Dreijerink
articles.abstract:
Background
Despite a growing population of older transgender and non-binary (TNB) adults, evidence-based guidance for their care remains limited. There is a pressing need for a comprehensive research agenda developed in collaboration with, rather than solely about, this population.
Aim
This study aimed to co-create key areas and priorities for future research to improve quality of care for older TNB adults.
Methods
A four-day workshop was held with a group of 19 TNB individuals, clinicians, and academic researchers, representing diverse lived experiences, ages, occupations, and cultural backgrounds. A series of lived experience and scientific presentations were given and co-creation sessions were held to collaboratively explore the study’s aim. Concept mapping and design thinking principles were employed to generate and thematically organize insights during co-creation sessions. Data transcripts were then further analyzed using template analysis in consultation with co-creators.
Results
Three overarching themes were constructed. The first theme (reframing research best practice) offers guidance on future research conduct, emphasizing the importance of adopting participatory approaches, inclusivity, and the expansion of methodological perspectives to better capture complexity. The second theme (addressing knowledge gaps) highlights gaps in our understanding of gender-affirming medical care in older age and biopsychosocial influences on health. The third theme (practice-based research and knowledge mobilization) focuses on support for known challenges such as limited social support, best practice in elderly care, recognition and navigation of systemic power structures, and effective translation and dissemination of knowledge.
Discussion
This co-created agenda is intended to catalyze more responsive, inclusive, and equitable research and practice, ensuring that the voices and needs of older TNB adults remain central to efforts aimed at promoting healthy aging and quality of life in this population.

articles.title: A selection of environmental sounds for behavioural and neuroimaging research: Introducing the EnviSounds dataset
articles.authors: Magdalena Kachlicka; Jasper J.F. van den Bosch; Jian Kang; Frederic Dick
articles.abstract: With growing interest in ecologically valid stimuli and the proliferation of recognition and classification algorithms, environmental sounds are increasingly a topic of interest and study. However, there is a lack of consensus as to which sound categories should be selected, and which exemplars should represent these categories. Indeed, many existing datasets include only one exemplar per sound class. This is not characteristic of the actual acoustic environment and can lead to brittle and unrepresentative computational solutions and experimental results. Importantly, the existing literature provides relatively little information about how human listeners perceive the recognizability, similarity and representativeness of acoustically and perceptually varying sounds. Here, we introduce the Environmental Sounds (EnviSounds) dataset. It provides data from over 1000 human listeners performing different tasks (recognition, similarity judgement, goodness of fit) on 53 commonly encountered environmental sound categories of human, natural and man-made sounds, each represented by 10 different exemplars. We provide behavioural indices of within-class similarity and goodness of exemplar for all samples. Furthermore, we include normative data for recognition and identification latency, accuracy, and imageability, enabling researchers to select items for experiments based on pre-defined criteria across these dimensions. With this dataset, environmental sounds can be considered at various levels of complexity: sounds produced by the same sources varying in their acoustics (within-class acoustic variability) and acoustically similar sounds produced by different sources (between-category confusion). The dataset can be used for specifying training sets with individual sound sources, defining the categorical boundaries, or providing additional variables to building prediction models.

articles.title: Environmental Dynamics and Exam Stress among Nigerian  Adolescents: The Mitigating Role of Regional Community Support Structures
articles.authors: Ifunanyachukwu Daniella Ajaero
articles.abstract:
This study investigates the structural relationship between external environmental 
stressors, local community support networks, and examination-induced anxiety among 
secondary school seniors preparing for high-stakes standardized assessments in Nigeria. 
Using a descriptive quantitative survey layout framework, data was collected from a 
cross-sectional sample of N=95 Senior Secondary School 3 (SS3) students at Rochas 
Foundation College (Owerri, Imo State). Structural metrics focused on evaluating the 
mitigating capacity of multi-tier support structures, specifically comparing localized 
family communication channels and informal peer group interaction dynamics against 
standardized stress markers. Quantitative analysis revealed that peer support 
environments served as the primary self-reported buffer against exam panic, yielding a
dominant mean structural preference ranking of 1.38, followed closely by familial 
support networks at 1.69. Crucially, candidates lacking robust peer group integration 
exhibited extreme examination anxiety indices averaging 18.63 out of a 20-point max 
scale, whereas students with high peer-tier positioning dropped significantly to a mean 
stress index of 15.10, representing an empirical 18.91% buffer in psychological stress 
resilience. Lived-experience frameworks were integrated utilizing existential coping
metrics to evaluate individual cognitive adaptation under pressure. The findings suggest 
that structural implementation of peer-led learning circles within secondary academic 
institutions offers a low-cost, high-impact behavioral intervention to optimize student 
mental health during high-pressure educational transitions.

articles.title: Behavioral Emotion Regulation in Internalizing Disorders: A Transdiagnostic Psychometric Evaluation of the German Behavioral Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (BERQ)
articles.authors: Sarah Aline Wellan; Sarah Wyka; Till Langhammer; Gina-Isabelle Henze; Lars Schulze; Lydia Fehm; Norbert Kathmann; Babette Renneberg; Shuyan Liu; Susanne Erk
articles.abstract:
Background
Behavioral emotion regulation (ER) is conceptually distinct from cognitive ER, yet dedicated instruments to assess behavioral strategies are limited and rarely validated in clinical samples. Both ER domains relate to of the psychopathology of internalizing disorders, with potentially different contributions across diagnoses. 

Aims
This study evaluates the psychometric properties of the German version of the Behavioral Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (BERQ), including its factorial structure, reliability, construct validity, and measurement invariance across gender and three diagnostic groups: unipolar depression, anxiety disorders, and obsessive-compulsive disorder.

Methods
Baseline data from N = 462 outpatients with internalizing disorders prior to cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) were analyzed. Confirmatory factor analyses with subsample cross-validation, as well as measurement invariance testing and correlational and regression analyses, were conducted to evaluate the BERQ and its associations with clinical measures and other ER measures.

Results
The five-factor structure of the BERQ was supported, with good internal consistency and measurement invariance across gender and diagnostic groups. Several behavioral strategies were significantly associated with depressive symptoms and global symptom severity, with withdrawal showing the most pronounced effects. Behavioral ER explained additional variance in symptom severity beyond cognitive ER strategies. Latent mean differences indicated clinically relevant differences across diagnostic groups. 

Conclusions
The findings support the German BERQ as a reliable and valid instrument for assessing behavioral ER in clinical populations and highlight the relevance of behavioral strategies for understanding the psychopathology of internalizing disorders. Improved measurement of behavioral ER may therefore contribute to advances in targeted interventions and clinical research.

articles.title: A motor basis for a foreign accent?  Linguistic context constrains the generalization of second language speech motor plans
articles.authors: Lindsay Heyland; Samantha Palatinus; Douglas M Shiller; Daniel Robert Lametti
articles.abstract: Most second-language learners never achieve native-like speech, even after years of immersion. Contemporary models of second language speech attribute a foreign accent to interference between first and second language speech-sound representations. But difficulties in second language speech may also relate to limits on the adaptability of second language speech motor plans. Here, we test this hypothesis using sensorimotor adaptation to altered auditory feedback. Forty-five first-language English, second-language French bilinguals adapted their vowel production to compensate for a real-time formant alteration during the fluid production of English and French sentences. Critically, the formant alteration was equal in magnitude but opposite in direction between languages; to compensate for the opposing alterations, bilinguals acquired distinct sensorimotor adaptations in each language. We then examined the extent to which these language-specific adaptations might generalize to new linguistic contexts that systematically varied in their degree of syntactic similarity with the adapted sentences. Despite equivalent initial adaptation in English and French, second language (French) motor adaptations showed less generalization to new syntactic contexts than their first language (English) counterparts. The results reveal a motor constraint on second language speech. Compared to speech in one’s first language, second language speech motor representations generalize poorly beyond the linguistic context in which they are acquired—a deficit in motor control that may contribute to the persistence of a foreign accent.

articles.title: Metacontingency Analysis of Practices Associated to Mental Health
articles.authors: Blanca Patricia Ballesteros de Valderrama; Monica Novoa Gomez
articles.abstract:
This article proposes an analysis of mental health and psychotherapy as a result of cultural practices, based on contextual behavioral science, which combines behavioral analysis and cultural analysis. Similarly, it proposes complementing the analysis of public policy and other regulatory instruments with analytical tools from this scientific perspective, such as metacontingencies and macrocontingencies, which allow us to understand a policy or any regulatory instrument as a metacontingency system capable of constructing cultural practices related to mental health and facilitate the analysis of molar and molecular factors in psychotherapy. Reference is made to the case of Colombia as a country with particular conditions that could be addressed with an integrated model. Finally, methodological challenges and implications are addressed.
Keywords: metacontingencies; macrocontingencies; cultural practices; psychotherapy; public policy and mental health.

articles.title: Mind Wandering and Task-unrelated thoughts: off-task mental activity during rest, evoked by preferred music, does not impede short-term memory consolidation
articles.authors: Juan Felipe Ariza; Juan David Leongómez
articles.abstract: Research has shown that post-learning rest periods are beneficial for memory consolidation. These same periods of rest are also prone to evoke mind wandering (MW) episodes which, when prevalent during learning, have been observed to prevent consolidation. However, although research has focused on characterising the role of MW during encoding, less is known about its role on consolidation when occurring during post-learning rest periods. Using the caught probe paradigm, we measured the effect of task-unrelated thought (TUT) episodes (as a proxy for MW) occurring during two conditions of post-learning rest, in silence and with preferred music, over performance in a recognition and classification task. We found that TUT episodes during neither type of rest are predictive of performance in the classification task, even though participants are predicted to perform above chance. We additionally found that participants are predicted to perform above chance in the recognition task after rest with music, but no interaction between TUT episodes and performance after rest with music; and that the prevalence of TUT episodes interacts with rest in silence to predict performance in the recognition task, whereby a higher number of TUT episodes during rest in silence predicted higher performance.

articles.title: Social media and smartphone use are associated with blunted striatal reward response in early adolescence
articles.authors: Amrita Lamba; Matt R. Nassar; Oriel FeldmanHall
articles.abstract: Adolescents spend unprecedented time on smartphones, raising concerns that screen time, and social media use in particular, may negatively impact developing reward circuitry in the brain—the very system critical for motivation and flexible learning. We tested whether social media and overall smartphone use are associated with altered risk-taking, neural responsivity, and mental health outcomes in early adolescence. Twenty-nine participants (ages 13-15) completed a reinforcement learning gambling task while undergoing fMRI. We fit a computational model to estimate trial-wise reward prediction errors (RPEs) during a gambling task and quantified striatal RPE sensitivity, a canonical neural signature of dopaminergic reward learning. We also measured time spent on social media applications and total daily smartphone use. Adolescents averaged nearly 6 hours of screen time per day and exhibited strong preferences for the social media apps TikTok and Snapchat. Greater social media use predicted increased gambling behavior, indicating increased reward-seeking behavior. Increased screen time and social media use were associated with blunted striatal activity, suggesting a reduced neural responsiveness to rewards. App-specific analyses indicate that these diminished striatal responses were largely driven by TikTok use. Finally, smartphone use was associated with greater anxiety, depression, and intolerance of uncertainty, which were also related to reduced striatal sensitivity. These findings provide evidence consistent with an emerging maladaptive profile that links technology exposure, mental health, and reward circuit function.

articles.title: Object perception encodes mutually exclusive possibilities
articles.authors: Gabor Brody; Peter Mazalik; Roman Feiman
articles.abstract: Human adults represent and reason about possibilities: multiple, mutually exclusive, non-actual ways the world could be. It is debated whether this is a late-developing, human-specific capacity and whether it requires natural language. We propose that instead of a unitary high-level capacity, multiple cognitive capacities might independently represent possibilities in different ways and with different properties. Our case study is object perception, a capacity shared with infants and non-human animals. Using methods that tap specifically into visual processes, across four experiments (N = 160), we found possibility representations in vision tasks that do not involve high-level reasoning or language. In Experiments 1 and 2, we found evidence for the visual encoding of possible object identities: When there were two possible ways that features might map onto visual objects, both possible mappings were primed. In Experiment 3, we found that visual feature-binding is a necessary precondition for such priming, ruling out low-level associative and high-level reasoning-based alternatives. Finally, in Experiment 4, we discovered that the visual system cannot encode one visual object possibly being at two possible locations. This pattern helps to explain a host of previous findings from the comparative and developmental literature and suggests that the unit of analysis for possibility representations should be the cognitive capacity rather than the agent.

articles.title: Common Humanity and Resilience: A Scoping Review
articles.authors: Sarah McAllister; Milan Le; Kiera Young; Patrick R. Labelle; Jess Whitley
articles.abstract:
Objectives: Social support and self-compassion are well-established predictors of resilience across diverse populations and contexts. While the associations between resilience and both social support and self-compassion are well documented in the literature, common humanity—a subcomponent of self-compassion related to social support that involves recognizing the universality of painful experiences—has received comparatively little attention in resilience research. Accordingly, a scoping review was conducted to map the empirical literature on common humanity and resilience, synthesize their associations, and examine definitions and conceptualizations of both constructs.
Methods: To identify studies, a search strategy was developed and executed across seven databases. The titles and abstracts as well as the full texts of emerging publications were independently screened by three reviewers. Data from 40 studies that met the inclusion criteria by consensus were extracted and summarized.
Results: Among the 4,353 references identified, findings from 40 included studies indicate a consistent positive relationship between common humanity and resilience, with higher common humanity associated with more effective coping, adaptation, and recovery from adversity. Regardless of the definitions and conceptualizations of both common humanity and resilience—as well as differences in geographic location, research type, study population, sample size, or form of adversity under investigation—common humanity consistently evidenced a robust association with resilience. Nevertheless, this relationship varied depending on the constructs examined, the form of adversity studied, the underlying mechanisms, and the conceptualizations of common humanity. These results elucidate the association between common humanity and resilience while identifying the factors that shape this relationship.
Conclusions: This scoping review advances knowledge in the field by identifying conceptual and methodological gaps, offering a foundation for future research, and informing the development of interventions that draw on common humanity to enhance resilience.
Preregistration A review protocol was developed a priori and registered on the Open Science Framework (https://osf.io/rby8e/).
Keywords: Common humanity, resilience, adaptation, adversity, scoping review

articles.title: Planning paths not taken: False memories for unexecuted actions
articles.authors: Maia Juliet Armstrong; Daniel Yon; Silvia Seghezzi
articles.abstract: Action planning involves representing multiple possible actions, including alternatives that are ultimately not taken. While such representations support flexible behaviour, they may also bias memory for one's own actions. Participants (N = 24) performed a goal-directed maze navigation task in which they planned routes with multiple alternatives before executing a chosen path, followed by a recognition memory test. On some trials, an available path was blocked after planning, forcing participants to revise their plan. False memories were higher for paths that were blocked than for unchosen but still-available alternatives, and this effect was strongest when the blocked path was the shortest available route. This pattern suggests that the depth to which a route is incorporated into an action plan determines the strength of its subsequent mnemonic trace. A second experiment (N = 33) confirmed that the effect requires prior plannability and cannot be explained by the visual salience of the block alone. Together, these findings reveal a direct link between action planning and memory distortion for one's own actions.

articles.title: SCEIMA: Social Coordination Evaluation through Integrated Model Analysis
articles.authors: Bavo Van Kerrebroeck; Caroline Palmer; Guillaume Dumas; Alexander Pantelis Demos
articles.abstract: Computational models are increasingly used as interactive partners in studies of human coordination, yet it remains unclear whether observed differences in human behavior reflect properties of the models themselves, changes in human behavior elicited by such artificial partners, or both. We introduce SCEIMA (Social Coordination Evaluation through Integrated Model Analysis), a two-stage framework designed to disentangle human-specific, model-specific, and interaction-driven contributions to coordination in human–machine interaction paradigms. In the empirical stage, human participants perform a coordination task with both human partners and computational models, establishing reference human–human and human–model interaction patterns. In the analytical stage, the same models are paired with one another and optimized through simulations to reproduce empirical coordination metrics. Comparing human–human, human–model, and simulated model–model interactions reveals whether coordination differences arise from intrinsic model dynamics, from human adaptation to artificial partners, or from their interaction. SCEIMA treats computational models as contrastive instruments whose capacity to elicit and reproduce human behavior can be systematically evaluated. We illustrate the framework with two distinct case-studies, a sensorimotor synchronization task and a conversational turn-taking task, showing how distinct outcome patterns diagnose the sources of coordination differences. By providing a principled methodological framework for evaluating interactive computational models, SCEIMA improves interpretability in human–machine interaction research and informs the design of artificial agents that coordinate with humans more naturally and responsively.

articles.title: Silent and Underestimated Climate Change Concern Without Behavioural Backlash: Findings From a Representative Dutch Survey on Climate Change Communication
articles.authors: Sandra Klaperski-van der Wal; Erin Rose Newman-Grigg; Esther K. Papies
articles.abstract:
Notwithstanding the scientific evidence and widespread concern about climate change, insufficient action is being taken to mitigate it. The misperceived social norm that others do not care about climate change—conceptualized as pluralistic ignorance—and a lack of interpersonal communication about topics related to climate change are seen as possible inhibitors of climate action and pro-environmental behavior. However, empirical evidence on the relationship between pluralistic ignorance, climate change communication, and pro-environmental behavior is sparse.
We surveyed a representative Dutch sample (N = 926). Most participants talked about climate change-related topics with friends and family less than once per month or “occasionally”. Using one-sample t-tests we found empirical support for pluralistic ignorance, with participants underestimating levels of climate change importance, worry, and felt individual responsibility. Regression analyses showed that hearing others talk about climate change negatively predicted pluralistic ignorance, while talking more often with people who share one’s opinion positively predicted pluralistic ignorance. Contrary to our expectations, participants reporting higher levels of pluralistic ignorance engaged in more pro-environmental behavior. An additional investigation of a small sample of climate change communication experts (N = 56) furthermore demonstrated the existence of pluralistic ignorance in individuals talking frequently about climate change. 
Our findings suggest that most adults in the Netherlands talk about climate change infrequently and that engaging in these conversations is not necessarily linked to a more accurate picture of climate change concern levels. At the same time, pluralistic ignorance did not seem to be linked to the expected problematic collective bystander effect.

articles.title: Effectiveness and uptake of ROOM to grow: Results of a randomized controlled trial
articles.authors: Tajda Laure; Marilisa Boffo; Stefan Konigorski; Djameela Dulloo; Rutger Engels; Danielle Remmerswaal
articles.abstract:
Background
One in four university students face mental health challenges, underscoring the need for effective support systems. Within Erasmus University Rotterdam student wellbeing initiative, we developed a mobile intervention providing universal and targeted preventive support, focusing on emotion regulation to address common issues like depression and anxiety. This study reports the results of a superiority Randomized Controlled Trial testing the intervention impact and uptake.
Methods
Students (N = 202) were randomized to immediate ROOM app access (n = 101) or a 3-month waitlist control (n = 101); 171 (85%) completed the trial. Questionnaires were completed at baseline and weeks 3, 8, and 12, alongside continuous in-app activity logs. Primary outcomes were perceived stress and emotion regulation; secondary outcomes included depressive and anxiety symptoms, resilience, and objective and subjective engagement. Analyses followed an intent-to-treat approach, with exploratory subgroup analyses assessing differences by engagement level.
Results
The intervention showed no statistically significant impact on any outcomes. Subgroup analyses suggested that highly engaged users experienced reduced stress, while moderately engaged users showed improved resilience but elevated depressive symptoms. Engagement was initially high but declined sharply after week 1, stabilizing by week 6. More than half of participants still used the intervention at day 30. Breathing and relaxation exercises were favoured, whereas transfer-elements and self-assessments were used less than expected.
Conclusions
 ROOM showed limited overall impact but yielded benefits for highly engaged users. Future refinement should focus on identifying how much and what type of engagement is needed for the app to effectively support students.

articles.title: Transitions in Teachers’ Technology Utilization: Evidence from a Large-Scale, Longitudinal, and International Panel Study
articles.authors: Evi Konstantinidou; Ronny Scherer
articles.abstract: Teachers’ technology utilization varies substantially within and across educational systems, yet its longitudinal dynamics remain poorly understood. Using data from the International Computer and Information Literacy Study (ICILS) 2018-2020 Teacher Panel Study covering Denmark, Finland, and Uruguay, this study examined latent profiles of teachers’ ICT utilization, their stability and change over time, and associations with teacher characteristics. Profiles ranged from minimally engaged to comprehensive integrators, with intermediate profiles reflecting selective or functionally embedded ICT use. Two theoretically important profiles exhibited a paradoxical combination of positive ICT attitudes and low classroom practice, consistent with second-order barriers to technology integration. Profile stability varied cross-nationally, with Finland showing the greatest stability and Uruguay exhibiting a consolidation of profiles between occasions. ICT self-efficacy and collaborative ICT use were the most consistent predictors of lower-engagement profile membership. Our findings highlight cross-national heterogeneity in ICT utilization profiles and a persistent attitude-practice gap across educational systems. Effective professional development should target barriers preventing attitudinally committed teachers from enacting ICT in practice, with collaborative teaching cultures as a key enabling condition.

articles.title: Measurement of American adolescents’ experiences of online harms: A nationally representative survey
articles.authors: Angela Yuson Lee; Carmen Hui Jing Lim; Xun Liu; Kang-Xing Jin; Anneke Buffone; Ravi Iyer; Zachary M Rausch; Jonathan Haidt; Nathanael Fast; Jeffrey Hancock
articles.abstract:
Importance: Concerns about the risks adolescents face online are rising, but existing research on online harms typically examines limited outcomes, rarely allows for platform-specific comparison, and lacks practical standardized measurement that would be suitable for public health monitoring.
Objective: We introduce and implement a measurement approach estimating the prevalence of negative experiences among US adolescents online, offline, and by platform.
Design, Setting, and Participants: Cross-sectional, nationally representative, probability-based survey of US adolescents aged 13-17 conducted February-December 2025 through the Understanding America Study panel. Self-reported use of 20 online platforms (e.g. Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, TikTok, YouTube, Facetime, email etc) was assessed.
Main Outcomes and Measures: Self-reported prevalence of major risks and harms experienced in past 4 weeks, including harassment, sexual solicitation, and exposure to violent or self-harm content, as well as sleep disruption (measured online, offline, and by platform). Survey-weighted logistic regression identified associated factors.
Results: Weighted sample included 482 adolescents (mean age 15 years; 47.1% female; 65.1% White) from 47 US states. Across online and offline contexts, 44.3% witnessed harassment and 29.6% had experienced it directly in the past 4 weeks. Overall prevalence varied by gender and age: girls were more likely than boys to feel worse about themselves due to others' posts, messages, or communications (29.1% vs 15.5%), and older adolescents (ages 15-17) were more likely than younger adolescents (ages 13-14) to have witnessed discrimination against others (48.6% vs 38.5%). Online exposure exceeded offline exposure for numerous harms, including exposure to violent imagery (10.5% vs 1.6%), unwanted sexual images (13.5% vs 2.0%), and content encouraging self-harm (9.6% vs 5.1%). More than half (51.7%) reported sleep disruption due to technology use. Parental education, income, and parental controls were not significantly associated with online harm exposure (all P > .05). Platform differences in exposure to some harms were substantial, e.g., 42.8% of TikTok users and 28.3% of Instagram users attributed disrupted sleep to those platforms, compared with 11.3% of text-messaging users and 0.6% of email users.
Conclusions and Relevance: Negative online experiences are common among US adolescents and vary by platform, gender, and age. Establishing rigorous, multi-layered measurement approaches with platform-by-platform, social media versus other digital environments, and online versus offline comparisons is essential for transforming fragmented evidence into actionable data that can guide effective decisions by youth, parents, platforms and
regulators.

articles.title: Object perception encodes mutually exclusive possibilities
articles.authors: Gabor Brody; Peter Mazalik; Roman Feiman
articles.abstract: Human adults represent and reason about possibilities: multiple, mutually exclusive, non-actual ways the world could be. It is debated whether this is a late-developing, human-specific capacity and whether it requires natural language. We propose that instead of a unitary high-level capacity, multiple cognitive capacities might independently represent possibilities in different ways and with different properties. Our case study is object perception, a capacity shared with infants and non-human animals. Using methods that tap specifically into visual processes, across four experiments (N = 160), we found possibility representations in vision tasks that do not involve high-level reasoning or language. In Experiments 1 and 2, we found evidence for the visual encoding of possible object identities: When there were two possible ways that features might map onto visual objects, both possible mappings were primed. In Experiment 3, we found that visual feature-binding is a necessary precondition for such priming, ruling out low-level associative and high-level reasoning-based alternatives. Finally, in Experiment 4, we discovered that the visual system cannot encode one visual object possibly being at two possible locations. This pattern helps to explain a host of previous findings from the comparative and developmental literature and suggests that the unit of analysis for possibility representations should be the cognitive capacity rather than the agent.

articles.title: A test of the digital Give-a-Number task
articles.authors: Khuyen N. Le; Jingyi Liu; Daniel C. Hyde; David Barner
articles.abstract: For over 50 years, the Give-a-Number task has been used as a measure of children’s understanding of number words. Also, the knower level framework that results from the task has also featured prominently in theoretical proposals about children’s number acquisition and the development of number concepts. However, because different labs test children with different objects, trial structures, and numbers, the field lacks a standardized version of the task that can be used as a stable predictor across studies. In this study, we took a step toward standardization by asking whether a digital version of the task could replace the standard version. When individual children were tested with both a physical act-out version and a digital version of Give-a-Number, we found moderate to high concordance between the two versions. However, we also found that children tended to perform more accurately on the physical version, though this difference had little effect on knower level classification. We conclude that the digital version may replace the physical act-out task in many contexts, with minimal impact on classification validity.

articles.title: Humor: The Amusing Side of the Psychology of Art and Aesthetics
articles.authors: Paul Joseph Silvia; Vi Bui
articles.abstract: Humor was an interest of Berlyne’s new experimental aesthetics movement, but the psychology of aesthetics and humor have since drifted apart. To reunite them, we apply an aesthetics lens to some classic and modern findings in the psychology of humor, drawing parallels between humor and the broader psychology of aesthetics and the arts for aspects of the product (e.g., words, puns, and jokes), the experience (e.g., feeling amused, irritated, or embarrassed by jokes), and the audience (e.g., the massive individual differences in what people find funny). Its intrinsic interest aside, the psychology of humor reminds us of the sheer ubiquity of comic creative work and highlights the importance of amusing, playful, and lighthearted experiences of the arts.

articles.title: The Emperor's New Diagnoses: Psychedelics, Psychosis, and the Limits of Psychiatric Distinctions
articles.authors: Maja Willard
articles.abstract: Psychedelic and psychotic experiences are institutionally treated as belonging to different orders — yet in phenomenology and in cultural interpretation, they often converge. The present paper examines that gap. It sets out the similarities and differences between the two — chiefly the predominance of visual rather than auditory hallucinations in psychedelic experience, the role of insight, the influence of context in both, and the asymmetry in clinical response. It does not adjudicate the causal question of whether psychedelics can induce psychosis; the clinical, population, and naturalistic literature answer different questions, and none of them closes the case. I argue, rather, that psychosis research has much to gain from attending to features long central in psychedelic science: the significance of subjective experience and meaning-making, the influence of set and setting, and the moral commitment to impartial care. The aim is narrow — to mark what the available comparison does and does not warrant — but the implications, I suggest, bear on how psychiatry frames, studies, and responds to altered states more broadly.

articles.title: Executive Functioning as a Developmental Scaffold for Emotion Regulation: Longitudinal Neural and Behavioral Associations Across Adolescence
articles.authors: Kayleigh Esparza; Shannon O'Conner; Ava J. White; Dalia El-Shafie; Martin P Paulus; Robin Aupperle
articles.abstract: Adolescence is a critical developmental period marked by rapid changes in limbic and prefrontal regions, alongside gains in executive functioning and the development of emotion regulation strategies. Prior work suggests reciprocal relationships between executive function and emotion regulation, with each being important for supporting optimal developmental outcomes. However, further understanding the longitudinal relationships and underlying neural mechanisms is important for informing the timing and targets of intervention approaches. The present study utilized longitudinal data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (N = 11,868) to examine these relationships from approximately ages 9–16. Exploratory factor analyses were conducted using fMRI data from the emotional N-back task, identifying three factors each for the 2-vs.-0-back and Faces-vs.-Places contrasts, corresponding to Sensorimotor-Insular, Central Executive, and Visual-Occipital networks. Cross-lagged panel models were used to test prospective associations amongst these network factors, scores on the emotion regulation questionnaire, and an NIH Toolbox executive function factor score across time. Results indicated that the Central Executive factor of the 2-vs.-0-back contrast predicted subsequent executive functioning across all time points, with some evidence of bidirectional associations in preadolescence. Moreover, executive functioning at ages 13–14 positively predicted both cognitive reappraisal and emotion suppression at ages 14–15, while neither emotion regulation strategy predicted later executive functioning. These findings highlight the dynamic and reciprocal nature of neural and behavioral development across adolescence and support the importance of executive network development for supporting cognitive function, and for executive functioning as an important developmental scaffold for emotion regulation strategy use.

articles.title: Exploratory Validation of the Black Female Athlete Anxiety Scale
articles.authors: Luka Ojemaye; Joyce Olushola Ogunrinde; Billy Hawkins; Micheal Cottingham; Jie Zhang
articles.abstract:
Black female collegiate athletes (BFCAs) navigate a distinct set of stressors at the intersection of race, gender, and athletic participation that may contribute to elevated levels of anxiety. Although awareness of mental health concerns among collegiate athletes has increased, existing anxiety assessment tools often lack sensitivity to the intersectional experiences shaping the lives of BFCAs. Consequently, these instruments may fail to capture the culturally specific stressors that influence anxiety within this population. The present study addressed this gap by conducting a preliminary validation of the Black Female Athlete Anxiety Scale (BFAAS), a culturally responsive instrument designed to assess anxiety-inducing stressors experienced by BFCAs.
A total of 116 NCAA Division I BFCAs completed the newly developed 21-item Black Female Athlete Anxiety Scale (BFAAS). Exploratory factor analysis (EFA) was conducted to examine the instrument’s underlying factor structure. Results identified a three-factor model, with factors labeled Navigating Marginalization, Performance Pressure, and Athletic Time Constraints. Additional analyses, including analysis of variance (ANOVA) and independent-samples t tests, were conducted to explore group-level differences in anxiety-related stressors within the sample. Internal consistency estimates demonstrated strong reliability across factors (Cronbach’s α > .80).
These findings provided preliminary evidence supporting the BFAAS as a reliable and culturally responsive measure of anxiety-inducing stressors among BFCAs. The scale offers practical utility for improving mental health assessment in collegiate sport contexts and may inform culturally responsive interventions, practitioner training, and policy initiatives aimed at supporting the anxiety and well-being of BFCAs.

articles.title: Engagement-centered demonstrations in large enrollment introductory psychology classes: effects on understanding, retention, and student engagement
articles.authors: Amanda MacIntyre; Christine Ausman; Daniel Blustein
articles.abstract:
Background. Large enrollment introductory psychology courses often struggle with low student engagement, which can hinder learning outcomes. 

Objective. This study evaluated the effectiveness of interactive demonstrations on student engagement, short-term understanding, and long-term retention of key concepts in a large introductory course.

Method. Eight demonstrations were developed and delivered quasi-experimentally across two course sections (n = 276). Student understanding was assessed via in-class polling, and retention was measured using the same questions on the final exam. Engagement was evaluated using the Classroom Observation Protocol for Undergraduate STEM.

Results. Demonstrations significantly increased student engagement; however, linear mixed-effects models revealed no statistically significant effect on understanding or retention. 

Conclusion. Structured demonstrations increase engagement but have limited effects on test scores. Their learning effectiveness may depend on concept difficulty. 

Teaching Implications. Psychology instructors should consider incorporating interactive demonstrations into their teaching to foster engagement. Further research is warranted to better understand factors that can improve associated learning outcomes, including content complexity and demonstration style.

articles.title: Polarity and the speed of scalar implicature processing
articles.authors: Michael Vrazitulis; J E Loy; Vera Demberg
articles.abstract: Scalar implicatures (SIs) such as the inference from ‘some’ to ‘some but not all’ are known to vary in processing cost. According to the Polarity Hypothesis (van Tiel et al., 2019; van Tiel & Pankratz, 2021), this cost depends on the polarity of the scalar term: SIs from positively polar expressions (e.g., ‘some’ implicating ‘some but not all’) are expected to be slower to compute than literal interpretations, while the reverse should hold for SIs from negatively polar expressions, sometimes referred to as indirect SIs (e.g., ‘not all’ implicating ‘not all but some’). We tested this prediction in a sentence–picture verification experiment with 538 English-speaking participants. Each participant judged whether sentences containing weak or strong scalar terms matched a corresponding picture, allowing us to measure response times as an index of processing effort. Our results showed no clear evidence for the Polarity Hypothesis; specifically, we found it to not generalise across the different scales included in our experiment. We additionally conducted a meta-analysis synthesising all existing evidence so far—this meta-analysis shows that, overall, the Polarity Hypothesis can be vindicated. Surprisingly, our results do also not clearly validate the traditional assumption of a B&N effect (Bott & Noveck, 2004), i.e., a general polarity-independent processing slowdown under SIs: a subsequent analysis including trial order revealed that the B&N effect was present in early trials of our study, whereas later trials do not show the effect. Overall, we conclude that polarity may influence the speed of scalar implicature processing, but that this effect is not robust across all types of scales.

articles.title: Can Multi-Task Eye-Movement Profiles Identify People Across Recording Blocks?
articles.authors: Marian Sauter
articles.abstract:
Eye-movement biometrics are often evaluated in narrowly defined tasks, leaving open the
question of whether a broader behavioral profile can identify people more robustly across
time. We address this question by combining features from multiple eye-tracking tasks,
including grid-based viewing, smooth pursuit, microsaccades, blinks, free viewing, and
pupil responses, into one oculomotor profile per participant and recording block. We then
test whether a held-out block can be matched back to the correct participant. All three
models we evaluated identified people far above chance: a family-weighted cosine fusion
model, which learns cross-validated weights for each task family in the cross-session
benchmark, reached the numerically highest top-1 accuracy in every cohort (0.88 in the
primary Neon-only cohort and 0.79 in the EyeLink dual-setup cohort), but a subject-level
paired bootstrap showed that its advantage over a simpler unweighted cosine baseline ($0.80$
Neon; 0.71 EyeLink) was not statistically distinguishable. Feature analyses showed that
useful identity information was distributed across multiple task families---most strongly
grid and smooth-pursuit features---rather than being driven by a single measure. Because the
Neon-only and dual-setup Neon profiles come from different participant samples, a cohort
classifier separated them almost perfectly (AUC = 0.99), reflecting a combination of
recording-context and between-sample differences. These findings indicate that multi-task
oculomotor fingerprinting is feasible, but that careful attention to acquisition conditions
and to the limits of small-sample model comparisons is essential when interpreting biometric
performance.

articles.title: The Arousal-Space Metaphor: Evidence from Spatial Mapping in a 3D Volumetric Environment
articles.authors: Josef Kundrát; Karel Rečka; Eric Hiris; Radim Peša; Fernando Marmolejo-Ramos; Aleš Neusar; Tereza Benešová
articles.abstract: Spatial metaphors linking valence to physical location—such as feeling up or being down—have been extensively documented. However, valence and arousal co-vary systematically, leaving open the question of whether previously reported vertical-space effects reflect valence, arousal, or both. The present work addresses this question by having participants positioned Czech affective adjectives in a three-dimensional virtual space. Stimuli were selected via Shannon entropy to orthogonally represent valence and arousal, minimizing their usual correlation. Across three studies (data collected in 2025 and 2026), we examined spatial placement (Studies 1–2) and perceptual validation of adjective location (Study 3). Linear mixed-effects models revealed that both valence and arousal independently predicted vertical placement (Y-axis), with positive valence or high arousal being placed higher. Valence consistently predicted depth placement (Z-axis), with positive adjectives placed closer to the body and negative adjectives further away, consistent with approach-avoidance frameworks. No consistent effects emerged on the horizontal axis. Study 3 confirmed perceptual validity: congruent pairings of valence and arousal the strongest agreement judgments (e.g., OR = 122.41 for positive, high-arousal adjectives placed high), while incongruent pairings of valence and arousal (e.g., positive, low-arousal adjectives) produced uncertainty about the correct location of these adjectives. These findings demonstrate that arousal constitutes an independent contributor to vertical spatial-affective mapping—a dimension largely overlooked in prior work due to its natural covariation with valence—and that the depth axis operates primarily as a valence domain. Substantial individual differences in the application of affective-spatial metaphors were observed across all studies.

articles.title: Assessing the efficacy of crowdsourced fact-checking for TikTok videos
articles.authors: Sze Yuh Nina Wang; Jennifer Nancy Lee Allen; Hause Lin; Cameron Martel; David Gertler Rand; Gordon Pennycook
articles.abstract: Social media companies are increasingly shifting towards crowdsourced fact-checking approaches, which are more scalable than solely relying on professional fact-checkers. While past work suggests these approaches are effective for news headlines, it is unclear whether they are effective for short-form video content like TikToks, which are increasingly prevalent as a source of news/information. We compare the accuracy ratings of three professional fact-checkers to those of layperson crowds on three politicized topics: the Ukraine/Russia conflict, climate change, and abortion. Professional fact-checkers showed reasonable agreement on TikToks about the Ukraine/Russia conflict and abortion, but poor agreement on TikToks about climate change. Poorer performance by Republican laypeople meant that politically-balanced crowds performed as well as professional fact-checkers on Ukraine/Russia TikToks, while only Democrat-only crowds performed as well as professionals on abortion TikToks. A classifier using layperson ratings to predict professionals’ ratings performed well on TikToks where professionals’ ratings were unanimous, suggesting that crowdsourcing may be effective for unambiguously true or false TikToks.

articles.title: A Two-Stage Scaffolding Model of Color and Luminance Development: Retinal Waves, Global Impoverishment, and Luminance-Grounded Object Representations
articles.authors: David Henry Peterzell
articles.abstract: Vogelsang et al. (2024) reported that individuals who gain sight late in life after congenital blindness from dense bilateral cataracts rely disproportionately on color cues for object recognition. They proposed that this reflects the adaptive value of neonatal color immaturity: because typical infants experience a period of degraded chromatic input, they build object representations grounded in luminance rather than color. The companion paper (Peterzell, 2026) showed this color-specific account is untenable, because the same photoreceptor immaturity that degrades chromatic input equally degrades luminance and spatial processing, leaving no developmental window in which luminance-first representations can form while color is specifically suppressed. The present paper develops a positive alternative. I propose a two-stage scaffolding model: a prenatal stage in which retinal waves, achromatic by physical necessity, establish the foundational spatial and luminance architecture of the visual system before any visual experience occurs; and a postnatal stage in which the globally degraded neonatal visual signal forces the first object representations to be built on luminance structure, the only dimension with sufficient fidelity under maximal photoreceptor immaturity. The late-sighted Prakash patients retain the prenatal structural scaffold (their intact retinas should have generated normal retinal waves) but lack the postnatal experiential scaffold (the luminance-grounded representational architecture normally built through years of globally impoverished visual experience). The accounts make different predictions: whereas Vogelsang et al. predict color-specific fragility, the scaffolding account predicts generalized fragility to any form of image degradation, a distinction testable on the existing Vogelsang et al. computational architecture without new patient data.

articles.title: Mismatch Between Routine Care and Personalized Selection of Low-Intensity Psychological Interventions Using Causal Machine Learning
articles.authors: Christian A. Webb; Jaime Delgadillo
articles.abstract:
Importance: Low-intensity interventions can help to widen access to treatment for common mental disorders.

Objective: To develop a data-driven model for personalized assignment to one of three available low intensity interventions, and to evaluate the clinical outcomes of model-matched cases.

Design: Analysis of electronic health records for an observational cohort. A multi-arm causal forest estimated conditional treatment effects in a training sample of early cases (~70%) and evaluated the potential benefit of personalized treatment selection in a hold-out test sample of cases treated at a later date (~30%).

Setting: Sixteen NHS Talking Therapies services offering low intensity psychological interventions in England.

Participants: N= 89,467 patients receiving one of three interventions.

Interventions: cCBT (computerized CBT), iGSH (individual guided self-help), and gGSH (group guided self-help).

Main Outcomes and Measures: Reliable recovery on psychometric measures of depression (PHQ-9) and anxiety (GAD-7). Personalized intervention benefit (PIB) indexed recommendation strength as the predicted advantage of the recommended treatment over alternatives.

Results: Observed assignment in the test sample was highly concentrated in iGSH (87.9%), with 9.0% receiving gGSH and 3.1% cCBT. The causal forest recommended cCBT for 64.1%, iGSH for 33.5%, and gGSH for 2.4%. Only 30.7% received their model-recommended treatment. Overall reliable recovery was 41.1%. Receiving the recommended treatment was associated with higher reliable recovery, with the benefit increasing across PIB quartiles: Q1 = 0.5%, Q2 = 2.5%, Q3 = 6.8%, Q4 = 11.3% (p < .001). Predictors of observed assignment and model-recommended treatment modestly overlapped (Spearman ρ = 0.36).

Conclusions and Relevance: Causal forest recommendations diverged substantially from observed care, with current practice markedly underutilizing cCBT. Personalized treatment selection could improve reliable recovery rates by up to 11%.

articles.title: Bayesian Evidence Synthesis under Heterogeneity
articles.authors: Björn Alfons Edmar; Duco Veen; Irene Klugkist
articles.abstract: Bayesian evidence synthesis (BES) provides a way of synthesising research without pooling effects, allowing for quantitative synthesise procedures when the more traditional common-effects and random-effects models cannot be used. BES can be applied through the multiplication of independent Bayes factors assessing a common theory, quantifying the evidence for same informative hypothesis given all observed studies. This paper evaluates the behaviour of BES under varying levels of heterogeneity, and extends the methodological framework to incorporate model space inference (MSI) for more nuanced synthesis results. We do this through simulation of a standard mean difference scenario, examining how various hypothesis evaluations complement one another and provide independent information that can mitigate the potential for false positive inferences. Our results depict the high sensitivity and specificity of BES, and illustrate how heterogeneity affects BES. Our simulation results provide guidance for real world application of BES, discussing what aspects of the method to keep in mind when synthesising evidence comparing two means. In addition to this, we present an empirical example further detailing the application of the method.

articles.title: Ethnoracial and Linguistic Diversity in Treatment Research for Primary Progressive Aphasia: A Systematic Review
articles.authors: Rylee Catherine Manning; Wenfu Bao; Maria Fernanda Quiñones Vieta; Lucas Ansín Gómez; Jo-Ali Acosta; Jessica de Leon; Júlia Filella Mercè; Estefanía García Hernandez; Karla P. Gutiérrez Sánchez; Jan C. Holst Chaires
articles.abstract:
Purpose: This systematic review sought to characterize the ethnoracial and linguistic representation of participants in the nonpharmacological intervention literature for primary progressive aphasia (PPA) and primary progressive apraxia of speech (PPAOS). In addition, we aimed to identify which social determinants of health (SDOH) factors are most frequently reported across studies. 

Method: We performed a comprehensive search across six electronic databases: OVID Medline, OVID PsycINFO, EBSCO Medline, EBSCO CINAHL Plus, Embase, and Web of Science, as well as unpublished literature. We identified 149 original studies that administered neuromodulation and/or speech-language interventions to individuals with PPA or PPAOS and reported communication outcomes relative to a baseline or control group. From these studies, we extracted data on participants’ race, ethnicity, spoken languages, occupation, years of education, and other relevant sociodemographic variables.

Results: Ethnoracial and linguistic diversity in the current PPA literature is limited and underrepresented. Among studies that reported the extracted variables, the majority of participants were identified as non-Hispanic White, and most studies were conducted in the United States. English was the most commonly reported spoken language and was frequently used as an inclusion criterion. Regarding SDOH, participants tend to have medium-high education and hold higher skill-level occupations. Very few studies reported retirement status and whether retirement was due to symptom onset.

Conclusions: Current intervention research for PPA and PPAOS lacks ethnoracial and linguistic diversity.  This underrepresentation is further compounded by limited reporting of factors related to SDOH. The concentration of research in homogenous populations limits the generalizability of current findings and restricts their relevance for managing PPA and PPAOS across diverse demographic, cultural, linguistic, and socioeconomic contexts. Future research on treatment should not only ensure comprehensive reporting of participant characteristics but also actively expand the inclusivity of recruitment practices.

articles.title: Intersecting Identities and Campus Well-Being: Mental Health, Substance Use, and Service Barriers Among Racial/Ethnic and Sexual/Gender Minoritized Students at a Canadian University
articles.authors: Victoria Doan; Erin Courtice; Tara Raessi; Sarah Dermody
articles.abstract: Sexual and gender minoritized (SGM) and racial and ethnic minoritized (REM) and university students face unique minority stressors that increase risk of mental health problems and substance use. Yet, little research examines how intersecting identities influence barriers to accessing campus services. We investigated associations between these identities - including their intersections - and mental health, substance use, and obstacles to utilizing campus-based services. We conducted an online survey with 1,009 undergraduate students (Mage= 20.6, SD= 5.1) at a Canadian university about mental health symptoms and substance use, as well as experiences with and barriers to accessing campus mental health and substance use services. Regression analyses revealed few differences in mental health severity and barriers to accessing campus services between most REM subgroups and white students. Most REM subgroups reported lower rates of substance use, greater awareness of campus services, and found services more helpful relative to white students. Sexually minoritized students reported greater severity of mental health symptoms and more barriers to accessing campus services relative to heterosexual students. Interactions between SGM and REM identities did not correspond with differing outcomes. Our findings highlight the need for tailored campus services that address unique challenges faced by diverse minoritized student populations.

articles.title: Bidirectional associations between executive function and social behavior across childhood: A registered report using a nationally representative sample.
articles.authors: Blake Glatley; Andrew David Ribner; Sammy F. Ahmed
articles.abstract: Executive function (EF) refers to a set of cognitive skills involved in deliberate, goal-oriented behavior. Although research has established links between children’s EF and aspects of their social skills, it remains unclear how EF skills are related to specific social behaviors, such as prosociality and social aggression. Additionally, the co-development of EF and social behavior during childhood is less understood. Therefore, the current registered report aimed to examine bidirectional associations between two EF skills - working memory and cognitive flexibility - and children's prosocial behavior and social aggression using the Early Childhood Longitudinal Study - Kindergarten Class of 2010-11 (ECLS-K:2011; N = 18,174). Based on Sameroff’s Transactional Model of Human Development, we expected that EF and social behavior would be bidirectionally related from third-to-fifth grade (8 to 11 years old). We hypothesized that both working memory and cognitive flexibility would be positively associated with later prosocial behavior and negatively associated with later social aggression. Furthermore, we hypothesized that prosocial behavior would be positively associated with later EF skills and that social aggression would be negatively associated with later EF skills. Our results indicated that, at the between-person level, EF skills were positively associated with prosocial behavior and only working memory was negatively associated with social aggression. At the within-person level, we found no bidirectional associations between EF skills and social behaviors. However, we observed a small number of concurrent correlations between EF skills and social behaviors, along with a limited set of cross-lagged associations. Findings are discussed in the context of children’s social development.

articles.title: Signatures proposed to index perceptual effects emerge in a purely cognitive task
articles.authors: Sixing Chen; Michaela Bocheva; Dobromir Rahnev
articles.abstract: A central question in many studies on perception and consciousness is whether the effects of a given manipulation are perceptual or cognitive. Typically, studies seek to find evidence that the raw sensory experience has been altered—that is, a manipulation produces perceptual rather than cognitive effects. Here, we test two specific proposals for behavioral signatures that could allow this type of inference. First, a long-standing proposition is that different parameters of the drift–diffusion model (DDM) map onto different effects, such that a drift-rate bias indicates a perceptual effect, whereas a starting point shift indicates a cognitive effect. Second, a newer proposal holds that, when plotted as a function of a sensory feature, changes in the peak of the distributions for confidence and reaction time (RT) imply that a given manipulation’s effects are perceptual. Here, we test both proposals. We designed a purely cognitive task where no effects can be perceptual and examined whether the proposed signatures of perceptual effects occur in this task. Participants viewed numbers generated by two distributions and indicated which distribution the numbers likely came from. In separate conditions we manipulated prior beliefs and rewards related to each distribution. We found that both manipulations elicited the same signatures previously proposed as indicative of purely perceptual effects. Our results demonstrate that individual DDM parameters and the peaks of confidence and RT distributions in isolation are insufficient to distinguish between perceptual and cognitive effects.

articles.title: Shared neural representations of barriers to agents’ actions and objects’ motions
articles.authors: Minjae J. Kim; Samuel Maione; Shari Liu
articles.abstract: In the human brain, distinct regions preferentially respond to the actions and interactions of animate agents, and the motions and interactions of inanimate objects. Yet, evidence across the cognitive sciences suggests that we process animate agents fundamentally as physical bodies (Liu et al., 2025). This suggestion predicts that processing of agents and processing of objects depend in part on common neural substrates. Here, we used functional brain imaging to test this prediction. Our case study concerned a fundamental, developmentally early-emerging intuition that because agents and objects are solid bodies, their motions are constrained by physical barriers (Gergely & Csibra, 2003; Saxe et al., 2006; Spelke et al., 1992). Participants (N = 28) were scanned using functional magnetic resonance imaging while they watched videos of agents navigating or objects moving in environments containing physical barriers, and videos containing similar physical features that did not block agents’ or objects’ paths. We report a domain-invariant code for barriers to agents’ actions and objects’ motions in regions associated with physical reasoning and multiple demand (superior parietal lobule), object processing (lateral occipital complex), and scene processing (occipital place area, parahippocampal place area, and retrosplenial cortex). Our results generalized across two distinct tasks, and are not explained by differences in luminance, motion energy, or the number of objects in the stimuli. These results suggest that brain regions specialized for reasoning about physical dynamics and spatial layouts support our capacity to make sense of agents, raising questions about interactions more broadly between social, physical and spatial cognition.

articles.title: ManyBabies 6: A Multi-Lab Investigation of Neonatal and Early Imitation
articles.authors: Andrew N. Meltzoff; Sumeet Farwaha; Virginia Slaughter; Elizabeth Ann Simpson; Rechele Brooks; Mark Nielsen; Heidi A Baumgartner; Laurie Bayet; Silvia Benavides-Varela; Veronica Boyce
articles.abstract: Classic theory in developmental science holds that the imitation of facial gestures is a late developing capacity in human infancy. Meltzoff and Moore (1977) reported that 12- to 21-day-old infants imitate facial and manual gestures, theorizing that young infants can recognize cross-modal correspondences between observed and performed actions and suggesting that early imitation plays a role in social learning from other people. Subsequent studies yielded mixed findings and alternative interpretations have been proposed. Thus, whether young infants can imitate adult gestures remains a debated question. As part of the ManyBabies consortium, a large-scale collaborative network in developmental science, we tested whether very young infants (14–28 and 40–54 days old) demonstrate facial imitation using a protocol that was standardized for use across multiple independent laboratories. In a within-subjects design, infants saw mouth-opening and tongue-protrusion gestures in randomized order, presented by the same experimenter at the same distance and rate; responses were videorecorded for later behavioral scoring. [Insert study results and interpretations here.]

articles.title: Graded gender perceptions in personal names predict difficulty in reference resolution
articles.authors: Hassan Khan; Yoonjung Kang; Dave Kush
articles.abstract: We conducted two experiments to better understand how comprehenders use probabilistic knowledge of the associations between genders and personal names when inferring the gender of new referents during reading. Personal names are, surprisingly, under-investigated in studies on gender inference compared to role nouns (e.g., nurse) despite being strongly connected to gender. In a self-paced reading task involving variably gender biased names and gendered reflexives, we observe a gender mismatch effect proportional to the incongruence between a name’s perceived gender and that of the reflexive. In a novel variant of the Maze task involving forced ambiguity resolution between two gendered reflexives, we find decisions track the perceived gender of names and decisions take longer with more ambiguous names. We interpret the results to support a model in which comprehenders make graded, not categorical, inferences about gender during incremental comprehension, contrary to previous proposals.

articles.title: Controlled real-world object generation for visual memory experimentation and theory-building
articles.authors: Jiachen Zou; Chi Zhang; Deng Haotian; Quanying Liu; Chen Wei; Maria Martinovna Robinson
articles.abstract:
Real-world objects are visually rich and semantically structured, but due to this complexity, theories of visual memory are generally tested with more tractable stimulus spaces, such as color. To address the challenge of studying real-world objects with high experimental control,
we used a novel generative artificial intelligence (AI) Concept-based Controllable Generation (CoCoG) model, grounded in human similarity judgments, to create systematically structured object spaces. We then combined CoCoG-derived embeddings with a computational model of memory to predict memory error distributions. Across seven online visual-working-memory studies (N = 1,019), we found that these stimuli can be used to investigate established phenomena, such object-state versus -exemplar memory, and new phenomena, specifically memory for novel object morphs. These results build on recent work that bridges generative AI with cognitive modeling and demonstrate how architectures like CoCoG can be used as a methodological and theory-constraining tool for systematically studying real-world visual cognition.

articles.title: Neurodiversity and learning in context: Development and validation of a self-report measure of engagement with learning activities (MELA)
articles.authors: Julie Bailey; Sara Baker
articles.abstract: While neurodivergent students’ learning engagement is recognised as important, there is not yet a suitable measure of engagement with learning activities to support research into inclusive teaching. This paper outlines the development and validation of a new self-report instrument (MELA: Measure of Engagement with Learning Activities) intended for exploring neurodivergent students’ engagement. In Phase 1, existing measures were adapted and presented to autistic undergraduates (n = 33) and experts (n = 5). The measure was then refined following interviews with autistic students (n = 7) and presented in Phase 2 to autistic (n = 53) and non-autistic (n = 121) undergraduates to test for convergent validity through comparison against exiting measures. In Phase 3, the measure’s reliability and performance were examined. MELA demonstrated strong validity and therefore provides a robust research instrument for exploring students’ learning engagement, making it valuable for inclusion research supporting improvements to teaching in higher education.

articles.title: Choose your own PAS? Quantitative and Qualitative Validity Evidence for the Perceptual Awareness Scale
articles.authors: Alicia Franco-Martínez; Alicia Ferrer-Mendieta; Carmen Peiro Lanchares; Miguel A. Vadillo; Francisco Garre-Frutos
articles.abstract: The most preferred visibility measure in unconscious processing studies is the Perceptual Awareness Scale (PAS). Since it was originally proposed as a methodological approach, no PAS labels, instructions or display are currently official. Authors tend to adapt this scale to their experimental tasks often without specifying their PAS properties or without providing adequate justification for the modifications. In this work, we tested how “choosing your own PAS” affects response-process validity both quantitatively and qualitatively, in the same  unconscious working memory (WM) task as in Franco-Martínez et al. (in press) replication. In a preregistered 2×2×2 between-subjects design (N = 176), we manipulated: PAS Version (Original vs New labels, updated based on pilot participants’ reports), Examples (With brief demonstrations of PAS = 4 and PAS = 1 trials vs Without them), and Display (Scale&Text shown onscreen trial-by-trial vs ScaleOnly). In a post-experiment PAS questionnaire participants were asked to qualitatively report how they recalled and interpreted the scale.
