The unconscious WM effect and its reliability were robust under the Version and Examples manipulations, but presenting the New Version and/or With Examples increased the proportion of valid trials, enhancing statistical power. Although the unconscious WM effect increased in the ScaleOnly Display, the qualitative evidence suggests participants in this condition tend to forget the PAS labels and ultimately use them differently from intended. Finally, we provide practical recommendations for the use of PAS and a post-experiment PAS questionnaire to evaluate its validity.

articles.title: Partisans update their contentious political views in the face of contradictory facts and evidence
articles.authors: Sze Yuh Nina Wang; Thomas H Costello; David Gertler Rand; Gordon Pennycook
articles.abstract: It is commonly held that politically motivated reasoning causes partisans to reject information that counters their views, driving political polarization and public disagreement about the facts. We challenge this view across three experiments (N=2,921), where Large Language Models are used to provide partisans with strong evidence-based arguments that directly contradict their previously stated positions about democracy, corruption, and government funding. In contrast to predictions from theories of motivated reasoning, we found that these conversations were effective at shifting Republicans’ opinions about whether President Trump is undermining democracy (Study 1, d=.39), engaging in corrupt actions (Study 2, d=.71), or defunding programs that people support (Study 3, d=.70). These conversations also decreased overall support for Trump (d’s=0.37-0.46). Partisans updated their views despite being directly confronted about having contradictory positions on political topics.

articles.title: Adaptive search promotes asymmetrical learning
articles.authors: Samuel David Jones; Paul Rauwolf
articles.abstract: Recent theoretical work has argued that learning trajectories are shaped by adaptive search and satisficing. The central idea of this work is that the value of an information source (i.e., its utility or valence) modulates engagement, sampling density, and encoding fidelity, providing a behavioural pathway to variation in learning outcomes. The current manuscript reports the first explicit empirical test of this idea in adults using a modified version of a multi-step decision-making paradigm known as 'Mouselab'. Across two experiments and six sub-tasks, N=101 participants searched a decision tree at a cost until settling on and executing a high-value policy. While Experiment 1 involved a non-symbolic decision space, Experiment 2 incorporated symbolic cues that allowed us to determine the extent to which associative learning is sensitive to underlying value structure. We found that value-sensitive adaptive search promoted rank-dependent learning asymmetries at test – estimates for the value of the best symbol were near veridical, while those for low-value symbols were at chance. In addition, we found that (i) informative symbolic cues shifted search from balanced exploration (breadth ≈ depth) towards depth-first exploitation; (ii) cue-guided search developed rapidly, with high-value symbol exploitation emerging within a handful of trials; (iii) search evidenced a win-stay tendency and was sensitive to cue valence, with individuals quickly pruning negatively valued branches; (iv) raising cost constrained search volume but preserved its depth-first, cue-sensitive character; and (v) observed individual differences in search behaviour were not predicted by common measures of cognitive function. These results provide new insight into how adaptive search and satisficing shape learning, supporting an emerging theory of the behavioural origins of individual differences in human development.

articles.title: Community Notes as debunking intervention: Correction design and topic context determine effectiveness
articles.authors: Nicole Antes; Martina Gierlich; Anastasia Kozyreva; Nadia Said
articles.abstract: Crowdsourced on-platform corrections, such as Community Notes, have gained popularity as a scalable response to misinformation. Yet it remains unclear whether they function as effective debunking interventions. This study investigates whether corrections presented in the forms of Community Notes reduce continued influence of misinformation and whether their effectiveness varies across neutral and socially divisive content. In an online experiment, 542 adults from a quota-based German sample viewed simulated social media posts containing misinformation. The misinformation was shown again with a Community Note in one of three forms: no correction, a simple retraction, or a retraction with an alternative explanation. For neutral content, both corrective notes reduced reliance on misinformation, with explanatory notes performing best. For socially divisive content, Community Notes reduced continued influence only when providing an alternative explanation. These findings suggest that Community Notes can serve as an effective debunking intervention, but only under specific design and context conditions.

articles.title: Polarity and the speed of scalar implicature processing
articles.authors: Michael Vrazitulis; J E Loy; Vera Demberg
articles.abstract: Scalar implicatures (SIs) such as the inference from ‘some’ to ‘some but not all’ are known to vary in processing cost. According to the Polarity Hypothesis (van Tiel et al., 2019; van Tiel & Pankratz, 2021), this cost depends on the polarity of the scalar term: SIs from positively polar expressions (e.g., ‘some’ implicating ‘some but not all’) are expected to be slower to compute than literal interpretations, while the reverse should hold for SIs from negatively polar expressions, sometimes referred to as indirect SIs (e.g., ‘not all’ implicating ‘not all but some’). We tested this prediction in a sentence–picture verification experiment with 538 English-speaking participants. Each participant judged whether sentences containing weak or strong scalar terms matched a corresponding picture, allowing us to measure response times as an index of processing effort. Our results showed no clear evidence for the Polarity Hypothesis; specifically, we found it to not generalise across the different scales included in our experiment. We additionally conducted a meta-analysis synthesising all existing evidence so far—this meta-analysis shows that, overall, the Polarity Hypothesis can be vindicated. Surprisingly, our results do also not clearly validate the traditional assumption of a B&N effect (Bott & Noveck, 2004), i.e., a general polarity-independent processing slowdown under SIs: a subsequent analysis including trial order revealed that the B&N effect was present in early trials of our study, whereas later trials do not show the effect. Overall, we conclude that polarity may influence the speed of scalar implicature processing, but that this effect is not robust across all types of scales.

articles.title: The mental health emergency of heatwaves: Implications for Australian policy, clinical practice and social support
articles.authors: Hasini Gunasiri; Sunny N Nguyen; Caroline X. Gao; Jana Menssink; David Baker; Cybele Dey; Rebecca Patrick; Neerja Singh; Samantha Julia Legaspi Eala; Fiona Charlson
articles.abstract: As climate change drives more frequent and severe heatwaves, its impacts on population mental health are becoming increasingly evident. Extreme heat is associated with increased psychological distress, aggression, suicide, and mental health-related service use. The risks are further heightened among people with pre-existing mental illness, due to physiological, social, and medication-related factors. Drawing on a rapid survey of Australian state and national heat-health policies, this perspective highlights the gap in integrating mental health into heatwave responses and outlines actionable recommendations to embed mental health within preparedness and response policies, clinical care, and community-based strategies in a warming climate.

articles.title: Mental Well-Being and Stress in Farming Communities in the UK and Canada: The Roles of Personal Vulnerability and Event Load
articles.authors: Sarah Nyczaj Kyle; Emma Barkus; Andria Jones; Stephen Dunne
articles.abstract:
Objective: Farmers, their families, and employees face daily stressors that adversely affect their mental well-being. To improve understanding of stress and mental well-being in farming populations, this study examines this relationship in the United Kingdom (UK) and Canada. 
Method: Using an online cross-sectional survey, 569 adults from farming communities (37% male, 63% female; Mage = 47.57, SD = 12.55) completed measures of stress event load (EL), personal vulnerability to stress (PV), and subjective mental well-being. Data were analysed using analysis of variance and hierarchical multiple regression, with simple-slopes analysis. 
Results: Farming community members who reported higher PV reported significantly lower well-being, regardless of whether EL was low or high. In regression, when considered together, PV eliminated the predictive power of EL, highlighting the importance of PV. Although a significant interaction was observed, after Bonferroni correction, significance remained only for those with high PV, where well-being among farming community members was substantially lower than at low or average PV, across all levels of EL. While UK participants reported significantly lower mental well-being than Canadian participants, mental well-being was low in both countries at levels that necessitate support. 
Conclusion: While studies have traditionally focused on farming-specific causes of stress, personal vulnerability was a stronger determinant of mental well-being than stress event load across the two countries. Interventions targeting the psychological mechanisms that underpin stress appraisal will therefore help protect mental well-being in farming communities. Future research must utilise longitudinal measurements to explore the psychological resources of farming community members worldwide.

articles.title: People Overestimate Support for “Cancellation”
articles.authors: Jakob Kasper; Randy T. Lee; Jabin Binnendyk; Gordon Pennycook; Ovul Sezer
articles.abstract: Social exclusion has long served as a mechanism of norm enforcement. However, in contemporary discourse, there is a common narrative about “cancel culture” that implies an overextension of social exclusion – often driven by social media. Given the ambiguity surrounding what cancellation entails and how often it occurs, people may struggle to accurately judge their own likelihood of being cancelled for expressing controversial views. Across two preregistered studies (NS1 = 753, NS2 = 753), we examined judgments of cancellation using naturalistic statements that participants (N  = 49) in a pretest believed could get them “cancelled.” We found that people think they are more likely to get cancelled for making statements than is warranted, both when compared with other people’s judgment of the same statement and with others’ expressed willingness to impose such sanctions. Their estimates are also only weakly related to how others judged the same statement. Although participants generally judged cancellation as similarly likely whether they imagined themselves or another person making a statement, this self-other asymmetry emerged once cancellation was framed explicitly as taking place on social media: participants then perceived a greater likelihood of cancellation when simply imagining themselves, rather than another person, making the same statement. Together, these findings suggest that people systematically overestimate the risk of being cancelled and raise the possibility that concerns about cancellation are fueled by inaccurate beliefs about others’ willingness to impose social sanctions.

articles.title: The recoverability, reliability and generalisability of reward processing parameters and relation to mental health symptoms
articles.authors: Alexandra Claire Pike; Rowan Board; Eoin Travers; Megan Croal; Yeuk Ching Lam; Vincent Valton; Oliver Joe Robinson; Jonathan Paul Roiser
articles.abstract: Theory-driven computational psychiatry attempts to use cognitive models of computation to understand how mental health problems might relate to (or be caused by) changes in cognitive processes such as learning and decision-making. However, the potential applications and relevance of this approach are contingent on several (often implicit) assumptions, including that computational parameters 1) are recoverable, 2) are reliable over time, 3) reflect conceptually similar processes across different tasks, and 4) relate to symptoms. To illustrate and test these assumptions for a selection of commonly used tasks, we recruited a large online sample of participants (n=548), who completed seven mental health questionnaires and five tasks. A subset of n=115 was re-invited to complete the five tasks 14 days later. For each task, five models (including a null model, or model of no interest) were fit and the winning model was selected through Bayesian model comparison. The parameters from the winning models showed good recovery (mean r=0.853, sd=0.135), and good to excellent levels of test-retest reliability (mean ICC=0.635, sd=0.30). Parameters that were theoretically or mathematically similar to each other were, however, generally not related, with relationships only found within the class of ‘inverse temperature’-like parameters (significant r values ranged from 0.11 to 0.23): indicating that in general, parameters do not generalise across task contexts. Finally, only parameters from two of the five tasks (four-armed bandit and cognitive effort) related to symptoms, and these relationships were weak (maximum absolute r values ~0.10). To conclude, it seems that at least some of the implicit assumptions when advocating for the use of computational models in psychiatry are not met for these popular tasks and computational models. This limits the clinical and translational utility of this approach. Computational psychiatry researchers should carefully assess the assumptions and psychometric properties of their tasks and models as part of their early-phase development to ensure robustness of the field going forward.

articles.title: Color cues, developmental coupling, and the limits of the late-sighted model: A commentary on Vogelsang et al. (2024)
articles.authors: David Henry Peterzell
articles.abstract:
Vogelsang et al. (2024) report in Science that congenitally blind individuals who gain sight late in life rely disproportionately on color cues for object recognition, unlike normally sighted controls. They propose that this pattern reflects the adaptive value of neonatal color immaturity: because typical infants experience a period of degraded chromatic input, they develop object representations grounded in luminance rather than color.
The theoretical account, however, rests on an incomplete engagement with the developmental literature. Decades of research indicate that color and luminance sensitivity are developmentally coupled through shared photoreceptor immaturity: the same front-end bottleneck that degrades chromatic input in neonates simultaneously degrades luminance and spatial processing. If this coupling is real, there is no luminance-first window, because the bottleneck lifts for both dimensions together. The account's central premise is therefore undermined not by arguing independence, but by showing that color and luminance do not develop in the sequence the account requires.
I further identify methodological concerns: a small and unusual sample, a fixed order confound in the primary behavioral task, a computational model implementing a binary switch rather than the gradual chromatic cascade of normal development, and a color sensitivity test using non-equiluminant stimuli, leaving open the possibility that luminance rather than chromatic contrast sensitivity drove performance.
Together, these issues require qualification of the conclusions of Vogelsang et al. and reconciliation of the proposed account with classical findings in infant visual development. This broader question remains important, but the answer is unlikely to hinge on color alone.

articles.title: PARADOx: A Randomized Controlled Trial Examining Short- and Long-Term, Family-Specific, and Side Effects of a Digital Parenting Intervention
articles.authors: Rick van Logchem; Anne Bülow; Crystal R. Smit; Savannah Boele; Bart Soenens; Loes Keijsers
articles.abstract: Few digital parenting interventions for preventing adolescent mental health problems exist and little is known about their effectiveness. This preregistered Randomized Controlled Trial examined short-term (1 month post-intervention) and longer-term (6 months) effects, side-effects, and family-specific effects of a novel digital parenting intervention. Dutch adolescents (N = 547, 53% female, Mage = 14.1) and one of their parents (N = 541; 81% mothers, Mage = 46.7) were assigned to a control or intervention group. The intervention group received a parenting feedback report and 18 parenting tips across six weeks, in the second half of a 100-day diary study. No explicit intervention effects were identified, but no harmful side-effects were identified either. At 6-month follow-up, parent- and adolescent-reported overprotection decreased and longer-term adolescent well-being improved. Furthermore, improvements in overprotection and adolescent well-being were correlated and changes in overparenting were generally stronger when parents were exposed to more tips, tentatively suggesting a benefit of 100 days of journaling. The trial was retrospectively registered (CCMO; registration NL-009451) on 19-02-2025.

articles.title: Manipulating Causal Priors Affects the Outcome-Density Bias
articles.authors: Simon Stephan; Michael R. Waldmann
articles.abstract: Non-contingent learning data can lead to the (seemingly) illusory perception of causality if the potential cause and effect often co-occur – an effect known as “outcome-density bias.” Bayesian models of causal induction explain this effect as the result of a rational learning process in which the data do not fully override non-zero causal priors. Convincing evidence for this rational explanation requires the demonstration of an experimentally manipulated effect of causal priors, which has been lacking. We successfully manipulated participants’ (𝑁 = 300) causal priors through visually conveyed causal mechanism information. This manipulation influenced the outcome-density effect in the predicted way: in a condition inducing low causal priors, the effect disappeared. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of visual mechanism information in manipulating priors and strengthen the Bayesian view of causal induction.

articles.title: An Experimental Paradigm for Evaluating Shared Agency in Humans and LLMs
articles.authors: Anita Braga; Ty Hayes; Simon Myers; Paolo Turrini; Nick Chater
articles.abstract: Coordination is central to social life, yet LLMs are poor coordinators. We introduce the Hangman game as a novel experimental paradigm to study why. Our focus is on shared agency, a precondition for coordination, defined as the ability to form shared goals, intentions, and plans. Success in Hangman requires agents to coordinate on word choices across rounds. Human teams reliably develop shared strategies, coordinating on mutually predictable word patterns. LLMs fail to do so. Across Human-Human, Human-LLM, and LLM-LLM teams, humans outperform all tested state-of-the-art LLMs, and humans paired with LLMs perform worse than when paired with other humans. As LLMs are increasingly deployed as collaborative agents, coordination failures undermine their usefulness. Hangman offers a flexible, controlled method for measuring and tracking coordination skills as models develop.

articles.title: An Evolution of the UCLA Isolation Scales: Distinguishing Feelings of Loneliness from Social Disconnection in UK and US Online Adult Samples
articles.authors: Paddy Maher; Yavor Dragostinov; Ross David Stewart; Andrew John Cooper
articles.abstract: Loneliness measurement has faced serious challenges in recent years, and recent reviews have cited indeterminate factor structures and poor construct validity as the principal causes. The field's most widely used instrument, the Revised UCLA Loneliness Scale (R-UCLA), has come under particular scrutiny. We address these challenges, firstly by clarifying the structure of the UCLA, and then by redeveloping it through a scale purification process (N = 21,589) in which we prune cross-loading items to create the UCLA-V (“UCLA-Five”). We confirm that the new scale behaves consistently across gender, geographic regions and age groups, and that it is functionally and structurally equivalent to its predecessor, but with a clear structure and improved model fit. We also derive the UCLA-V-short, the first true three-item representation of the UCLA (rparent = .92). We validate these scales in a multi-rater follow-up study (N = 910), finding that the UCLA is an isolation scale with three dimensions, one of which is loneliness.

articles.title: Cognitive drivers of feedback-seeking decisions differ between socially fearful and reassurance-seeking individuals
articles.authors: Yukta Thyagaraj; Caroline J Charpentier
articles.abstract: Seeking feedback about oneself from others is an important aspect of social communication, and its dysfunctions can be characteristic of underlying psychopathology, especially social anxiety. However, the factors driving active feedback-seeking decisions and how they vary along social anxiety and related internalizing symptoms, remain poorly understood. To address this, in this study, we developed a novel online task that allowed us to quantify the effects of expected feedback valence, confidence about performance, feedback directness, and perceived feedback instrumentality on decisions to seek or avoid feedback. Across two independent datasets (final N=344), our findings show that individuals are more likely to seek feedback when they expect it to be positive and when they perceive it as helpful. Symptom-based clustering analyses reveal differences in the drivers of feedback-seeking based on the relative levels of different social anxiety symptoms (social fear-avoidance v/s reassurance-seeking), suggesting a dissociation between these constructs. Our results contribute to understanding the cognitive mechanisms underlying feedback-seeking decisions and their variation along social anxiety, potentially informing cognitive-based interventions for subtypes of social anxiety.

articles.title: Who reciprocates whom? Mutual influence in emoticon use and perceived responsiveness between chat workers and young people seeking help
articles.authors: Gerard Chung; Andrew Zi Han Yee; Nataliya Thell
articles.abstract:
Research on computer-mediated communication has shown that reciprocal emoticon and emoji
use predicts how warm, responsive, and engaging a text exchange is perceived to be. This work,
however, has been conducted almost entirely in symmetric dyads, such as peers, friends, dating
partners, or colleagues, in which both parties enter the exchange on equal terms. We extend this
literature to a role-asymmetric helping dyad, examining emoticon reciprocity between trained chat
workers and help-seeking young people in a Singapore youth chatline. Drawing on the Interpersonal
Process Model of Intimacy and Communication Accommodation Theory, we analysed 86,424 lagged
turns nested within 1,435 sessions using the Actor–Partner Interdependence Model, operationalising
emoticon use in both binary (presence) and count (intensity) forms. Three findings emerged. First,
emoticons were a routine feature of the channel (87% of sessions), with chat workers using more and a
narrower affirming repertoire than users. Second, positive actor and partner effects were present in
both directions, but the partner effect from chat worker to user was substantially larger than the
reverse, indicating asymmetric reciprocity tracking the role and power asymmetry of the dyad. Third,
partner effects were stronger in sessions rated higher on worker responsiveness and session
satisfaction; actor effects were also moderated by both ratings, though in opposing directions
depending on operationalisation. What matters in text-based help, our results suggest, is not either
party's emoticon use considered alone but the reciprocal exchange between them.

articles.title: Distinct neural representations of perceptual and numerical absence in the human brain
articles.authors: Benjy Barnett; Stephen M Fleming
articles.abstract: The ability to represent absences is fundamental to human perception and cognition. Sensitivity to perceptual absences depends on inferring a lack of sensory evidence, enabling the recognition of a lack of environmental resources or other objects. In turn, encoding absence as a concept – in the form of the number zero – has enabled a multitude of technological and mathematical advances. Whether perceptual and conceptual absences rely on overlapping or distinct neural representations in the human brain, however, remains unknown. We employed three different tasks together with magnetoencephalography (MEG) to characterise neural representations of absence evoked within perceptual and numerical domains. Using multivariate decoding and Bayes factor analyses, we replicate previous findings of shared representations of numerical absence across symbolic and non-symbolic formats. In contrast, we show that these representations of numerical absence are distinct to those evoked by perceptual absences. This result held even after controlling for stimulus-level confounds which generate surface-level similarities between perceptual absences and non-symbolic empty sets. Our results extend a boundary between perception and cognition to the domain of absence and provide insight into the different mechanisms employed by the human brain to both perceive and conceive of absences.

articles.title: People Overestimate Support for “Cancellation”
articles.authors: Jakob Kasper; Randy T. Lee; Jabin Binnendyk; Gordon Pennycook; Ovul Sezer
articles.abstract: Social exclusion has long served as a mechanism of norm enforcement. However, in contemporary discourse, there is a common narrative about “cancel culture” that implies an overextension of social exclusion – often driven by social media. Given the ambiguity surrounding what cancellation entails and how often it occurs, people may struggle to accurately judge their own likelihood of being cancelled for expressing controversial views. Across two preregistered studies (NS1 = 753, NS2 = 753), we examined judgments of cancellation using naturalistic statements that participants (N  = 49) in a pretest believed could get them “cancelled.” We found that people think they are more likely to get cancelled for making statements than is warranted, both when compared with other people’s judgment of the same statement and with others’ expressed willingness to impose such sanctions. Their estimates are also only weakly related to how others judged the same statement. Although participants generally judged cancellation as similarly likely whether they imagined themselves or another person making a statement, this self-other asymmetry emerged once cancellation was framed explicitly as taking place on social media: participants then perceived a greater likelihood of cancellation when simply imagining themselves, rather than another person, making the same statement. Together, these findings suggest that people systematically overestimate the risk of being cancelled and raise the possibility that concerns about cancellation are fueled by inaccurate beliefs about others’ willingness to impose social sanctions.

articles.title: Artificial intelligence as work design: A scoping review of motivational and demotivational mechanisms in professional work contexts
articles.authors: Samira Hamouche; Josette M. P. Gevers; Jan C. Fransoo; Toni Waefler
articles.abstract:
Work design has long been recognized as a central determinant of intrinsic motivation, well-being, and performance. As artificial intelligence (AI) increasingly enters professional work, it should not be understood merely as a technical tool, but as a force that directly reshapes working conditions by altering how tasks are structured, decisions are made, feedback is delivered, and workers interact with their work and with one another. This makes work design and motivational theories especially important for the design and implementation of AI systems. To clarify this fragmented field within human-AI interaction research, including closely related work on human-AI collaboration and human-AI teaming, we conducted a scoping review of AI in professional, knowledge-intensive work contexts, focusing on how different AI configurations relate to motivation and demotivation. Following PRISMA-ScR, we reviewed 31 empirical and non-empirical sources published between 2009 and 2025.
Only three sources proposed explicit motivational AI design frameworks, suggesting that motivation is still more often treated as an outcome of AI use than as a direct design objective. Across the corpus, AI was described as motivationally supportive when it preserved meaningful human influence, provided developmental feedback, or reduced routine burden without hollowing out meaningful work. Demotivational risks were described when AI expanded surveillance, narrowed self-organization, prescribed work too tightly, or translated social relations into comparison and performance signals. These risks were especially prominent in conceptual discussions of managerial or algorithmic-management configurations, whereas empirical evidence was more concentrated in assistive AI settings.

articles.title: The Medusa Effect Revisited
articles.authors: Kaito Takashima; Takahiro Kawabe
articles.abstract: The Medusa effect refers to the cognitive phenomenon in which individuals depicted directly in a photograph (Level 1; L1) are judged as possessing more mind than individuals depicted within a photograph embedded inside another photograph (Level 2; L2). Although the Medusa effect has been replicated in several studies, a number of issues still remain to be addressed. One concerns the measurement scale used for mind perception, and another concerns the size of the face and body of the person depicted in the stimulus images. The present study addressed these unresolved issues across three experiments. Experiment 1 examined whether the Medusa effect could be replicated using the 18 concrete mental capacity items derived from Gray et al. (2007). Results showed that L1 targets were judged as possessing greater mental capacities than L2 targets across several capacities, particularly Agency-related capacities. Experiment 2 tested whether this effect would persist when L1 and L2 targets were presented at equal displayed sizes. Under size-controlled conditions, the Medusa effect was not observed. Experiment 3 independently manipulated target size and hierarchical frame cues. Presentation size showed significant main effects across multiple mental capacity judgments, whereas frame presence showed no reliable main effects. Together, these findings suggest that stimulus size contributes substantially to previously reported Medusa effect when mind perception is assessed using concrete mental capacity items.

articles.title: How Schemas Shape Memory for Everyday Activities: The Roles of Surprise and Prediction  Uncertainty
articles.authors: Tan Nguyen; Ken McRae; Jeffrey M. Zacks
articles.abstract:
Schemas provide structured knowledge that guides comprehension and memory of 
everyday experience. Schema violations have powerful effects on memory, but sometimes 
in apparently opposing directions—schema consistency can apparently boost or impair 
memory. We used narrative texts describing everyday activities to try to resolve the 
apparently conflicting effects. We investigated three fundamental issues: how schema 
consistency and violation affect memory formation, what dimensions of schema violation 
contribute to subjective surprise, and how prediction uncertainty influences memory 
encoding. In Study 1, gist but not detail memory was monotonically better for more 
surprising events. This is consistent with the proposal that schema violations trigger 
additional processing, but not with the proposal that schema-consistent information 
garners any advantageous processing. Schema-consistent information was not forgotten 
more slowly over 24 hours. Study 2 investigated factors leading to schema-driven surprise. 
Surprise was driven primarily by an event's appropriateness (contextual fit) rather than its 
relevance to activity goals. Critically, inappropriate events elicited stronger surprise when 
they occurred during goal-relevant actions, consistent with predictive processing theories, 
suggesting that humans develop more precise predictions about functionally important 
aspects of activities. In Study 3, gist memory was better for high-uncertainty information, 
supporting proposals that additional attention is allocated to moments of uncertainty. 
Together, these findings suggest that gist memory is enhanced when events are surprising 
or uncertain, and that surprise arises from contextual inappropriateness modulated by goal 
relevance.

articles.title: Non-linear Sensitivity to Binaural Interaural Correlation Changes: The Impact of Correlation Change and Interaural Delay
articles.authors: Yizhen Yang; Liang Li
articles.abstract:
Human listeners’ ability to detect a binaural interaural correlation (BIAC) change is crucial for auditory scene analysis. The present study investigated whether human listeners can detect a binaurally correlation-changed arbitrary-noise fragment [a ‘break’ in correlation, break in interaural correlation (BIAC)] embedded in binaurally steady-noise markers, and further examined how this detection sensitivity is modulated by different levels of correlation change. The longest interaural delay was used to index the BIAC detection sensitivity.
In Experiment 1, twenty-three normal-hearing participants were tested for the longest interaural delay remained to detect a binaurally correlation-changed arbitrary-noise fragment embedded in identical arbitrary-noise (BIAC fragment correlations: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8). In Experiment 2, another twenty normal-hearing participants were tested for the longest interaural delay remained to detect a binaurally uncorrelated arbitrary-noise fragment in steady-noise (the background steady-noise correlations: 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1). 
As the BIAC correlation level increased, the longest interaural delay remained to detect the BIAC in identical noises decreased significantly (Exp 1); conversely, as the background steady-noise correlation level increased, the longest interaural delay remained to detect the uncorrelated BIAC increased significantly (Exp 2). Results showed that detection sensitivity is non-linearly modulated in both cases. Specifically, thresholds followed exponential decay (Exp 1) and growth (Exp 2) models, revealing a "mutation" in sensitivity as correlation contrast decreased. These findings suggest that people could detect a BIAC in steady-noise markers with binaural delay whether the correlation of BIAC fragment or background decrease, and this sensitivity was nonlinearly modulated by the magnitude of correlation change.

articles.title: Disrupted Geometric Constraints on Connectome Wiring in Children and Adolescents with ADHD
articles.authors: Nanfang Pan; Francis Normand; Arshiya Sangchooli; Christopher Adamson; Priscila Levi; Victor Barnes; Isaac Z. Pope; Stuart Oldham; Alexander Holmes; Trang Cao
articles.abstract: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is thought to result from aberrant neurodevelopmental wiring of connectome architecture, yet the precise mechanisms underpinning this miswiring remain unclear. Here, we used a generative mathematical model to understand how physical and geometric constraints on brain network development might be altered in the disorder. Leveraging multisite diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (dMRI) datasets comprising 713 ADHD children and 304 neurotypical controls (aged 6-18 years), we fitted the recently developed geometric eigenmode model (GEM) to individual data describing connectivity between each pair of cortical regions, as quantified using probabilistic tractography. Replicating past work, the GEM was able to recover both topological and topographical properties of the empirical connectomes, but reconstruction accuracy was lower in ADHD children, suggesting a relative untethering of connectome architecture from geometric constraints. This untethering was particularly salient for long-range connections, which were disproportionately affected in the ADHD cohort. Regional variations in the degree to which connectivity was untethered from geometry were correlated with spatial maps of cortical developmental expansion, myelination, and transcriptomic organization, consistent with a neurodevelopmental basis for geometric untethering that is spatially patterned along the cortical hierarchy. Together, these findings suggest that aberrant neurodevelopment in ADHD may untether connectome development from typical geometric and physical constraints, resulting in subtle disruptions of long-range inter-regional connectivity.

articles.title: Can conversational AI influence confidence in pre-existing political beliefs?
articles.authors: Semra Sevi; Can Serif Mekik
articles.abstract: Conversational artificial intelligence (AI) may influence political polarization by shaping the strength with which political attitudes are held. Generative AI systems are increasingly capable of conducting adaptive, human-like conversations, raising questions about their ability to strengthen or weaken belief certainty. We report results from a preregistered, two-wave randomized experiment (N = 1,968) testing whether GPT-4 can strengthen or weaken individuals' attitudes on salient, evolving political issues. Participants engaged in personalized, multi-turn conversations in which the AI was instructed to either strengthen or weaken their stated views, or to engage in non-political discussion. Across issues including housing, immigration, and economic policy, AI-mediated conversations consistently reduced attitude strength. Specifically, belief confidence, while showing no evidence of strengthening. These effects persisted for up to 21 days. Our findings indicate that generative AI can durably influence political attitudes, primarily by weakening rather than strengthening belief certainty. By examining both pro and counter-attitudinal persuasion among individuals with existing views in dynamic political contexts, this study provides a demanding test of AI’s persuasive capacity and clarifies its implications for political polarization and democratic discourse.

articles.title: Innovations in Practice: A Pilot Randomised Trial on Smartphone and Social Media Abstinence: Effects on Sleep Quality and Psychological Well-being in Adolescents
articles.authors: Emma Caitlin Sullivan; Scott Cairney; Lisa Henderson
articles.abstract: There is growing concern that smartphones and social media (S/SM) disrupt adolescent sleep, wellbeing and cognition, yet causal evidence from robust interventions is limited. To assess the acceptability of a total 21-day S/SM detox we conducted a preregistered (https://osf.io/79zd2) pilot randomised control trial with eighty-two adolescents aged 13-18 years from UK schools. Participants were randomly assigned to a total S/SM detox (N=26), total S/SM detox with access to a basic “brick” phone (N=26) or a business-as-usual control (N=30). Pre-and post-intervention measures of sleep, wellbeing, and cognition were collected using surveys, wearables, and cognitive tasks.  Retention was high, with 13% attrition across groups. At 21-days, one or both detox groups showed tentative improvements in sleep duration, daytime sleepiness, depressive symptoms, perceived stress, and working memory relative to the controls, however these changes were not maintained at a two-month follow-up. Open-ended post-detox reflections reflected a range of both positive and negative experiences, with reported challenges including boredom, functional reliance on smartphones, and fear of missing out.  Nevertheless, 83% endorsed the need for government action. Digital detoxes appear feasible for generating causal evidence; however, they can also pose functional challenges to young people. Fully powered trials should address these challenges, potentially via more targeted intervention approaches (e.g. switching off the internet on smartphones, particularly at bed-time).

articles.title: Extracurricular Activities and Educational Expectations of Children with Undocumented Backgrounds: Evidence from Los Angeles
articles.authors: Jeehye Kang; Philip N. Cohen
articles.abstract:
Executive Summary
	The academic benefits of extracurricular participation for disadvantaged children are well known. However, whether such participation would promote educational expectations for children of undocumented families is unclear, given the formidable institutional barriers facing them. Using the Los Angeles Family and Neighborhood Study (L.A. FANS), we investigate the role of extracurricular activities in immigrant-origin youth’s academic expectations, focusing on children of undocumented immigrants. Major findings include the following: 
•	While extracurricular participation is positively associated with educational expectations for US-origin children, the benefit is unclear for immigrant-origin youth. 
•	Compared with US-origin children, extracurricular participation of any type (sports-oriented, and non-sports-inclusive) is associated with smaller gains in high school expectations for both children of documented and undocumented immigrants. 
•	Compared with US-origin children, sports-oriented activities are associated with smaller gains in college expectations for children of undocumented immigrants. 
•	The disparities were salient among children (ages 12-17) than among younger children (ages 9–11). 
These findings are consistent with segmented assimilation theory and racialized organizations theory, where structural inequalities embedded in extracurricular settings operate in ways that marginalize racial-ethnic minorities. To better serve these minoritized youth, practitioners should create more inclusive environment where mutual respect for racial and ethnic differences is established and maintained. More importantly, educational and immigration policies should address structural inequalities and legal barriers facing immigrant-origin youth.

articles.title: Moral Adaptation: Sustaining Climate Activism When Futures Fail
articles.authors: Daphne Fietz
articles.abstract:
How does collective action persist when the futures that once motivated it become implausible? Existing scholarship highlights moral commitments and expectation management, yet it typically treats these dimensions in parallel. Based on a two-year longitudinal study of a German climate resistance group, this paper argues that moral grammars are temporally structured and subject to reorganization.
As doubts about the preventability of climate catastrophe grew, the group engaged in moral adaptation: shifting from a consequentialist orientation anchored in catastrophe prevention toward a virtue-ethical orientation centered on responsible conduct under conditions of crisis. Unlike temporal work, which preserves future horizons, moral adaptation relocated moral evaluation away from anticipated outcomes and toward present conduct, redefining the meanings of strategy, obligation, and community, and preceding the group’s later organizational transformation.
The paper theorizes moral adaptation as a mechanism of resilience distinct from identity maintenance, emotional management, and temporal work. In doing so, it advances the case for treating moral grammars as dynamic, evaluative structures whose temporal orientation can shift under adverse conditions, contributing to the integration of the sociology of morality and the sociology of temporality.

articles.title: Factors associated with disclosure of protected characteristics: a systematic review and meta-synthesis
articles.authors: George Wood; Rachel Connolly; David Nicholls; Jeremy Dietz
articles.abstract:
Background: Employers increasingly collect equality, diversity and inclusion data, and reducing non-disclosure rates is often used as a key performance indicator. However, why people with protected characteristics choose either to disclose or withhold this information at work has not, to our knowledge, been systematically reviewed. This review is a thematic synthesis of qualitative studies examining the experiences of disclosure in the workplace of people with protected characteristics according to UK law including disability, gender reassignment, race and sexual orientation. 

Methods: A grey literature search was conducted in October 2022 using Google, Lens.org and Web of Science to identify English-language systematic reviews and qualitative studies on adults’ workplace experiences of disclosing or not disclosing protected characteristics. A thematic synthesis was developed from these studies, and the search was rerun in July 2025 to identify additional evidence and update the themes. The risk of bias of each study was assessed using the CASP qualitative checklist.

Results: Findings from 60 papers were included in the review, including two meta-ethnographies and a further 19 studies. The thematic synthesis yielded five main themes from the experiences and views on disclosure in the workplace from adults with protected characteristics: 1) Disclosure is an important, ongoing life event that has significant implications; 2) When people do not disclose, they may develop strategies to moderate disclosure that require energy to maintain; 3) Moderators of disclosure; 4) Positive consequences of disclosure; 5) Negative consequences of disclosure.

Limitations: There was limited evidence on the experience of people with visible disabilities, bisexual people, and trans people, and no evidence discussing the experiences of people disclosing information about ethnicity. No studies were found discussing intersectionality of identities and how this might affect disclosure. Limited information was reported regarding participants' jobs, and some of the studies were older and conducted in contexts that might be less likely to reflect UK culture today.

Conclusions: Employers who focus on creating a trusting, supportive environment; actively mitigating the risks of disclosure; reassuring staff about how disclosures will be handled; and meeting accessibility and adjustment needs can improve the workplace experiences of people with minority identities and potentially increase disclosure rates.

articles.title: Real Estate Wealth Is Not a Monolith: Non-Primary Real Estate and Its Role in Wealth Concentration
articles.authors: Pietro Valetto; Stefano Filauro; Ive Marx; Janet C. Gornick; Carolyn Fisher
articles.abstract: Housing wealth is widely recognized as the most equally distributed major asset class in advanced economies, anchoring middle-class wealth portfolios and overall wealth inequality. Yet, in this paper we observe how treating real estate as a monolith obscures a critical divide. Disaggregating net housing wealth into primary real estate equity and non-primary real estate equity reveals different distributional patterns that fundamentally alter our understanding of housing's role in the wealth distribution. Using harmonized microdata from the EU HFCS (2021) and U.S. SCF (2022), we show that while primary residences are indeed more equally distributed than overall wealth, non-primary real estate such as vacation homes, rental properties, land, and business properties makes up the most highly concentrated component of household portfolios, surpassing even financial assets. Our Gini factor decomposition analysis across various European countries and the U.S. reveals that non-primary real estate exhibits extreme concentration, with Gini coefficients ranging from 0.88 to 0.96, and contributes disproportionately to overall inequality relative to its wealth share. Therefore, the equalizing effect of housing relative to other asset classes emphasized in prior research holds only for owner-occupied homes. These findings suggest that policies treating all housing uniformly may inadvertently amplify wealth concentration, particularly in Europe where non-primary property ownership is nearly twice as prevalent as in the United States. (Stone Center Working Paper Series)

articles.title: Can Nonprofits Survive their Founders? Assessing Succession Effects in Private Museums
articles.authors: Johannes Aengenheyster
articles.abstract: Executive succession has widely been seen as disruptive for nonprofit organizations, negatively affecting performance in general and survival in particular. However, quantitative research on the succession-survival link in nonprofit organizations is limited, and findings are mixed. To extend knowledge in this area, a study on succession effects is conducted for a population of private art museums. These arts organizations show heavy founder involvement in funding, governance and vision, and have hence been argued to be vulnerable to executive succession. Succession effects in event-history analysis (368 organizations, 4371 organization-years) are only moderate and insignificant, yet might be more detrimental in some cases, such as for organizations that are small and opened by male founders. It is argued that considering the contingencies of organizational succession is required to comprehensively assess its hazards and opportunities.

articles.title: Stalled or distorted? Tempo Effects in Africa’s Fertility Transition
articles.authors: Duke Mwedzi
articles.abstract: Fertility rates in Africa have remained high, declined slowly, and stalled before reaching replacement level. However, there is little consensus on which countries truly stalled as existing research uses a variety of competing methods that are disconnected from fertility theory, do not account for tempo effects in the Total Fertility Rate, and rely too heavily on subjective researcher judgment. This study revisits fertility stalls using the first and second derivatives of tempo-adjusted TFR and a piecewise regression approach. This method reveals four previously unidentified stalls in Sudan, Botswana, Mauritania, and South Africa, as well as nine further stalls in other countries. Stalls in Botswana, South Africa, and Zimbabwe are particularly severe. While tempo effects do not fully explain stalls, they are positively associated, suggesting that stalls may partially reflect changes in the timing rather than total amount of fertility.

articles.title: Sanctioned Scholars: The Price Of Speaking Freely In Today’s Academy
articles.authors: Nathan Honeycutt; Sean T. Stevens
articles.abstract: The Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE), a nonprofit organization committed to defending and sustaining the individual rights of all Americans to free speech and free thought, maintains a database documenting the ways and reasons scholars have faced calls for sanction because of their speech. This report presents the results of a survey of 209 scholars in this Scholars Under Fire database who were targeted for sanction because of their speech between 2020 and 2024.

articles.title: Relational Wealth: Ownership Diffusion and Ownership Confusion in Wealthy Families
articles.authors: Doron Shiffer-Sebba
articles.abstract: Economic elites have accumulated staggering resources. But scholars tend to conceptualize their wealth as singularly owned by individuals or households, neglecting long-established relational aspects of economic behavior. Using 30 interviews and six months of in-depth ethnographic observation at a “family office” that manages large fortunes, I ask how relationships shape elite perceptions of wealth ownership and the subsequent flows of family wealth. I find that when assets are legally shared (“diffused”), ownership perceptions can mismatch, and ownership can be “confused”. When owner- ship is confused, diffusing actors like family offices can influence ownership perceptions, shaping how elites wield wealth in practice. These shifts usually, but not exclusively, benefit “patriarchs.” Understanding the increasingly consequential resources of economic elites requires us to examine the assumption of unambiguous ownership: elite wealth is fundamentally entangled in familial and professional relationships.

articles.title: ROI and the Misclassification of Criminal Justice Education Programs: How Accountability Metrics Shape Public Universities and the Evaluation of CJ Education
articles.authors: Sandra L. Trappen
articles.abstract: Criminal Justice (CJ) education programs at public universities are increasingly evaluated through state level ROI frameworks that tie program value to short term earnings and workforce alignment. This article shows how these models, now embedded in many funding and accountability regimes, tend to misclassify CJ education programs. Descriptive data from the National Center for O*NET Development, BLS, College Scorecard, and state salary databases reveal three key structural mismatches: most CJ occupations do not require a bachelor’s degree; public sector wage structures depress early career earnings; and ROI classifications shift with occupational and temporal definitions. As a result, ROI models capture labor market composition and state funding priorities rather than program value, obscuring CJ’s civic and community roles.

articles.title: CONVERGENT FAULTS : A Quantitative Forensic of Private Credit's Synchronized Systemic Risk (2026–2027)
articles.authors: Djellal Djouad
articles.abstract: Private credit has gone from roughly $400 billion in 2009 to about $3 trillion in mid-2026. U.S. business development companies (BDCs) now hold close to half a trillion of that, split across three architectures with very different liquidity and disclosure properties. The official line, repeated by the Federal Reserve, the Financial Stability Board and most sell-side credit desks, calls the resulting risk "limited and manageable." Fund-level leverage is low, defaults are below the long-run leveraged-loan average, and bank exposure looks bounded. We argue that the official line works only when each stress channel is examined alone, and breaks down once the channels are read together. This paper documents five channels: software and SaaS valuation compression under AI disruption; bank-to-non-bank credit tightening, with JPMorgan as first mover; retail liquidity gating at perpetual non-traded BDCs; the "Bermuda Triangle" of related-party lending between Apollo and Athene, KKR and Global Atlantic, Brookfield and its affiliated insurers; and the 2026–2027 refinancing wall hitting a sector that has just lost its bid. Using Bloomberg Intelligence proprietary research, regulatory data and the public filings of the largest BDCs, we show that these channels are coupled. Each one tightens the others under plausible macroeconomic conditions, and the joint stress horizon is short enough to read on a calendar. The cascade is already visible. On 4 June 2026 Blackstone Private Credit Fund, the largest BDC, $82.1 billion of holdings, gated quarterly redemptions at 5% of NAV against requests of 10%. Its $1 billion 6% 2032 notes trade 150 basis points wide of triple-B financial peers, against an adviser (Blackstone Inc.) rated A+/A+. We map the catalysts that lie ahead, assign probabilities to three outcome scenarios, and identify a convergence window centred on Q1–Q2 2027.

articles.title: Satellite Sociology: Interpreting Spatial Traces of Human Activity from Earth Observation Data
articles.authors: Yuichiro Otani
articles.abstract:
We introduce Satellite Sociology, a framework for observing and interpreting social processes using Earth observation data. The framework treats artificial satellites as devices that capture the material traces through which human activity becomes expressed in space. These traces are not limited to urban environments but include any spatial configurations shaped by human behavior, institutions, and economic processes.

A central feature of Satellite Sociology lies in the definition of the unit of analysis. Spatial units—such as buildings, grids, or administrative regions—are not treated as neutral technical choices, but as explicit constructions of the social system under investigation. Different unit definitions yield different representations of social structure, enabling multiple interpretations of the same spatial domain.

The framework interprets spatial patterns as observable traces from which underlying behavior can be inferred, and emphasizes the feedback relationship between activity and its spatial manifestations. Spatial configurations both reflect accumulated decisions and influence subsequent behavior, linking observation with process.

To illustrate one application, we analyze urban space as an accumulated outcome of social decisions and examine its structural persistence. Using Shinagawa Ward (Tokyo) and Christchurch (New Zealand), we construct two building-level indicators: the Building-Level Vegetation Exposure Index (BVEI) and the Built--Vegetation Imbalance Index (BVII).

The results reveal distinct allocation regimes and identify spatial configurations consistent with structural constraints on environmental redistribution. These findings demonstrate how satellite-derived spatial patterns can support inference about the processes that generate and stabilize spatial structure.

Satellite Sociology provides a general framework for interpreting spatial data as traces of human activity and for linking Earth observation with sociological analysis.

articles.title: Political News Personalization in Conversational AI: Experimental Evidence from Three Large Language Models
articles.authors: Zachary P Dickson; Miriam Sorace; Thomas Samuel Robinson
articles.abstract: As AI is increasingly integrated into everyday digital life, a growing share of users turn to AI chatbots for information about current events. We run a series of experiments to test whether three large language models (LLMs)---GPT-4o-mini, Grok, and DeepSeek Chat---tailor political content to implicit user signals embedded in conversation. Two computational experiments each employ a demographic arm---in which cues about gender, life stage, and occupation are established in conversation before a political request---and an ideological arm in which cues spanning left-wing to right-wing orientations precede the same request. Across 2,000+ trials per arm, demographic cues systematically predict variation in political news provision across all three models, with occupation and life stage as the strongest predictors. Ideological cues show little effect on historical event framing for GPT-4o-mini but produce substantial framing effects for Grok and DeepSeek. Ideological cues also strongly predict news provision across all three models. Search query analysis reveals that GPT-4o-mini and Grok additionally vary their retrieval queries based on user signals, adding an upstream layer of tailoring.

articles.title: State Atrocities, Rebel Governance, and Public Opinion on Humanitarian Intervention
articles.authors: Jungmin Han; Hyunjung Park
articles.abstract: How do rebel groups shape democratic publics’ attitudes toward humanitarian intervention? Many state-led atrocities occur during civil wars, yet rebels are often portrayed solely as sources of instability rather than as governing actors who shape external perceptions. We argue that rebels’ modes of governance—whether inclusive or exclusive in service provision and decision-making—fundamentally shape public opinion on intervention. Inclusive governance that embodies equality and participation reinforces identification with democratic values, strengthening the moral imperative to protect and the instrumental confidence that intervention would be both justified and effective. In contrast, exclusive governance, marked by coercion or discrimination, undermines perceptions of shared identity and moral legitimacy, making state repression appear justified and intervention less defensible. We test these claims through survey experiments in the U.S. (N=1,615) and the U.K. (N=1,583). Across both countries, inclusive governance boosts perceived legitimacy and credibility of rebels, fostering positive attitudes toward humanitarian intervention, while exclusive governance undermines these perceptions and weakens public endorsement.

articles.title: When technology levels: the cost of arms and wealth inequality in the ancient world
articles.authors: Diego Castañeda Garza
articles.abstract:
A long tradition in historical sociology holds that broad military participation makes societies more
egalitarian, but the idea is hard to test. This paper uses a global archaeological database of
settlement-level wealth Gini coefficients (Bogaard et al. 2025) to test a version of the Andreski
(1954) hypothesis: that iron, a cheap, abundant, and mass-producible metal, lowered inequality by
broadening who could be armed, while bronze, an expensive and elite-controlled metal alloy,
favored the concentration of wealth by keeping the means of violence tightly controlled. In the
Eurasian ancient world, where both metals were indigenous, three econometric designs support
the “democratization of violence” thesis. First, within the same locality and holding the
appropriability of agricultural production constant, iron is associated with a Gini of wealth about
0.07–0.10 lower, while bronze carries no association. Second, an event study around iron’s local
adoption shows a similar effect. Third, in a spectrum of technologies, iron is the only one that
reduces inequality: bronze, the plow and animal traction, cavalry, and writing are all null, as the
cost structure of technology predicts. The estimates are robust to plausible error in the coded
adoption dates. The findings are exploratory and concentrated in the Iron Age Mediterranean and
Britain.

articles.title: Salary Expectations in Academia: A Large-Scale Study of Gender Differences
articles.authors: Marek Kwiek; Wojciech Roszka
articles.abstract: In this examination of gender differences in financial expectations in academia, we used survey data (N = 10,788 scientists and scholars, 18 academic disciplines) integrated with individual-level bibliometric and demographic data from Scopus. We studied what Polish scientists and scholars with various demographic characteristics, academic attitudes, and bibliometric profiles considered their expected salary, or the most appropriate salary for their current position. Top salary-expectation academics were defined as the upper 10% of scientists and scholars in terms of their self-reported expected salary. Our results show that gender differences are much weaker than expected: Women generally tend to have almost equal expected salaries compared to men across age groups, academic positions, and disciplines. Although men are 56% overrepresented in the top salary-expectation group, their financial expectations are similar to those of women. Among top expected-salary academics, the median expected salary for men is only 5% higher than the median expected salary for women. Across all STEM disciplines combined, men on average expect salaries 19% higher than women (in non-STEM 15%, and in MED only 11%). Strongly research-oriented men expect higher median salaries than strongly research-oriented women; male top performers (the upper 10%) expect higher salaries than female top performers; and male internationalists in research expect higher salaries than female internationalists. Finally, as quantile logistic regression shows, salary expectations in science are not homogeneous: academic position and gender dominate in the lower quantiles of distribution while internationalization, motivation and family capital are important in the higher quantiles.

articles.title: Quantities of Interest for Interactions and the Pitfalls of Assuming Linear Treatment Effects
articles.authors: Josep Serrano-Serrat
articles.abstract: While quantitative social sciences often rely on estimating models in which treatment effects vary across groups, researchers rarely specify which causal quantity they aim to estimate or justify their empirical modeling choices. This paper makes two contributions. First, it clarifies the distinct quantities of interest when studying interactions: comparisons at different treatment intensities (the difference in Conditional Average Marginal Effects) and comparisons at similar intensities (what I term the Average Interactive Partial Effect). When treatment effects are non-linear and treatment distributions differ across groups, these quantities diverge. Second, the paper assesses estimation strategies to estimate these quantities. It demonstrates that linear interaction models produce biased estimates of either quantity when treatment effects are non-linear and explores two alternatives that explicitly accommodate such non-linearities. This paper is accompanied by an \texttt{R} package that implements these approaches. Through simulations, stylized examples, and an empirical application, the paper shows that explicitly defining the quantity of interest and selecting appropriate models is essential for valid interaction analysis.

articles.title: Prediction Markets? The Accuracy and Efficiency of Political Prediction Markets in the 2024 Presidential Election
articles.authors: Joshua D. Clinton; TzuFeng Huang
articles.abstract: Nearly 2,200 political prediction markets involving more than $3.2 billion in platform-reported trading volume (Confino, 2024) were traded across the Iowa Electronic Markets, Kalshi, PredictIt, and Polymarket during the final nine weeks of the 2024 U.S. presidential campaign. Overall, 86% of these actively traded political markets performed better than a coin-flip, and they were also generally well-calibrated. Even so, there was modest evidence of longshot bias, and the impressive topline accuracy partly reflects the composition of the contracts being traded. 66% of all markets were priced below $0.20 or above $0.80 on the eve of Election Day, and 96% of those contracts resolved as priced. Among markets priced between $0.40 and $0.60 (12% of the sample), 55% resolved in favor of the priced favorite. Market efficiency was more mixed. Prices involving related political events generally covaried, but daily price changes were considerably less synchronized than price levels — suggesting that short-run movements were often market-specific. Arbitrage opportunities in presidential winner-take-all markets were also present on nearly every day. The prices of political prediction markets may provide useful signals about expected outcomes overall, but aggregate evidence of past accuracy and calibration neither guarantees future market performance nor implies that the price of any particular contract provides a definitive or fully efficient assessment of political reality.

articles.title: Monetary Policy Transmission and Equity Market Performance in Ghana: Nonlinear, Distributional, and Time-Frequency Evidence
articles.authors: Isaac Nyame; Gabriel Osei Forkuo
articles.abstract: Monetary policy transmission to equity markets in frontier economies remains incompletely understood, particularly regarding nonlinear dynamics, distributional heterogeneity, and structural instability. This study investigates how monetary policy shocks affect equity market performance in Ghana — a frontier economy characterised by persistent macroeconomic volatility, shallow financial markets, and recurrent structural breaks — over the period January 2007 to December 2024 (N = 216 monthly observations). We employ a multi-method framework integrating Bayesian Quantile Regression (BQR), Toda–Yamamoto augmented VAR causality testing, impulse response functions (IRF), forecast error variance decomposition (FEVD), and continuous Morlet wavelet coherence analysis. Zivot–Andrews structural break unit root tests account for endogenous breaks associated with the 2008–2009 Global Financial Crisis, the 2014–2015 exchange rate crisis, the COVID-19 pandemic, and the 2022–2023 sovereign debt and inflation crisis. Monetary policy rate shocks are associated with statistically significant, asymmetric, and quantile-dependent negative effects on both the Ghana Stock Exchange Composite Index (GSE-CI) and the Financial Stock Index (GSE-FSI), with the largest contractionary effects concentrated at upper return quantiles. Toda–Yamamoto tests indicate unidirectional causality from monetary policy to equity returns. Wavelet coherence identifies medium-run co-movement (6–18 months) as the dominant transmission band, while FEVD estimates attribute approximately 21.4 percent of forecast error variance in GSE-CI returns to monetary policy innovations at a twelve-month horizon. These findings have practical relevance for investors managing interest rate risk, policymakers balancing disinflation against capital market development, and regulators designing macroprudential frameworks in frontier market contexts.

articles.title: Reliable Panel Regression: A Default Workﬂow for Slow-Moving, Mismeasured Variables
articles.authors: Andrew Rosenberg
articles.abstract: Political scientists often interpret coeﬃcient shrinkage under ﬁxed eﬀects as evidence that pooled associations are confounded. This paper shows why that inference is unreliable for slow moving, mismeasured regressors. Fixed eﬀects can remove much of the signal and identify coeﬃcients from within unit variation that is disproportionately measurement error, attenuating estimates toward zero. A lone ﬁxed eﬀects coeﬃcient may therefore be unable to distinguish confounding from measurement error. I show that the attenuation depends on a regressor’s empirical intraclass correlation and measurement reliability. I then propose a default workﬂow for panel regression. Researchers estimate reliability when possible, report pooled and ﬁxed eﬀects estimates with corrected within reliability, use partial identiﬁcation bounds when the estimates share a sign, and report ﬁxed eﬀects as a within unit estimate when they do not. For variables with no reliability estimate, I introduce an autocorrelation frontier that bounds the attenuation factor directly. I conclude by applying this workﬂow to several published results to show that the data often cannot distinguish attenuation from confounding, and the workﬂow makes clear which case the researcher faces.

articles.title: When Does AI Pay for Itself? A Cost-Benefit Analysis of AI-Assisted Police Report Writing
articles.authors: Erik Alda
articles.abstract: Police departments across the US are rapidly adopting AI-assisted report-writing, yet its cost-benefit case remaines largely unexamined, and the field-trial evidence can be read as either favorable or unfavorable. This paper presents a probabilistic cost-benefit analysis of one such deployment at the Manchester Police Department, varying how the tool's cost is attributed and how much of the measured productivity gain is credited to the AI rather than to organizational learning. Under per-officer subscription pricing, the tool pays for itself. For a medium department, the average net present value is $2.3 million under a favorable, frontier-based reading of the productivity evidence and $0.8 million under a conservative reading anchored to the null result of the department's randomized controlled trial, with a positive return likely in both. Attributing a bundled vendor contract to the program instead yields a negative return. Cost attribution, therefore, determines the sign of the result, while the extent to which the gain is credited to the tool governs how confident a department can be in adopting this technology.

articles.title: Broad Support for Democratic Erosion: The Case of Trump’s TSA Order
articles.authors: Matthew H. Graham
articles.abstract: Public opinion surveys consistently find high support for democracy in the abstract. Studying the case of a popular but unconstitutional executive order, this note shows that broad, cross-partisan support for democratic erosion can emerge when it is attached to a popular policy goal. In March 2026, U.S. president Donald Trump ordered government to pay Transportation Security Administration (TSA) agents despite the lack of a congressional appropriation, in plain violation of Article I, Section 9 of the U.S. Constitution. About two-thirds of Americans approved of the order, including 59% of Democrats and 2024 Kamala Harris voters. Approval was substantially higher among those who correctly answered a question about which branch of government decides how money is spent under the Constitution. The findings suggest that episodes of executive aggrandizement can enjoy broad support when they are attached to popular policy goals, and that civic knowledge does not necessarily act as a bulwark against such support.

articles.title: Bridging the Oral Proficiency Gap: Sociocultural Insights into Chinese Language Learning among Sri Lankan University Students
articles.authors: Chathuni Weerakoon; Syed Tahir Abbas
articles.abstract:
Abstract
Oral proficiency is an essential aspect of second language competence, but most learners are good at taking written exams and have a problem with oral communication. The qualitative research analyses the experiences of eight final-year undergraduate students at a Sri Lankan university in their study of Chinese and the disconnect between written performance and oral skills. Semi-structured interviews uncovered curriculum constraints, poor scaffolded oral practice, and communication difficulties in the workplace, and psychological implications on confidence and study intentions. The strategies that the participants self-regulated to curb these challenges included cooperation with peers, immersion, and use of digital tools. In line with the Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD) and sociocultural theory proposed by Vygotsky, the study identifies the significance of task-based and learner-centered pedagogical change to improve oral competence and match the skills of learners with the international standards

articles.title: Ethically Informed Algorithmic Matching and Refugee Resettlement
articles.authors: Ahmed Ezzeldin Mohamed; Craig Damian Smith
articles.abstract: This article discusses real-world projects using algorithms to match resettled refugees with sponsors and services. It argues that ethically informed algorithmic matching can support larger-scale and better-informed resettlement while avoiding simplistic techno-sceptical or techno-optimistic framings. The article emphasizes the dual-use function of technology in international mobility and the importance of ethical design.

articles.title: Dissuasion and polarization vs. persuasion in nano-targeting: experimental evidence from climate policy messaging
articles.authors: Miriam Sorace; Thomas Samuel Robinson; Simon Hix; Joris Frese
articles.abstract: Micro-targeting is entering a new era: interacting a large set of voter characteristics with multiple message sub-components -- "nano-targeting'' -- will become increasingly cheap and accessible. We combine evidence from two survey experiments (N=5,000) and predictive modeling to test the effectiveness of nano-targeted messages on climate policy. We find that, on average, nano-targeting does not perform better than an overall "best message'', but that nano-targeted messages were more persuasive than the best message for individuals who were already supportive of climate action. We also discover backlash effects from "mis-targeting'', and find an important asymmetry: being randomly assigned messages predicted to be far from one's best policy has stronger dissuasive effects than the persuasive effects of being shown one's best message. Together these results suggest that these technologies may not be practical for most actors, but may be effective for entrepreneurs who aim to dissuade, demobilize or polarize.

articles.title: Macroeconomic Effects of Climate Anomalies on Subnational Inflation in India: Heterogeneity, Monetary Policy and the Role of Inflation Targeting
articles.authors: Naveen Kumar
articles.abstract:
This paper provides novel subnational evidence on the inflationary effects of climate shocks in India
using monthly data for 21 states during 2013–2023 and panel local projections. Four main findings emerge.
First, temperature shocks persistently raise both headline and food inflation, with no evidence of mean
reversion over the medium run. Second, these effects are heterogeneous, with stronger responses in rural
areas, post-monsoon months, and agriculture-dependent states. Third, the response is asymmetric: warm
anomalies drive most of the inflationary effect through agricultural supply disruptions, while cold anomalies
remain statistically insignificant. Fourth, flexible inflation targeting weakens, but does not eliminate, climate induced inflationary pressures.

articles.title: “The Guardian is credible but leans towards opinion”: Shadow mechanisms in GPT-5’s web search and the politics of credibility
articles.authors: Petros Terzis; Kehan Sheng; Marijn Sax; David Moats; Max van Drunen; Rebecca Sokol-Snyder; Ernesto de León; Asia Bega
articles.abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly replacing traditional search engines as people’s primary gateways to information. Yet, despite their growing significance and the increasingly central role of LLMs in political communication and participation, legal frameworks provide limited opportunities for transparency, let alone changes, over how politically sensitive prompts are handled. At the same time, scholarly debates about LLM objectivity remain fixated on statistical formulas for measuring political bias that presuppose a neutral ‘centre’ for contested issues (e.g. abortion), the deviation from which can be measured following traditional mental maps of bias analysis; an assumption, however, that lacks foundation in political and legal philosophy. This paper proposes an epistemic shift in how neutrality is framed and dealt with in LLM research and practice. First, we systematically critique the dominant ‘political bias’ paradigm, showing how scholars as well as government and industry actors frame neutrality in ways that entrench methodological limitations. Then, we conduct a multi-stage, mixed-methods pilot study to illuminate aspects of GPT-5’s web search that are core to the processes of information retrieval. To do so, we prompted GPT-5 API with a corpus of binary and politically loaded prompts focusing on the human rights stances of no
table political figures (US, UK, EU). For each prompt, we retrieve all URLs (n= 22,500), search queries, and reasoning traces, paying close attention to the logic and parameters disclosed by GPT-5’s web search.
